additional_measurements.description: Crystal structure determined by Rietveld PXRD refinement: monoclinic space group P21/m (#11), lattice parameters a=4.8573(3) Å, b=3.2892(1) Å, c=8.877(1) Å, β=97.557(3)°, V=140.59(1) Å³
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Crystal structure and lattice parameters establish the material identity and coordination geometry; relevant as a baseline for future qubit-relevant TMTC materials, though direct qubit connection unclear for this newly reported compound

additional_measurements.description: Semiconducting electrical transport: normalized resistance R(T)/R0 measured 130–300 K; activation energy from Arrhenius fit
additional_measurements.value: Ea ≈ 0.16 eV (from linear fit of ln(R/R0) vs 1/T); R0 ≈ 1 kOhm at 300 K for ~0.8×1.0×2.0 mm piece
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Semiconducting behavior with ~0.16 eV band gap indicates VS3 is not a superconductor in this form; relevant for ruling out VS3 as a qubit electrode material, but low-dimensional TMTCs in this family can exhibit CDW/superconductivity under pressure or doping

additional_measurements.description: Magnetic susceptibility measured 1.8–300 K under 2 T applied field; Curie-Weiss fit yielding effective magnetic moment
additional_measurements.value: μeff = 0.31 μB (Curie-Weiss fit 1.8–70 K, χ0=0); Weiss temperature θ = -7.4 K
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Low effective moment (much less than 1.73 μB expected for spin-1/2 V4+) suggests partial spin correlation or incomplete S-S pairing; paramagnetic impurities or spin correlations can contribute to magnetic noise and decoherence in qubit environments, though connection to qubit performance unclear for this bulk material

additional_measurements.description: Heat capacity measured 2–200 K under zero field; Debye fit yielding characteristic temperatures
additional_measurements.value: Debye temperature ΘD(V) ≈ 214 K, ΘD(S) ≈ 574 K; no phase transition anomaly detected
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Absence of heat capacity anomaly consistent with absence of CDW or superconducting transition in measured range; Debye temperatures characterize phonon spectrum relevant to electron-phonon coupling and potential superconducting pairing if doped

additional_measurements.description: S-S dimer bond distance within VS3 crystal structure
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.1, paragraph 2: 'S-S dimers with a bonding distance of 2.23 Å'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Longer S-S bond than in TiS3 (2.04 Å) suggests weaker chalcogen-chalcogen pairing and may affect electron delocalization; structural details of S-S dimers in TMTC materials are relevant to CDW formation which competes with superconductivity

additional_measurements.description: Density of VS3 from Rietveld refinement
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Connection to qubit performance unclear; material density is a basic structural parameter

additional_measurements.description: Synthesis pressure required to stabilize VS3 phase
additional_measurements.value: 6 GPa (VS2 forms instead at 4 GPa at 650°C for 2h)
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.1, paragraph 5: 'a 4 GPa batch at 650 °C for 2 h resulted in a main product of VS2 with no VS3 peaks'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: High-pressure requirement indicates VS3 is a metastable phase; metastable TMTCs are of interest as quantum materials where CDW-superconductivity competition is tunable, but direct qubit performance connection unclear

additional_measurements.description: DFT electronic band structure calculation predicts metallic behavior for undistorted TiS3-type VS3 structure
additional_measurements.value: Metallic band structure predicted; observed behavior is semiconducting with narrow gap (~0.16 eV)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Discrepancy between predicted metallic and observed semiconducting behavior suggests structural distortion (CDW, V-V pairing) opens a gap; CDW-to-superconductor transitions under doping or pressure are relevant to identifying new qubit electrode candidates in TMTC family

anomalous_observations.description: VS3 shows semiconducting behavior despite nominally odd electron count (V4+, d1) and DFT prediction of metallic band structure
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Metal-to-insulator transition may occur above 300 K due to a CDW transition, or structural defects and V-V pairing in the system may open a gap by affecting electron filling of the metal dz² orbital, analogous to semiconducting NbS3 polymorph where Nb-Nb dimerization reduces symmetry

anomalous_observations.description: Effective magnetic moment (μeff = 0.31 μB) is much smaller than the spin-only value of 1.73 μB expected for a single unpaired V4+ electron (spin-1/2)
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: There may be correlation of spins in the system; considering the relatively long S-S dimer bond distance (2.23 Å vs 2.04 Å in TiS3), the S-S pairing may be incomplete, leaving a partial unpaired electron rather than a full S2²⁻ dimer

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Applied pressure during high-pressure solid-state synthesis, in GPa
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: For metastable superconducting and CDW materials in the TMTC family (NbS3, TiSe3, VS3), synthesis pressure determines which polymorph or phase forms; tracking this would allow systematic comparison of pressure-stabilized quantum materials that may be candidates for qubit electrodes or Josephson junction barriers
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Section 2.1: '6 GPa'; Section 3.1: comparison of 4 GPa vs 6 GPa results

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Semiconducting activation energy extracted from Arrhenius fit of temperature-dependent resistance, in eV
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Activation energy characterizes the band gap of semiconducting TMTC materials; materials with small gaps may be driven metallic or superconducting by doping or pressure, making this a key parameter for identifying candidates for pressure-induced superconductors relevant to qubit research
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Section 3.2, Figure 5A inset: 'activation energy of around 0.16 eV'

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Crystallographic space group of the synthesized material (e.g., P21/m #11)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Space group determines symmetry-allowed distortions (CDW, dimerization) that compete with or precede superconductivity in TMTC materials; systematic tracking would allow comparison across the NbS3, TiS3, VS3 family to identify structure-property relationships governing superconducting vs. semiconducting behavior

catchall.free_notes: VS3 is a newly reported compound with no prior literature; this is the first synthesis report. The material is polycrystalline and requires high-pressure synthesis (6 GPa) to stabilize. Purification requires low-temperature vapor transport at 120°C to remove excess S (higher temperatures cause decomposition to VS4 or other phases). Small amounts of vanadium metal impurity remain even after optimization. The material is isostructural with TiS3 and one polymorph of NbS3 (space group P21/m). Evidence for possible V-V pairing along b-axis and chain pairing perpendicular to b was found in TEM-SAED and Rietveld refinement respectively, but no long-range ordered superstructure was confirmed. The paper is funded by C2QA (Co-design Center for Quantum Advantage, DOE), explaining its inclusion in quantum materials research context, though VS3 itself is not yet a qubit material. No superconductivity was observed or claimed.

VS3-high-pressure.profile_notes: VS3 is a newly synthesized bulk polycrystalline vanadium trisulfide with no superconductivity observed; it is a semiconducting TMTC material with no qubit device fabricated, making loss_mechanisms and coherence_tier not applicable. Growth method is high-pressure solid-state synthesis, which has no equivalent in the controlled vocabulary, so 'other' is used.

catchall.free_notes: Device fabricated at Yale SEAS Cleanroom and YINQE. Fabrication sequence: HSQ mask EBL patterning → Ar RIE half-etch for ridge waveguide → full-etch for air holes → RCA-1 clean to remove RIE redeposition → BOE wet etch to suspend cavity → critical point drying. The photonic crystal mirror uses a tapered unit cell design (n=10 taper cells) that linearly reduces lattice constant from 498 nm to 450 nm and hole size from 600×350 nm to 400×233 nm at the cavity side. Waveguide sidewall angle is 60° from the etching process. The paper reports this as a photonics/optomechanics device on TFLN; its relevance to superconducting qubit research is indirect — the piezo-optomechanical coupling at GHz frequencies is relevant to quantum transduction between superconducting qubits and optical photons.

FP cavity with tapered photonic crystal.sample_id: FP cavity with tapered photonic crystal mirrors (this work)
FP cavity with tapered photonic crystal.profile_notes: This sample is a lithium niobate (LiNbO3) photonic/optomechanical Fabry-Perot cavity — not a superconducting material or qubit device. coherence_tier is not_applicable because no qubit or microwave resonator performance is measured; the reported Q of 5.7e5 is an optical loaded Q, not a microwave internal Q. Its relevance to superconducting qubit research is indirect (piezo-optomechanical transduction at GHz frequencies).

catchall.free_notes: Device is a 3D-circuit-QED architecture: transmon qubit and λ/2 stripline resonator fabricated on sapphire chip, housed in Al-6061 alloy metal block with 4 mm diameter hollow tube. The qubit drive port has a Mini-circuits ZLSS-A2R8G-S+ low-pass filter to enable subharmonic control. Four qubits from the same wafer with the same fabrication process were measured (3 with LPF, 1 without) to assess filter benefit, but individual data for these comparison qubits are in the Supplement and not extracted here as separate primary samples. Qubit control uses Xilinx RFSoC ZCU216 (QICK framework). Room-temperature electronics were enclosed in a PID-controlled temperature box to stabilize pulse parameters, as subharmonic gates are 3x more sensitive to amplitude/phase drift than resonant gates due to cubic Rabi rate dependence. Authors project gate times of 10 ns or lower are achievable with improved hardware.

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Alpha-Ta (bcc) is the low-loss phase for superconducting qubits; beta-Ta has higher defect density and higher surface loss, so phase purity directly sets the loss floor

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Background pressure of UHV sputtering chamber during Ta film deposition
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Authors explicitly identify a threshold: base pressure > 1e-8 Torr causes minority beta-phase or TaSi2 formation. Tracking this parameter systematically would allow correlation of chamber pressure with phase purity (XRD) and ultimately with qubit Qi and T1 across fabrication runs.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Electrical resistivity of the silicon substrate
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Authors identify high-resistivity Si (>20 kΩ cm) as the key enabler of low bulk dielectric loss, with a lower bound on bulk-limited QTLS,0 of 5.3e7 vs ~7.7e6 for sapphire. Resistivity is proposed as a proxy for material purity. Tracking this across samples would enable systematic study of bulk loss mechanisms.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Methods paragraph 1 and main text page 346 paragraph 3

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Metal-substrate surface participation ratio, a geometry-dependent quantity scaling with capacitor gap
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: SPR is the key independent variable used to disentangle surface and bulk TLS loss contributions in resonators. Tracking SPR alongside QTLS,0 enables separation of surface vs bulk loss channels, directly informing which material improvement path has largest impact on T1.

schema_promotion_candidates.parameter: Junction deposition vacuum level (HV vs UHV)
schema_promotion_candidates.description: Pressure regime for Al/AlOx Josephson junction deposition: HV (~1e-7 mbar) vs UHV (3e-10 Torr base, 1-5e-9 mbar during deposition)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Authors demonstrate statistically significant improvement in T2E (from 0.45T1 average to 1.2T1 average) by switching from HV to UHV junction deposition, attributed to hydrocarbon contamination reduction. This is a fabrication parameter with direct, large impact on qubit coherence.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text page 345 paragraphs 2-3, Methods paragraph 4

catchall.free_notes: Ta deposition process includes a getter step: Ta sputtered at room temperature (300 W, 3 mTorr Ar, 500-1000 s) onto chamber walls before sample deposition to reduce base pressure. Si wafer heated to 600-650 °C for 20 min to degas before Ta deposition. Deposition rate 0.3 nm/s for 750 s. Native SiO2 removed by 4.9% HF dip for 2 min within 15 min of transfer to loadlock to avoid regrowth. Wafers sourced from SIEGERT WAFER GmbH (intrinsic, double-side-polished, FZ) and Topsil (undoped, single-side-polished, FZ, 20-99 kΩ cm). Wafer thickness 525 ± 25 μm.

junction_present.source: Main text page 344: 'we measure resonators to isolate the contributions of the materials system without the Josephson junction'

loss_tangent_surface.source: Main text page 344 paragraph 3 and Figure 2 caption: 'tanδ = (11.3 ± 0.5) × 10^-4'

Qi_internal_quality_factor.source: Main text page 344 paragraph 3: 'lower bound on the bulk-limited QTLS,0 of 5.3 × 10^7'

additional_measurements.description: QTLS,0 as function of metal-substrate SPR (pMS) for resonators of varying geometry
additional_measurements.value: QTLS,0 increases from ~1e6 to >1e7 as pMS decreases from ~2e-3 to ~1e-4; no saturation at low SPR (unlike Ta-on-sapphire)
additional_measurements.source: Figure 2 and main text page 344 paragraph 3
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Absence of bulk-loss saturation in Q vs SPR confirms that high-resistivity Si has negligible bulk dielectric loss compared to sapphire (tan_delta_bulk < 1/5.3e7 ≈ 1.9e-8), directly enabling higher qubit T1. This measurement separates surface from bulk TLS loss mechanisms.

additional_measurements.description: Estimated surface loss tangent for resonators with 1.5 nm SiOx after 65 days ambient exposure
additional_measurements.source: Main text page 345 paragraph 1 and Figure 2 caption
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Comparable loss tangent to fresh devices implies SiOx contributes <10% of total surface loss; dominant surface TLS source is amorphous tantalum oxide, not silicon oxide, informing which surface treatment would most improve Qi

additional_measurements.description: Resonator SPR (pMS) range for coplanar waveguide resonators
additional_measurements.value: pMS between 1.5e-3 and 2e-4 for gaps 2-16 μm
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: SPR range determines sensitivity to surface vs bulk loss; this geometry sweep is the experimental basis for disentangling loss mechanisms

additional_measurements.description: Resonator SPR (pMS) range for lumped element resonators
additional_measurements.value: pMS between 6e-3 and 7e-5 for gaps 2-100 μm
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Wider SPR range than coplanar waveguides allows better extrapolation to bulk loss limit

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that as SPR decreases, QTLS,0 increases consistently without saturation for Ta-on-Si resonators, in contrast to Ta-on-sapphire where QTLS,0 saturates at large device sizes due to bulk substrate loss
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation (lower SPR → higher QTLS,0), with no bulk-loss saturation for Si substrate

catchall.free_notes: Resonators measured with varying trench depths (50-1000 nm) to vary SPR. Resonator types include coplanar waveguide (5.5-8.0 GHz, Qc ~1e6) and lumped element (4-5 GHz, Qc ~1e7). Data fit using loss model from Crowley et al. (PRX 2023) with separate TLS, quasiparticle, and other loss channels.

substrate_material.source: Methods paragraph 1 and Extended Data Table 1

deposition_method.value: DC magnetron sputtering (UHV) for Ta; electron-beam evaporation (Plassys UHV) for Al/AlOx junction

junction_present.source: Extended Data Table 1 row 34: 'Deposition vacuum: UHV'

T1_us.source: Extended Data Table 1 row 34: T1avg = 1003.0 ± 233.1 μs

additional_measurements.source: Extended Data Table 1 row 34 and main text page 344
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Record T1 for 2D transmon; corresponds to Qmax = 2.5e7, directly setting the upper bound achievable with this material platform before further improvements

additional_measurements.description: Time-averaged quality factor Qavg for qubit 34
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Qavg sets the effective energy relaxation rate averaged over TLS spectral diffusion; more relevant than Qmax for estimating gate fidelity in real operation

additional_measurements.description: Maximum quality factor Qmax for qubit 34
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Qmax = 2.5e7 is the highest reported for a 2D transmon, setting a benchmark for the Ta-on-Si platform
Qubit frequency sets the thermal photon population and the operating temperature margin; lower frequencies have longer photon wavelengths which can couple more effectively to dielectric loss channels
Gap size determines surface participation ratio (SPR); larger gaps reduce SPR and hence surface TLS loss, contributing to higher T1

catchall.free_notes: Qubit 34 has no T2E data reported in Extended Data Table 1 (dash entries). This is one of the qubits on which T2E was not measured because the team was iterating rapidly on chips to improve T1. UHV junction deposition (Plassys UHV, base pressure 3e-10 Torr after Ti getter step).

samples.sample_id: 45-qubit ensemble (HV and UHV, Extended Data Table 1)

substrate_material.source: Main text Abstract and Methods paragraph 1

deposition_method.value: DC magnetron sputtering (UHV) for Ta; electron-beam evaporation for Al/AlOx junction (HV or UHV depending on qubit)
deposition_method.source: Methods paragraphs 2 and 4; Extended Data Table 1

T1_us.source: Main text Abstract: 'mean time-averaged T1 (T1avg) is 0.45 ± 0.14 ms', converted to 450 μs

Qi_internal_quality_factor.source: Main text Abstract: 'time-averaged quality factors (Qavg) of 9.7 × 10^6 across 45 qubits'

single_qubit_gate_fidelity_pct.source: Main text Abstract: 'single-qubit gates with 99.994% fidelity'; page 346: 'average error per gate of (6.4 ± 0.3) × 10^-5'

additional_measurements.description: Mean T1 fluctuation span across 45 qubits (IQR normalized by T1avg)
additional_measurements.source: Main text page 344: 'the mean span is 36%'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Large T1 fluctuations (36% of mean) indicate ongoing TLS spectral diffusion and are a major limitation for large-scale processor performance even when average coherence is high; tracking this separately from average T1 is important for assessing processor yield

additional_measurements.description: Mean T2E for HV-junction qubits (subset with T2E measured)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: T2E < T1 for HV qubits indicates significant dephasing from junction-related TLSs; this directly limits gate fidelity for longer pulse sequences
T2E > T1 for UHV qubits indicates dephasing is suppressed below the relaxation limit; this is the regime required for high-fidelity gate operations limited only by T1

additional_measurements.description: Mean T2E fluctuation span across qubits
additional_measurements.source: Main text page 345: 'the mean span of T2E time fluctuations is 23%'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: T2E temporal fluctuations arise from strongly coupled TLSs in the junction undergoing spectral diffusion into the qubit resonance; this limits reproducibility and yield for large-scale processors

additional_measurements.description: Distribution of Qavg for HV-junction qubits (Extended Data Table 1 subset)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: No significant Qavg difference between HV and UHV qubits confirms that junction deposition quality affects dephasing (T2E) but not energy relaxation (T1), isolating the junction TLS contribution to each loss channel

additional_measurements.description: Distribution of Qavg for UHV-junction qubits (Extended Data Table 1 subset)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: See above; comparable Qavg for HV and UHV shows junction vacuum does not affect T1, meaning T1 is limited by Ta surface TLS loss not junction loss

additional_measurements.description: Median dephasing time (Tphi) for UHV-junction qubits
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Long Tphi confirms that pure dephasing is suppressed to the millisecond scale for UHV qubits; residual dephasing is dominated by photon shot noise from the readout resonator, not material TLSs

additional_measurements.description: Median dephasing time (Tphi) for HV-junction qubits
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Short Tphi for HV qubits (~10x worse than UHV) directly attributable to hydrocarbon contamination in junction, demonstrating junction cleanliness is the dominant dephasing mechanism in this material system once substrate loss is reduced

additional_measurements.source: Main text page 346: 'The gate error is minimized for a 40-ns pulse'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Gate time sets the trade-off between coherence-limited and leakage-limited errors; the 40 ns optimum with 99.994% fidelity demonstrates that millisecond T1 enables high fidelity without exotic control techniques

additional_measurements.description: Thermal photon population in readout resonator (estimated from CPMG noise spectroscopy)
additional_measurements.source: Main text page 345: 'the approximate thermal photon population in the resonator is n = 0.002, corresponding to an effective temperature of 52 mK'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Spurious photons in the readout resonator are identified as a major remaining dephasing source; this sets an upper bound on achievable T2E without improved filtering or readout schemes, and is now visible only because junction-related dephasing was suppressed

anomalous_observations.description: T2E < T1 observed for most HV-junction qubits despite low surface loss in this material platform
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: hydrocarbon contamination in the HV-deposited Al/AlOx junction creates TLSs that strongly couple to the qubit and cause dephasing via spectral diffusion. This was not previously observable because earlier generations of Ta-on-sapphire qubits had higher surface loss that masked junction-related dephasing.

anomalous_observations.description: After reducing photon shot noise by adding 10 dB attenuation, noise spectral density reduced by factor of 4 but shape remains qualitatively similar, suggesting photon shot noise is still a notable dephasing source
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: spurious photons in the readout resonator remain a significant noise source even after 10 dB additional attenuation on the transmission input line, indicating the photons may not originate exclusively from the input line (e.g., they may come from other thermal sources or reflections within the refrigerator).

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that switching from HV to UHV junction deposition significantly improves T2E (statistically significant at P < 0.01 by Mann-Whitney U test) without significantly changing T1 or Qavg
correlations_observed.nature: Positive correlation: UHV deposition yields higher T2E (mean 1.2T1 vs 0.45T1 for HV)

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that CPMG T2,CPMG increases as N^0.6 for N > 100 pi-pulses, approaching 2T1 limit
correlations_observed.nature: Positive correlation following power law N^0.6 for N > 100; saturates near 2T1

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Quality factor Q = omega * T1, averaged over repeated measurements spanning days to capture TLS spectral diffusion
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Qavg is a more reliable performance metric than instantaneous or maximum Q because TLS spectral diffusion causes T1 to fluctuate by ~36% of its mean. Tracking Qavg separately from Qmax enables comparison of materials platforms under realistic operating conditions rather than best-case measurements.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Difference between 1st and 3rd quartile T1 normalized by T1avg, measured over extended time series
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: T1 fluctuates by 36% of mean (similar to prior qubit generations despite 10x higher absolute T1), limiting processor performance for large-scale algorithms. Tracking fluctuation span separately from average enables identification of TLS spectral diffusion as a separate engineering problem from average loss reduction.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Simulated T1 limit imposed by Purcell decay through readout resonator, in ms
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Purcell limit must exceed measured T1 by a large margin to ensure material loss (not readout coupling) is the dominant relaxation channel. Tracking T1,p alongside measured T1 enables identification of when readout engineering rather than materials improvement is the next priority.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Extended Data Fig. 4 caption and Extended Data Table 3

catchall.free_notes: 45 qubits measured across 9 chips, each chip containing 6 transmons capacitively coupled to readout resonators inductively coupled to a central feedline/Purcell filter. Qubit frequencies 2-4 GHz, readout resonators 6.8-7.3 GHz, Ec/2pi = 200 MHz, kappa/2pi = 0.2-0.5 MHz, chi/2pi = 0.2-0.3 MHz, g/2pi = 50-60 MHz. Measurements run continuously for at least 3 days per qubit using QICK (RFSoC ZCU216) for T1/T2E and Quantum Machines OPX for RB/CPMG. Fabrication includes piranha clean, BOE native oxide reduction, and Cl2/Ar reactive ion etch (PlasmaTherm Takachi SLR ICP). Chip sizes are 25 mm or 10 mm squares diced from 4-inch wafers.

samples.sample_id: Qubit 47 (CPMG and RB characterization, UHV junction, Extended Data Table 2)

deposition_method.value: DC magnetron sputtering (UHV) for Ta; electron-beam evaporation (Plassys UHV) for Al/AlOx junction
deposition_method.source: Extended Data Table 2 row 47 (UHV) and Methods paragraph 4

T1_us.source: Extended Data Table 2 row 47: T1avg = 559.5 ± 100.3 μs

T2_echo_us.source: Extended Data Table 2 row 47: T2Eavg = 212.8 ± 40.7 μs

single_qubit_gate_fidelity_pct.source: Main text page 346: 'average error per gate of (6.4 ± 0.3) × 10^-5' for RB performed on qubit 47

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Flat T2,CPMG at low N indicates white (flat) noise spectrum dominating at frequencies above the qubit linewidth, consistent with photon shot noise as the primary dephasing source

additional_measurements.source: Main text page 345: 'linewidth of the qubit's readout resonator, κ/2π = 288 kHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Resonator linewidth sets the cut-off frequency of photon shot noise; dephasing from thermal photons is suppressed at frequencies above kappa, which is why CPMG pulse sequences with high N (probing high frequencies) recover T2 toward 2T1

additional_measurements.description: Fitted resonator linewidth from noise spectral density
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Agreement between fitted and measured resonator linewidth confirms photon shot noise model; this validates the identification of thermal photons as the dominant remaining dephasing channel

additional_measurements.description: T1avg after adding 10 dB attenuation on transmission line
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: T1 unchanged by added attenuation confirms T1 is not limited by photon-induced relaxation but by material loss (TLS in Ta oxide surface)

additional_measurements.description: T2Eavg after adding 10 dB attenuation on transmission line
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: T2E increases slightly but noise spectral density shape remains similar after attenuation, confirming photon shot noise is still present even after additional filtering; further optimization of readout isolation is needed

additional_measurements.description: CPMG T2,CPMG scaling with N for N > 100
additional_measurements.value: T2,CPMG ~ N^0.6, approaching 2T1 = 1.12 ms at 3000 pulses
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Power law scaling N^0.6 of T2,CPMG indicates the noise spectral density has a Lorentzian-like shape, consistent with photon shot noise filtered by the resonator. Approach to 2T1 limit shows pure dephasing is the dominant noise source and can be decoupled dynamically.
Sets the instantaneous coherence limit for RB experiments
T2Emax/T1max ≈ 0.4 confirms this qubit is significantly dephasing-limited relative to relaxation limit

catchall.free_notes: Qubit 47 used for CPMG and RB experiments. It has a dedicated drive line (chip design from Extended Data Fig. 4b) and measurements were initially conducted with 10 dB less attenuation on its transmission input line compared to standard Extended Data Table 1 qubits. T1 for qubit 47 during RB experiment: 0.56 ± 0.03 ms over 12 h. Dispersive shift (2chi)/2pi = 300 kHz. Note qubit 47 has T2E = 0.4T1 (below T1), making it a case study for junction-related dephasing; it was selected for noise spectroscopy because of this characteristic.

Ta-on-Si film (material characterization.profile_notes: Transport regime inferred as clean_limit from alpha-Ta phase purity and Tc=4.2 K; no microwave device measured so loss_mechanisms list is empty and coherence_tier is not_applicable. Key correlations drawn from XRD phase purity evidence and surface oxide characterization explicitly discussed by authors.

Ta-on-Si resonators (surface loss charac.profile_notes: Qi of 5.3e7 (bulk-limited lower bound) and measured Qi up to ~5.3e7 place this firmly in state_of_the_art for resonators. Loss mechanism decomposition into metal-substrate TLS interface, metal-vacuum (Ta oxide) surface TLS, and bulk dielectric substrate is the explicit science focus; SiOx contribution is investigated and found minor.

Qubit 34 (best single qubit, UHV junctio.profile_notes: T1avg=1003 µs and T1max=1682 µs are well above the 100 µs state_of_the_art threshold; loss mechanisms attributed to Ta surface oxide (metal-vacuum) and metal-substrate TLS based on resonator characterization of the same platform. Junction-related dephasing is present but T1 is the focus metric here.

45-qubit ensemble (HV and UHV, Extended.sample_id: 45-qubit ensemble (HV and UHV, Extended Data Table 1)
45-qubit ensemble (HV and UHV, Extended.profile_notes: Classified as multi_qubit_device (45 qubits across 9 chips); T1 median ~450 µs and gate fidelity 99.994% are state_of_the_art. Radiation included as a loss mechanism because spurious thermal photons from the readout resonator are explicitly identified as a dominant dephasing channel; charge_noise reflects junction TLS dephasing (HV vs UHV comparison). anneal_to_T1 is not directly evidenced here but deposition_conditions_to_film_purity and Qi_to_T1_upper_bound are explicitly demonstrated.

Qubit 47 (CPMG and RB characterization,.sample_id: Qubit 47 (CPMG and RB characterization, UHV junction, Extended Data Table 2)
Qubit 47 (CPMG and RB characterization,.profile_notes: T1avg=559 µs is state_of_the_art; this sample is specifically used for CPMG noise spectroscopy to identify photon shot noise (thermal radiation from readout resonator) and junction TLS dephasing as the dominant remaining dephasing channels, making noise_characterization and loss_mechanism_identification the primary science focus.

relevance_json.relevance_reason: The paper explicitly acknowledges C2QA funding, proposes a novel superconducting qubit (harmonium) with Josephson junction fabrication considerations, and computes qubit coherence times (T1, T2) using state-of-the-art noise models.

extraction_json.title: Nondegenerate Noise-Resilient Superconducting Qubit

film_material.source: Page 6 paragraph 4, Section II D: 'we will take a value at the upper limit of the typical range used in Al/AlOx JJs'

junction_present.source: Throughout paper; circuit described as comprising Josephson junctions (kite and fluxonium elements)

T1_us.source: Table I: bit-flip time from charge noise is 3 seconds; converted to µs = 3e9 µs. Note: this is a theoretically estimated bit-flip time, not a measured T1.

T2_echo_us.source: Table II: estimated dephasing time from charge noise is 80 ms, converted to 80000 µs. Caption notes these can be interpreted as rough estimates of echo times.

additional_measurements.description: Estimated bit-flip time from charge noise
additional_measurements.source: Table I, Estimated state transition times, Charge row, Bit-flip time column
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Bit-flip time sets the upper bound on logical qubit lifetime in noise-resilient qubit architectures; exponential suppression of bit-flips (seconds scale) vs conventional qubits (ms scale) directly reduces error correction overhead

additional_measurements.description: Estimated bit-flip time from flux noise
additional_measurements.source: Table I, Estimated state transition times, Flux row, Bit-flip time column
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Flux-noise-induced bit-flip suppression at >1000 s timescale demonstrates near-complete isolation of this logical error channel, critical for fault-tolerant operation

additional_measurements.source: Table I, Estimated state transition times, Quasiparticles row, Bit-flip time column
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Quasiparticle-induced bit-flip time of 200 s; quasiparticle tunneling matrix elements are four orders of magnitude smaller than in transmons, directly reducing this loss channel

additional_measurements.description: Estimated out-of-manifold heating time from charge noise
additional_measurements.source: Table I, Estimated state transition times, Charge row, Heating time column
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Heating events (leakage) at 2 ms timescale can be converted to erasure errors via dispersive readout, reducing their impact on logical error rate; erasure errors are less costly than computational-space errors in many error correction codes

additional_measurements.description: Estimated out-of-manifold heating time from flux noise
additional_measurements.source: Table I, Estimated state transition times, Flux row, Heating time column
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Long heating time from flux noise contributes to erasure error budget; erasure-based error correction threshold is more favorable than standard error correction

additional_measurements.description: Estimated out-of-manifold heating time from quasiparticles
additional_measurements.source: Table I, Estimated state transition times, Quasiparticles row, Heating time column
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Quasiparticle-induced heating is negligible at 5000 ms, consistent with the exponentially suppressed matrix elements for this circuit geometry

additional_measurements.description: Estimated pure dephasing time from flux noise (echo)
additional_measurements.source: Table II, Estimated dephasing times, Flux row
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Second-order flux noise sensitivity (operated at double sweet spot) yields 170 ms dephasing time; direct upper bound contribution to T2

additional_measurements.description: Estimated pure dephasing time from critical current noise (echo)
additional_measurements.source: Table II, Estimated dephasing times, Critical current row
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Critical current noise is a poorly-characterized 1/f noise channel; 80 ms estimate sets the co-limiting dephasing channel alongside charge noise and directly bounds T2

additional_measurements.description: Estimated pure dephasing time from thermal resonator shot noise (echo)
additional_measurements.source: Table II, Estimated dephasing times, Thermal resonator shot row
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Shot-noise dephasing suppressed by near-zero dispersive shift at nominal bias; dispersive shift chi << kappa at operating point naturally protects against this dominant dephasing channel in conventional qubits

additional_measurements.description: Estimated pure dephasing time from Aharonov-Casher effect (echo)
additional_measurements.source: Table II, Estimated dephasing times, Aharonov-Casher row
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Aharonov-Casher dephasing arises from coherent quantum phase slips in superinductor arrays; 1000 ms timescale indicates this is not a limiting channel but sets a design constraint on superinductor array parameters

additional_measurements.source: Page 7 paragraph 4: 'we find optimal circuit parameters of EJk/h = 35 GHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: EJk sets the barrier height for bit-flip protection and the energy hierarchy needed for Born-Oppenheimer approximation validity; directly controls the EJ/EC ratio governing charge noise sensitivity
EJf determines the fluxonium potential wells that provide the negative-EJ2 component; sets energy scale separation between computational and higher modes
Superinductance energy scale controls flux noise sensitivity and the Aharonov-Casher dephasing channel; also sets quasiparticle tunneling matrix elements through inductive phase drops
Fluxonium superinductance sets the flux-phase relationship controlling qubit frequency dispersion and second-order flux noise coupling
Large shunt capacitance reduces charging energy EC, exponentially suppressing charge noise sensitivity and bit-flip rates analogous to the transmon regime

additional_measurements.description: Assumed JJ critical current density (jc) for Al/AlOx JJs
additional_measurements.source: Page 6 paragraph 4, Section II D: 'jc = 4 µA/µm²'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Critical current density sets EJ/CJ ratio; higher jc allows smaller JJ area for given EJ, reducing parasitic capacitance and increasing energy hierarchy needed for heating suppression and BO approximation validity

additional_measurements.description: Assumed JJ specific capacitance (Cs) for Al/AlOx JJs
additional_measurements.source: Page 6 paragraph 4, Section II D: 'Cs = 50 fF/µm²'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Specific capacitance determines JJ capacitance for given EJ; minimizing CJ reduces intrinsic charging energy and improves energy hierarchy for noise protection

additional_measurements.description: Estimated single-qubit gate time (pi/2 pulse via Raman process)
additional_measurements.source: Page 9 paragraph 3: 'we can drive π/2 pulses in approximately 150 ns'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Gate time relative to coherence time sets achievable gate fidelity; 150 ns gate with ~80 ms dephasing time yields estimated incoherent error ~8e-6 per gate

additional_measurements.description: Estimated two-qubit CZ gate coherent infidelity
additional_measurements.source: Page 10 paragraph 2: 'infidelity of 4 × 10^-7 without decoherence, primarily due to leakage'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Coherent two-qubit gate infidelity sets fundamental lower bound on achievable two-qubit gate fidelity; this value is several orders of magnitude below state-of-the-art, indicating protocol is not limited by coherent errors

Incoherent two-qubit gate error directly sets fault-tolerance threshold distance requirement; 8e-6 is substantially below typical surface code threshold of ~1%, potentially enabling very low-overhead error correction

additional_measurements.description: Estimated dispersive readout time lower bound
additional_measurements.source: Page 11 paragraph 2: 'the readout time is bounded from below by 1/Γmsmt = 130 ns' for κ = 2π × 0.5 MHz, n_bar = 5
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Fast readout time relative to coherence enables mid-circuit erasure detection without significant dephasing of logical information; critical for erasure-based error correction

additional_measurements.description: Qubit frequency range as function of kite JJ asymmetry
additional_measurements.value: 200 kHz to 1.2 MHz for δEJk/EJk from 0 to 3%
additional_measurements.source: Page 8 paragraph 3: 'the qubit frequency varies linearly from 200 kHz to 1.2 MHz for the range of asymmetries plotted'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sub-MHz qubit frequency is a defining feature of this protected qubit; low frequency enables exponential suppression of charge noise coupling but complicates direct microwave driving, requiring Raman gate protocols

Quasiparticle density directly sets quasiparticle-induced bit-flip and heating rates via Fermi's golden rule; xqp = 1e-8 is consistent with state-of-the-art filtered dilution refrigerator environments

additional_measurements.value: Not explicitly quoted; described as exponentially suppressed
additional_measurements.source: Appendix B2 paragraph 3: 'ωq(ng) = ωge,0 + δωge,0 cos 2πng' with δωge,0 exponentially suppressed in EJ2/EC
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Charge dispersion is the key metric for charge noise immunity; exponential suppression with EJ2/EC ratio directly sets both charge-noise dephasing rate and bit-flip protection level

anomalous_observations.description: Optimal fluxonium parameters deviate from simple EJf = (π/2)ELf relation originally motivated
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Non-separability of the kite and fluxonium degrees of freedom due to similarity in their energy scales (~10-20 GHz) causes the BO approximation to break down; the full circuit Hamiltonian optimization finds a different optimum than the reduced BO model predicts

anomalous_observations.description: Charge coupling matrix elements between adjacent qubits are very small |<e|n_hat|g>| ≈ 2×10^-3, which would normally concern designers about controllability
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The small matrix element is actually a feature not a bug — it means qubit frequency collisions between adjacent qubits in a larger device are not problematic, since the cross-coupling strength is negligible

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that increasing kite JJ asymmetry δEJk/EJk increases dephasing rates from flux noise and critical current while decreasing charge-noise dephasing and bit-flip rates
correlations_observed.measurement_b: dephasing and bit-flip rates across noise channels
correlations_observed.nature: Flux/CC dephasing scales roughly linearly with asymmetry (positive correlation); charge dephasing and bit-flip rates decrease with asymmetry (negative correlation); creates a fundamental trade-off that the optimizer balances

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that smaller JJ asymmetry values cause the dispersive shift χlogical to drop linearly while χerasure remains relatively stable
correlations_observed.nature: χlogical scales linearly with asymmetry; χerasure is relatively insensitive to asymmetry; results in strong suppression of erasure-induced logical error perr ∝ χ²logical at small asymmetry

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Estimated or measured time for a computational bit-flip error to occur, in seconds; distinct from T1 (which measures energy relaxation within the qubit manifold)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: For noise-protected qubits, bit-flip times can reach seconds while T1 may be microseconds; conflating these would incorrectly assess fault-tolerance potential. Tracking bit-flip time separately from T1 is essential for comparing protected vs unprotected qubit architectures.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table I, Bit-flip time column; values range from 3 s (charge) to >1000 s (flux)

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Estimated or measured time for out-of-manifold (leakage) transitions from computational states to higher excited states, in milliseconds
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: In erasure-based error correction architectures, heating/leakage events must be detected and converted to erasure errors. The heating time sets the erasure detection rate requirement and determines whether the erasure conversion strategy is practical. This parameter has no schema field but is critical for evaluating protected qubit viability.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table I, Heating time column; values of 2 ms (charge), 1000 ms (flux), 5000 ms (quasiparticles)

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Operating frequency of the qubit in MHz (for sub-GHz qubits where reporting in GHz would be misleading)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Qubit frequency determines thermal population at operating temperature, gate speed limits, and control protocol requirements. For protected qubits with MHz-scale frequencies, the qubit is thermally mixed at dilution fridge temperatures, requiring active initialization. This is a qualitatively different operating regime from GHz transmons/fluxoniums.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Page 8 paragraph 3: qubit frequency ranges from 200 kHz to 1.2 MHz depending on JJ asymmetry

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Critical current density jc of Josephson junctions in µA/µm², which sets the EJ/CJ ratio for a given junction area
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: jc directly determines the minimum achievable JJ capacitance for a given EJ, setting the energy hierarchy needed for noise protection and BO approximation validity. Higher jc enables smaller junctions with lower parasitic capacitance, which is essential for protected qubit designs. This parameter is used in noise model calculations throughout the paper.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Page 6 paragraph 4: 'jc = 4 µA/µm²'; page 8 paragraph 5: 'jc ≈ 12 µA/µm² has been previously demonstrated'

catchall.free_notes: This is a theoretical proposal paper, not an experimental paper. No physical devices were fabricated or measured. All T1, T2, bit-flip, and dephasing values are numerical estimates from circuit Hamiltonian diagonalization combined with noise models. The harmonium circuit comprises a 'kite' element (two JJs in parallel with inductors, threaded by half-flux quantum) in series with a fluxonium, with a large shunt capacitor. The circuit has four degrees of freedom treated with full numerical diagonalization. JJ asymmetry δEJk/EJk is assumed to follow a normal distribution with 1% standard deviation based on typical fabrication variation; all decoherence estimates in Tables I and II are averaged over this distribution. The paper assumes Al/AlOx JJs with jc = 4 µA/µm² and Cs = 50 fF/µm² for all circuit calculations. A scaling strategy H→αH with α≈3 (requiring jc≈12 µA/µm²) is discussed as an alternative to erasure-based error correction, trading better heating suppression (2→20 ms) for faster dephasing. Single-qubit gate times (~150 ns) require a Raman protocol through higher excited states due to exponentially small direct matrix elements. Two-qubit gates use a SQUID coupler with flux-tunable ZZ interaction, achieving estimated coherent infidelity of 4×10⁻⁷ and incoherent infidelity of 8×10⁻⁶.

The device is classified as fluxonium as the closest vocabulary match to the harmonium circuit (a fluxonium-kite hybrid).

junction_present.source: Section II, page 3: 'two junctions in the outer SQUID loop'

additional_measurements.description: LINC mode frequency at operating point (φdc = π/2), from exact diagonalization with EL/h = 52.8 GHz, EJ/h = 15.84 GHz, EC/h = 100 MHz
additional_measurements.source: Figure 2(a) caption and Appendix B, page 12: 'ωc = 2π × 6.5 GHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Operating frequency sets the qubit/coupler microwave environment and determines drive frequency requirements for parametric processes; coupler frequency relative to qubit frequencies sets dispersive shift χ which directly limits coherence of coupled quantum memories

additional_measurements.description: Static Kerr nonlinearity (self-anharmonicity) of LINC at operating point φdc = π/2
additional_measurements.value: αL = 0 (exactly zero at operating point); analytical formula: αL[φdc] = −(2EJ cos φdc / (EL + 2EJ cos φdc)) × EC
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Zero idle Kerr means zero cross-Kerr (dispersive shift χ) to coupled quantum memories, suppressing thermal-noise-induced dephasing that would otherwise degrade T2 of coupled bosonic modes; directly impacts qubit coherence in bosonic control applications

additional_measurements.description: Three-wave mixing strength g3wm at operating point
additional_measurements.value: g3wm[π/2] = (EJ/2EL) × ωL; numerically ≈ 1–3 GHz/Φ0 from Figure 4(b)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets the parametric coupling rate for beam-splitting and squeezing operations; directly determines gate speed for bosonic qubit operations, trading off with flux-noise sensitivity at the operating point

additional_measurements.description: Flux-noise sensitivity (dωL/dΦ) at operating point — antisweet spot for both g3wm and flux noise
additional_measurements.value: Flux-noise sensitivity ≈ 2g3wm (equal up to factor of 2); at operating point both are near maximum. For 1/f noise with A ~ 1 µΦ0/√Hz at 1 Hz, infidelity estimate 1 − Fp ~ 10^−10
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Flux noise at the antisweet spot propagates to inherited dephasing in coupled resonators (κφ ∝ pres × |dωL/dΦ|); this is the dominant low-frequency dephasing channel for bosonic modes coupled to the LINC, directly limiting T2 of quantum memories

additional_measurements.description: Inherited dephasing rate of coupled quantum memory from thermal noise
additional_measurements.value: Minimized at operating point where χ = 0; for coupler thermal occupation nth_coupler = 2%, T1 = 20 µs, dephasing < ~0.1 ms^−1 at φdc = π/2
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Thermal noise-induced dephasing sets a floor on T2 for coupled quantum memories; suppressing it requires zero dispersive shift χ which the LINC achieves at its operating point

additional_measurements.description: Coupler decay rate used in Floquet-Markov simulations
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Coupler T1 sets the inherited decay rate into coupled quantum modes via energy participation ratio; with pres = 0.01, this implies an inherited decay rate ~1/(2.67 ms) for coupled resonators, setting an upper bound on bosonic mode T1

additional_measurements.description: Beam-splitting strength gBS between Alice (4.9 GHz) and Bob (6.0 GHz) modes coupled through LINC
additional_measurements.value: gBS = gTS = 2gSa ≈ g3wm|φac|√(pa×pb); at φac = 0.2π, gBS/2π ≈ 1–2 MHz from Figure 3(a)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Beam-splitting rate sets the two-qubit gate speed for bosonic dual-rail qubits; faster gates reduce susceptibility to decoherence during operation, directly improving two-qubit gate fidelity

additional_measurements.description: Driven LINC frequency (Zeeman) shift as function of drive amplitude
additional_measurements.value: ΔωL[π/2, φac] = −(EJ/2EL)^2 × ωL × |φac|^2; at φac = 0.1π, shift ~ −20 to −40 MHz from Figure 4(c)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Drive-induced frequency shift can cause frequency collisions with coupled qubit/resonator modes during parametric operations, inducing spurious transitions and degrading gate fidelity; this shift is present even in arrayed LINC configurations

additional_measurements.description: Driven Kerr suppression by arraying M LINC loops: Kerr suppressed by factor 1/M^2
additional_measurements.value: Arrayed 3× reduces inherited resonator Kerr by factor ~9 (Figure 5(c)); driven self-Kerr αL at φac = 0.2π reduced from ~5 MHz to ~0.5 MHz
additional_measurements.source: Figure 3(a) and Figure 5(c) and Equation (11), pages 7 and 13
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Inherited Kerr from coupler introduces cross-Kerr to coupled quantum memories, causing dephasing and state-dependent frequency shifts that limit coherence of multi-photon bosonic states; suppression by arraying directly improves bosonic qubit performance

additional_measurements.description: Steady-state coupler impurity (proxy for process infidelity) compared between LINC and SNAIL
additional_measurements.value: LINC impurity ~10^−3 over most drive frequencies 0.5–2 GHz; SNAIL impurity significantly higher with peaks at 2.17 and 3.25 GHz due to subharmonic excitations
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Coupler excitation during parametric operations directly translates to process infidelity via state-dependent shifts in gate rate and resonance condition; lower driven impurity in LINC vs SNAIL implies higher achievable two-qubit gate fidelity for bosonic operations

additional_measurements.description: Static Kerr in presence of junction asymmetry β_Δ and dc flux asymmetry φ_Δ
additional_measurements.value: αL^asym ≈ EC × β_Δ × sin[φ_Δ − (1+√2)β_Δ]; for β_Δ = 2% and φ_Δ = −(π/2)×1%, αL ≈ −130 kHz for EC = 100 MHz
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Residual idle Kerr from fabrication asymmetry creates cross-Kerr to coupled modes, introducing dephasing; knowing the asymmetry tolerance (~2%) is critical for device design and yield in manufacturing

additional_measurements.description: Energy participation ratio of resonator in LINC mode (assumed design parameter)
additional_measurements.source: Section III, page 4: 'which we assume for the rest of this article to be pres = 0.01'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Energy participation ratio pres directly sets the inherited coupler loss in the resonator (κres_inherited ~ pres/T1_coupler) and the inherited dephasing rate; 1% is the design target balancing coupling strength against loss

additional_measurements.description: Circuit Hamiltonian parameters for example LINC device
additional_measurements.value: EL/h = 52.8 GHz, EJ/h = 15.84 GHz, EC/h = 100 MHz, center shunt = array of 10 junctions
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: These parameters define the operating regime: EL > 2EJ ensures single-valued potential; EC/EL ratio sets impedance and zero-point fluctuations which determine coupling strengths and nonlinearity scale

anomalous_observations.description: The LINC operates at an antisweet spot with respect to flux noise — both g3wm and flux-noise sensitivity are simultaneously maximized at the operating point φdc = π/2
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: This is an inherent trade-off for flux-driven three-wave mixers; however, the authors argue this is acceptable because (1) flux-noise-induced infidelity is estimated at ~10^−10 per gate, (2) inherited flux-noise dephasing in coupled modes is low-frequency and can be mitigated by dynamical decoupling or stabilized bosonic codes, whereas thermal-noise-induced dephasing (which the LINC eliminates) is harder to suppress

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Effective three-wave mixing coupling strength g3wm in GHz, characterizing the rate at which parametric processes (beam-splitting, squeezing) can be activated by a driven nonlinear coupler element
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: g3wm directly sets the speed of parametric two-qubit gates for bosonic qubits and frequency conversion operations; tracking this across devices would allow systematic comparison of coupler performance and gate speed limits across different mixer architectures (SNAIL, DDS, LINC, ATS)

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Self-Kerr (anharmonicity) of the coupler/mixer element in MHz, both idle (undriven) and driven values
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Idle coupler Kerr directly determines the cross-Kerr dispersive shift to coupled quantum memories, which sets the thermal-noise-induced dephasing rate and limits T2 of bosonic modes; driven Kerr creates inherited anharmonicity in coupled linear resonators that degrades multi-photon state fidelity

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Dimensionless ratio (0 to 1) of resonator energy participation in the coupler mode, pres, characterizing the hybridization between a quantum memory and its coupler
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: pres directly scales both the inherited loss rate (T1_inherited ~ T1_coupler/pres) and inherited dephasing from the coupler; it is the key design parameter balancing gate speed against memory protection, and tracking it would allow systematic optimization of coupler-memory architectures across experiments

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Fractional asymmetry of Josephson junction energies in a balanced mixer element, β_Δ = (EJ1 − EJ2)/EL, as a percentage
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Junction asymmetry breaks the parity protection selection rule that suppresses parasitic processes in balanced mixers like the LINC and DDS; tracking this fabrication parameter across devices would allow correlation of asymmetry with residual Kerr, parasitic process strength, and gate fidelity, directly informing junction fabrication targets
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Section V and Appendix D, pages 7-8

catchall.free_notes: This is a purely theoretical paper proposing the LINC (Linear Inductive Coupler) as a new superconducting circuit element — no experimental samples were fabricated or measured. The 'Data Availability' statement confirms 'No data were created or analyzed in this study.' All numerical values are from circuit simulations (exact diagonalization, Floquet-Markov) using model Hamiltonian parameters. The LINC circuit topology is shared with the ATS (asymmetrically threaded SQUID) and gradiometric SNAIL, but operates at the opposing bias point (φdc = π/2 vs. 0). The center shunt inductor could be implemented as NbN or granular aluminum (high kinetic inductance material), a geometric meandering inductor, or a Josephson junction array (N junctions), with N > (pres^2 × n_bar × EC/κ)^(1/2) required for sufficient linearity in bosonic applications. The LINC shares parameter range with ATS which has been implemented experimentally (refs [20,60-62]). Comparison SNAIL parameters used in simulations: EC/h = 100 MHz, EJ/h = 276 GHz, α = 0.193, Kerr-free point at Φext = 0.442Φ0.

LINC (Linear Inductive Coupler) - theore.profile_notes: This is a purely theoretical paper with no fabricated samples or experimental measurements; all values are from circuit simulations. Coherence_tier is not_applicable, growth_method and transport_regime are not determinable, and material_class is 'other' since no specific superconducting film was realized. Flux noise and quasiparticle loss are discussed as relevant inherited dephasing/decay channels for coupled modes, but no experimental loss attribution is made.

catchall.free_notes: This paper reports a SNAIL-based parametric amplifier (SPA) device, not a qubit material sample per se. Three nominally identical devices (A, B, C) were tested in different cryogenic setups for reproducibility. Device A is the primary device reported in the main text. Devices B and C showed comparable but not identical performance due to fabrication variations (photoresist development, reactive ion etch uniformity). All three achieved >100 MHz bandwidth at 20 dB gain. The Nb circuit layer uses standard photolithography; the SNAIL Josephson junctions use Dolan-bridge e-beam lithography with double-angle Al evaporation. A 2-µm Ag layer with 20 nm Ti adhesion layer is evaporated on the wafer backside as a ground plane. The on-chip pump-port bandpass filter is a key innovation: it provides ~50-60 dB rejection at 9 GHz (signal band) while passing ~20 GHz (pump), preventing pump-induced qubit dephasing. The qubit devices (two 3D Al cavity transmons) are separate from the SPA chip. Qubit coherence values in Table III show ±10 µs temporal drift across several days. The device operates at ~17 mK in a dilution refrigerator.

anomalous_observations.description: Z2B recurrence does not show consistent improvement in Bell fidelity under idle T1/T2 noise, while X2B does show general improvement on the same device
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The dominant noise source on the qubits studied is T2 noise (Z errors). Z2B catches X/Y errors (T1-type), so it does not remove the dominant T2 error. X2B catches Z errors (T2-type), so it does remove the dominant noise and shows improvement.

anomalous_observations.description: ZZ coherent errors cause Bell fidelity to oscillate strongly with delay time, giving apparent large distillation improvement that is not a true gain over non-postselection methods
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The purity of the state is unchanged under coherent ZZ errors; distillation provides apparent benefit only because it postselects on parity, not because it genuinely reduces entropy. Staggered dynamical decoupling can cancel the ZZ term without postselection.

correlations_observed.nature: Positive correlation: matching the distillation protocol to the dominant error type determines whether improvement is observed

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Always-on longitudinal coupling between neighboring qubits in fixed-frequency transmon devices, causing coherent phase errors during idle periods
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: ZZ crosstalk causes coherent Bell fidelity oscillations that must be suppressed with staggered dynamical decoupling before T1/T2 noise models apply; this directly limits achievable Bell pair fidelity and distillation performance. Tracking ZZ rates across devices would enable systematic comparison of crosstalk-induced loss mechanisms.

catchall.free_notes: Experiments performed on IBM Eagle processor ibm_kyiv, a 127-qubit fixed-frequency transmon device with heavy-hexagonal lattice connectivity. Entangling gate is echoed cross-resonance (ECR). Multiplexed readout with 1.24 µs measurement delay. Experiments run in parallel across 13 sets of 4 adjacent qubits (for 2-Bell protocols) to obtain statistics. Tables III and IV give calibration data for qubits 0-3 used in Z2B and X2B recurrence experiments respectively (calibrated on different dates: May 14 and May 15, 2024). Tables V and VI give calibration data for qubits 3-8 and 59-64 used in ZX3B three-Bell experiments (calibrated May 17, 2024). The paper is primarily about quantum information protocols (entanglement distillation) rather than materials characterization; device noise parameters are extracted from backend calibration data to validate noise models.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Kinetic inductance per square (pH/sq) of superconducting thin film, extracted from CPW resonator frequency measurements and compared to Mattis-Bardeen prediction
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Kinetic inductance is a primary design parameter for NbN and TiN devices; it sets resonator frequency, participation ratios, and proximity to the SIT. Tracking this across samples would allow systematic correlation with Qi and loss mechanisms across the SIT phase boundary

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Superconducting energy gap Delta in meV, inferred from Tc via BCS strong-coupling limit
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Gap directly sets the quasiparticle poisoning rate at operating temperature; high-gap materials (NbN: 2.03 meV vs Al: ~0.17 meV) enable elevated-temperature operation. Tracking gap across samples allows comparison of thermal quasiparticle suppression across material platforms

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Total depth of NbN removed by atomic layer etching (ALE) in nm
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: ALE etch depth controls the final film thickness and thus the proximity to the SIT, surface roughness, and Qi. Systematic tracking of etch depth alongside Qi would allow optimization of the ALE process for minimal loss
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Supplemental appendix A page 12: 'all samples undergo a global ALE treatment that removes approximately 3 nm of NbN and oxide combined'

catchall.free_notes: NbN films grown by ALD on 2-inch annealed HEMEX sapphire wafers (650 µm thick). Standard chip size 7x7 mm. ALE carried out in RIE-ICP system using SF6/H2; chamber at 15°C; each cycle removes <1 nm. Global ALE pre-treatment (~3 nm removal) applied to all samples to improve surface quality. CPW resonators and Hall bars co-fabricated on same chip with 4 patches of different ALE-thinned thickness, enabling multiplexed characterization. Measurements at base temperature ~23 mK in dilution refrigerator with light-tight and mu-metal shields.

additional_measurements.description: Estimated Josephson energy from measured frequency and EC
additional_measurements.source: Page 6 paragraph 3: 'we extract an estimated Josephson energy of EJ/hbar = 2pi × 26.15 GHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: EJ/EC ~ 90 places device firmly in transmon regime; Josephson inductance LJ = 6.25 nH characterizes the weak link nonlinearity

additional_measurements.source: Supplemental appendix H, page 16: 'A shunt capacitance of CS = 68.7 fF extracted from finite-element simulations'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Shunt capacitance determines the charging energy and transmon regime; fully planar geometry eliminates junction capacitance (CJ) contribution present in sandwich junctions

additional_measurements.value: Designed: alpha/2pi = 306 MHz; Measured: 235 MHz (reduction of ~71 MHz or ~23%)
additional_measurements.source: Page 6 paragraph 3 and Supplemental appendix H: 'computed anharmonicity is alpha = 2pi × 306 MHz... The measured anharmonicity of 235 MHz is modestly reduced'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Discrepancy between designed and measured anharmonicity (beyond the ~12 MHz from stray inductance) is attributed to highly transparent channels in the NbN weak link, analogous to semiconductor junction effects; this has direct implications for gate fidelity

additional_measurements.value: width w ~ 1 µm, length l ~ 30 nm
additional_measurements.source: Page 4 paragraph 1 (Section III): 'We implement a weak link of dimensions w~1 µm and l~30 nm'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Weak link geometry controls critical current, inductance, and anharmonicity; different geometries lead to orders-of-magnitude variation in anharmonicity (from 235 MHz to ~10 Hz)

additional_measurements.description: Weak link thickness in insulating phase (thinned region)
additional_measurements.source: Fig. 2 caption: 'a thickness, dI of ~2.5 nm corresponding to a point deep in the insulating phase'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Thinning to ~2.5 nm drives NbN through the SIT, creating the nonlinear weak link; this thickness-controlled SIT is the novel junction mechanism replacing AlOx barriers

additional_measurements.description: Electron mean free path extracted from diffusive weak link model fit
additional_measurements.value: le = 0.03 - 0.5 nm (comparable to NbN inter-atomic distance)
additional_measurements.source: Page 4 paragraph 3 and page 7 paragraph 3: 'This results in le = 0.03 - 0.5 nm, comparable to NbN inter-atomic distance'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Extremely short mean free path (kF*le ≲ 1) indicates electron localization in the weak link (dirty/localized limit), which affects loss mechanisms and current-phase relation non-sinusoidality

additional_measurements.description: Diffusion coefficient extracted from Ic(T) fit using SNS diffusive model
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Very low diffusion coefficient confirms near-localized transport in the weak link; this is relevant for understanding the loss channel in the planaron qubit

additional_measurements.description: Critical current approaches zero near T = 7-8 K (weak links inherit NbN large gap)
additional_measurements.source: Page 7 paragraph 2 and Fig. 4: 'the extracted critical current approaches zero near T ~ 7-8 K'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Weak links retaining ~60-70% of bulk Tc demonstrates that ALE-thinned NbN preserves large gap, enabling potential operation above 1 K compared to Al-based junctions

additional_measurements.description: Half-integer Shapiro steps observed, indicating non-sinusoidal current-phase relation
additional_measurements.value: Half-integer steps at V = (m/2)*h*fRF/2e observed at fRF = 12.75 GHz
additional_measurements.source: Page 8 and Fig. 5e-h: 'we also observe Shapiro-like features which occur at fractions of the aforementioned voltages'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Fractional Shapiro steps indicate harmonics in current-phase relation from highly transparent channels, consistent with the reduced anharmonicity in the planaron; transparent channels are a known source of anharmonicity reduction and may contribute to loss

additional_measurements.value: IcRN ≈ 0.3 × pi*Delta/2e, with Delta_NbN = 2.03 meV; computed values 0.24-0.44 mV
additional_measurements.source: Page 4 paragraph 2 and Supplemental appendix D: 'IcRN ≈ 0.3 · pi*Delta/2e'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Suppressed IcRN product relative to Ambegaokar-Baratoff indicates long-junction or localized behavior, with ETh/Delta ~ 0.14, relevant to understanding junction loss and current-phase relation

additional_measurements.description: Thouless energy ratio extracted from IcRN comparison
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Small ETh indicates the weak link is in the long-junction limit with energy-phase sensitive Andreev levels, contributing to non-sinusoidal CPR and modified anharmonicity

additional_measurements.description: Single-photon Qi of L2UnA (linear lumped resonator with different weak link geometry, l=20nm, w=0.3µm)
additional_measurements.source: Supplemental appendix J, page 17: 'devices displaying low anharmonicity had a much higher single-photon internal quality factor Qi = 3.8 × 10^5'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Higher Qi in lower-anharmonicity (l=20nm) devices vs planaron (l=30nm) suggests weak link geometry and transparency are correlated with loss; shorter/narrower links may have lower junction loss

additional_measurements.description: Anharmonicity of L2UnA (linear lumped resonator with l=20nm, w=0.3µm)
additional_measurements.source: Supplemental appendix J, page 17 and Fig. S12c: 'We estimate alpha/2pi ≈ 10 Hz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Orders-of-magnitude variation in anharmonicity with similar inductance but different junction geometry is unexplained and is directly relevant to understanding how junction microstructure sets qubit parameters

anomalous_observations.description: Planaron qubit linewidth kappa_t/2pi ≈ 15 MHz is far larger than expected from CPW resonator Qi ~ 10^6, indicating strong excess loss in the weak link junction
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: 'The underlying decoherence mechanisms are not yet fully understood. We leave a detailed investigation of intrinsic junction loss, spurious mode coupling, and design optimization to future studies.' Authors note this is a 'marked difference from the coplanar waveguide resonators.'

anomalous_observations.description: Measured anharmonicity (235 MHz) is ~23% lower than designed (306 MHz), and this reduction cannot be explained by stray inductance alone (~12 MHz reduction estimated)
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: 'The discrepancy is therefore more likely due to the microscopic nature of the NbN weak link. Transparent transmission channels, for example, are known to reduce anharmonicity by up to a factor of four.'

anomalous_observations.description: Orders-of-magnitude variation in anharmonicity (235 MHz vs ~10 Hz) between devices with similar inductance and shunting capacitance but different weak link geometry
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: 'The large difference in anharmonicity, tuned by junction geometry, is currently not well understood and is subject of future work.'

anomalous_observations.description: Ic(T) temperature dependence of weak links is inconsistent with both the disordered insulator (Anderson localization) model and diffusive SNS model individually; the Anderson insulator model predicts |dIc/dT| ≈ 500 nA/K near Tc, which is >3x larger than measured ~150 nA/K
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: 'A possible resolution of the conflict presented by the two models is that in reality our system is close to the localization transition, so between the applicability domains of the two models.'

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Qubit anharmonicity alpha/2pi in MHz, the frequency difference between |0>->|1> and |1>->|2> transitions
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Anharmonicity must exceed linewidth to operate as a qubit; reduced anharmonicity from transparent junction channels directly limits gate fidelity and addressability. Tracking this alongside junction geometry would reveal systematic trends across novel junction types

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Total qubit linewidth kappa_t/2pi in MHz (= 1/T1 for energy relaxation dominated devices)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Linewidth directly sets T1 and gate fidelity; for the planaron, kappa_t >> kappa_c indicates junction-limited loss. Tracking linewidth allows identification of dominant loss channels across junction types

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Length l of the superconducting weak link in nm, the critical dimension controlling the Thouless energy and junction behavior
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Weak link length determines whether the junction is in the short (tunnel) or long (diffusive/localized) limit, setting IcRN product, current-phase relation harmonics, and anharmonicity; systematic tracking would reveal how junction length controls qubit parameters
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Page 4 Section III and Supplemental appendix D

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Josephson energy EJ/hbar in units of 2pi × GHz
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: EJ sets the junction inductance and together with EC determines EJ/EC ratio and transmon regime; tracking EJ across devices with different weak link geometries would systematize the design of NbN-based qubits

catchall.free_notes: The planaron is fabricated using Sample 1 (S1) process: weak link trench etched first, then capacitor pad geometry. Final BOE cleaning (~20 min) used to remove residual organics, fluorides, and native oxides without attacking NbN. Measurement performed in dilution refrigerator at T=23 mK with light-tight and mu-metal shields; same setup as CPW resonator measurements. The qubit is directly coupled to a transmission line in hanger geometry without a readout resonator, making it strongly undercoupled (kappa_c/kappa_t ~ 1/200). No T1 or T2 time-domain measurements reported; decoherence characterized only through linewidth. The paper simultaneously reports a companion device (L2UnA, Section J) with same capacitance but shorter/narrower weak link (l=20nm, w=0.3µm) showing Qi=3.8e5 but anharmonicity of only ~10 Hz — suggesting a tradeoff between Qi and anharmonicity with weak link geometry that is not yet understood.

NbN CPW resonators (ALE-thinned, bulk fi.sample_id: NbN CPW resonators (ALE-thinned, bulk film characterization)
NbN CPW resonators (ALE-thinned, bulk fi.profile_notes: NbN is inherently dirty-limit (sheet resistance near quantum of resistance RQ confirms strong disorder). Loss mechanism assignment is based on authors' observation that ALE surface treatment (roughness reduction >50%) drives Qi above 10^6, while unetched films show gap-dependent Qi consistent with interface/surface TLS; anneal_to_Qi is included because the sapphire substrate is annealed as part of the process and the paper correlates surface treatment steps with Qi improvement.

Planaron qubit.profile_notes: T1 is bounded by the measured linewidth kappa_t/2pi ~15 MHz giving T1 ~10.6 ns, placing this firmly in early_exploration; the dominant loss channel in the weak link is explicitly stated as not yet understood (hence 'unknown' included), but quasiparticle and TLS_interface mechanisms are plausible given the near-localized, disordered weak link and are discussed by the authors. The device is a transmon-regime qubit (EJ/EC ~90) despite using an ALE-defined weak link rather than a conventional tunnel junction.
