junction_present.source: Introduction paragraph 3: DR qubit consists of two adjacent microwave cavities coupled via a beam splitter, with a transmon ancilla dispersively coupled to one cavity

additional_measurements.description: Erasure check gate infidelity suppression achieved by robust joint parity gate UJP vs non-robust single-shot gate
additional_measurements.value: Two orders of magnitude reduction in erasure check errors at typical dephasing noise level gamma/chi ~ 10^-2
additional_measurements.source: Conclusion paragraph 1: 'Our methods reduce erasure check errors and logical two-qubit gate infidelities by two orders of magnitude, as is evident from Fig. 1(d) for typical levels of dephasing noise gamma/chi ~ 10^-2'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Erasure check fidelity directly determines erasure bias of dual-rail qubit; higher erasure bias means more errors are detectable rather than Pauli errors, reducing QEC overhead and improving logical error rates

additional_measurements.description: Logical two-qubit gate ZZ(pi/2)_L infidelity reduction using dynamically corrected gates
additional_measurements.value: Up to three orders of magnitude reduction in logical two-qubit gate infidelities
additional_measurements.source: Abstract sentence 5: 'reduce the logical two-qubit gate infidelities by up to three orders of magnitude'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Two-qubit gate fidelity is the dominant cost in fault-tolerant quantum computing; small improvements can reduce module count by large factors, and this is directly relevant to QEC overhead

additional_measurements.description: Scaling of robust joint parity gate infidelity with ancilla dephasing noise
additional_measurements.value: I proportional to gamma^4 (first-order dephasing cancellation achieved)
additional_measurements.source: Page 2, Robust joint parity gate section: 'yielding a dynamically corrected UJP with quartic sensitivity to noise, I proportional to gamma^4, as shown in Fig. 1(d)'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Transmon dephasing is a primary noise source limiting dual-rail qubit performance; quartic vs quadratic scaling means dramatic fidelity improvement at realistic noise levels, directly improving T2 and gate fidelity

additional_measurements.description: Scaling of non-robust single-shot joint parity gate infidelity with ancilla dephasing noise
additional_measurements.source: Page 2, paragraph 2: 'The infidelity scales quadratically, I proportional to gamma^2, confirming the absence of robustness against dephasing noise'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Establishes baseline performance of existing single-shot erasure check protocol, providing quantitative comparison point for the improvement offered by dynamically corrected gates

additional_measurements.description: Scaling of X1 otimes Z2 gate infidelity with ZZ crosstalk noise using robust design
additional_measurements.value: I proportional to xi^4 (first-order ZZ crosstalk cancellation achieved)
additional_measurements.source: Figure 3(d) caption and page 3-4: 'First-order robustness against ZZ crosstalk (blue) for several values of Z2 dephasing noise'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Residual ZZ crosstalk from always-on dispersive coupling is a major limitation of dual-rail qubit architectures; suppressing it to fourth order directly improves two-qubit gate fidelity

additional_measurements.description: Typical ancilla dephasing noise level in current dual-rail superconducting qubit experiments
additional_measurements.source: Conclusion paragraph 1: 'for typical levels of dephasing noise gamma/chi ~ 10^-2 [10, 19]'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets the operating regime for current hardware, grounding the theoretical improvement claims in experimental relevance

anomalous_observations.description: Transmon dephasing cannot be suppressed using beam splitter interactions alone because [H_DR, Z2] = 0, requiring explicit transmon driving to achieve noise robustness
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The commutation relation [H_DR, Z2] = 0 means the beam splitter interaction does not rotate the ancilla in any way that could refocus dephasing; only direct transmon drives can create the necessary closed space curves for noise cancellation

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that making the dispersive coupling tunable would reduce gate infidelities by an additional order of magnitude and allow shorter gate times
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation: lower residual coupling (more tunable) leads to lower infidelity; authors cite reference [32] for tunable coupler implementation

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Ratio of erasure error rate to Pauli error rate for an erasure qubit; dimensionless
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Erasure bias is the defining performance metric for erasure qubits — a higher ratio means more errors are detectable and convertible, directly reducing QEC thresholds and overhead. Tracking this across samples and gate designs would allow systematic comparison of how materials and control choices affect the fundamental advantage of the erasure qubit architecture
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Introduction: 'Photon loss, the dominant error, is converted into a detectable erasure, leaving Pauli errors suppressed by orders of magnitude'

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Dispersive coupling strength between transmon ancilla and cavity mode, in units of Hz or rad/s; sets the fundamental timescale for erasure check and entangling gate operations
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Chi is the master clock rate of the dual-rail system — gate times scale as 2pi/chi, noise robustness conditions are expressed as gamma/chi and xi/chi, and tuning chi is the primary lever for improving gate speed and fidelity. Without tracking chi, performance comparisons across DR qubit implementations are not possible
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Equation (1) and throughout: chi is the dispersive interaction strength between ancilla and mode a

catchall.free_notes: This is a theoretical control-pulse design paper, not a materials characterization paper. No physical samples were fabricated or measured. The system studied is a superconducting dual-rail cavity qubit: two microwave cavities coupled via a beam splitter, with a transmon ancilla dispersively coupled to one cavity. The logical qubit is encoded in the one-photon subspace |0>_L = |10>, |1>_L = |01>. The paper uses the Space Curve Quantum Control (SCQC) framework to design dynamically corrected gates (DCGs) for erasure checks and logical entangling operations. Key result: robust joint parity gate UJP achieves I ~ gamma^4 scaling vs I ~ gamma^2 for the single-shot gate, and the ZZ(pi/2)_L logical entangler achieves similar improvement. The BARQ (Bezier Ansatz for Robust Quantum) control method is used for the X1 otimes Z2 step. All results are simulation/analytical; no experimental implementation reported in this paper.

dual-rail-cavity-qubit-system.profile_notes: This is a purely theoretical control-pulse design paper with no physical sample fabricated or measured; all controlled vocabulary dimensions default to not-applicable or unknown values. The science focus reflects the paper's goal of demonstrating robust gate designs and characterizing dephasing/ZZ noise scaling in a dual-rail cavity qubit architecture.

catchall.free_notes: Plasma oxidation series, heated substrate at 400°C. 6-nm Nb seed layer below Ta film. O2 flow 20 sccm, 100W RF plasma. This sample used in trilayer fabrication studies (trilayer III in Fig. 5a, and others). Thickest plasma-grown oxide in series; used as basis for STEM-TEM trilayer sample V (Ta/Ta2O5/Ta/Nb/Si stack with uniform 15 nm Ta2O5 and sharp interfaces).

Table I Row 1.profile_notes: Control sample with native oxide only; no microwave device fabricated. Transport regime cannot be determined from available data.

Table I Row 2.profile_notes: High-temperature sapphire deposition control sample with native oxide; epitaxial-like grain structure noted but no transport or device data available to determine transport regime.

Table I Row 3.profile_notes: Tube furnace oxidation at 400°C for 10 min produces thick Ta2O5 (37 nm) and increased roughness; paper frames this in context of Josephson junction barrier engineering.

Table I Row 4.profile_notes: Tube furnace oxidation at 400°C for 30 min; oxide thickness grows to ~57 nm with increasing roughness, part of systematic oxidation time series.

Table I Row 5.profile_notes: Longest tube furnace oxidation (60 min, 400°C); roughness of 1.71 nm approaches levels authors cite as degrading Qi in Nb resonators, making this process unfavorable for qubit applications.

Table I Row 6.profile_notes: RTA oxidation at 700°C for 1 min produces extremely high roughness (7.28 nm); film too rough for XRR measurement of oxide thickness, ruling out RTA as viable junction oxidation method.

Table I Row 7.profile_notes: RTA oxidation at 700°C for 5 min; roughness of 16.38 nm is incompatible with reliable junction fabrication; oxide thickness unmeasurable by XRR due to surface roughness.

Table I Row 8.profile_notes: Longest RTA oxidation (10 min, 700°C); spatial non-uniformity of roughness across 1–10 µm scales explicitly cited by authors as making RTA unsuitable for Josephson junction barrier formation.

Table I Row 9.profile_notes: Room-temperature plasma oxidation for 10 min produces smooth surface (0.572 nm Ra) with ~7.6 nm oxide; low roughness favorable for Josephson junction barriers with minimal TLS density.

Table I Row 10.profile_notes: Room-temperature plasma oxidation for 30 min; oxide thickness nearly identical to 10 min sample (7.33 vs 7.64 nm), demonstrating time-insensitive saturation behavior of plasma oxidation at room temperature.

Table I Row 11.profile_notes: Plasma oxidation at 200°C for 60 min yields exceptionally low roughness (0.108 nm Ra); authors highlight this as producing the smoothest oxide surfaces in the study, optimal for minimizing TLS.

Table I Row 12.profile_notes: Plasma oxidation at 200°C for 120 min; roughness (0.105 nm) essentially unchanged from 60 min counterpart, confirming stable, smooth oxide formation at 200°C regardless of extended oxidation time.

Table I Row 13.profile_notes: Plasma oxidation at 300°C for 60 min grows thicker oxide (~10 nm) at moderate roughness (0.463 nm); still well below tube furnace or RTA values, representing a trade-off between oxide thickness and surface quality.

Table I Row 14.profile_notes: Plasma oxidation at 300°C for 120 min yields very low roughness (0.125 nm) despite thicker oxide (~10.8 nm); longer oxidation time at 300°C appears to smooth the surface, with only Ta2O5 species confirmed by XPS.

Table I Row 15.profile_notes: Plasma oxidation at 400°C for 60 min produces the thickest plasma-grown oxide (~15 nm) at moderate roughness (0.342 nm); used as basis for trilayer junction fabrication studies.

Table I Row 16.profile_notes: Plasma oxidation at 400°C for 120 min; delta-Ta2O5 crystalline phase observed in trilayer XRD built on this sample; used as the primary basis for STEM-TEM trilayer verification with sharp interfaces and uniform 15 nm Ta2O5.

substrate_material.source: Experimental Section, Diamond crystals paragraph: 'All data were acquired using electronic-grade, CVD-grown diamond substrates sourced from Delaware Diamond Knives.'

deposition_method.source: Abstract sentence 4 and Experimental Section, Diamond crystals paragraph

annealing_temperature_C.source: Experimental Section, Diamond crystals paragraph: 'subsequently annealed up to 850 C and 1200 C' — Diamond A referenced with 1200 C per citation 24

additional_measurements.description: NV center optical inhomogeneous linewidth at cryogenic temperature
additional_measurements.source: Page 1, Results section paragraph 1: 'typical inhomogeneous linewidths on the order of ~100 MHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Inhomogeneous linewidth determines spectral selectivity for resolving individual NV centers; narrower linewidths enable higher-fidelity single-qubit addressability and reduce crosstalk in multi-qubit diamond platforms

additional_measurements.source: Page 1, Results section: 'a collection of narrow optical transitions near 637 nm'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: ZPL wavelength identifies NV charge state and sets operating laser requirements; relevant to optical qubit readout fidelity

additional_measurements.description: Spatial clustering scale of NV centers — peak deviation from Poissonian distribution
additional_measurements.source: Page 4, paragraph 3: 'the deviation from randomness is strongest below ~2 µm and peaks near ~100 nm'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: NV clustering at ~100 nm scale implies proximal spin pairs with potentially coherent dipolar interactions — directly relevant to multi-qubit operations, entanglement-assisted sensing, and spin-based quantum memory in diamond platforms

additional_measurements.description: Field of view for wide-field PLE imaging
additional_measurements.source: Experimental Section, Data collection and processing paragraph: 'frequency-resolved PL maps over fields of view spanning 48×48 µm²'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets the scale of statistical sampling for NV spatial distribution analysis; connection to qubit performance indirect but relevant for yield mapping across device-scale areas

additional_measurements.source: Experimental Section, Optical microscopy: 'closed-cycle cryogenic microscope system operated at 9 K under high vacuum'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Operating temperature determines quasiparticle density and spin coherence; 9 K is above typical superconducting qubit operating temperature but relevant for characterizing diamond NV spin properties in hybrid qubit architectures

anomalous_observations.description: NV centers show statistically significant spatial clustering with overrepresentation of closely spaced pairs and higher-order clusters compared to a Poisson (complete spatial randomness) model
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Either pre-existing inhomogeneities in nitrogen incorporation during CVD growth (e.g., step-edge incorporation, nitrogen segregation near dislocations or sector boundaries) or biased vacancy diffusion during annealing (influenced by local strain, electrostatic fields, or extended defects) — or a combination. Direct NV-NV templating effects ruled out as acting range (~few nm) is far shorter than observed clustering scale (~100 nm).

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Fraction of identified NV centers that fall within the optical diffraction limit of their nearest neighbor, forming clusters
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Quantifies the prevalence of proximal NV pairs — the primary resource for entanglement-assisted sensing and multi-qubit logic in diamond; tracking this across growth conditions would enable systematic optimization of cluster yield for quantum information applications
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Page 4, Figure 4 caption and surrounding text

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Surface density of NV centers per unit area (µm⁻²)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Directly enters the Bayesian emitter-assignment metric (equation 10 in SOM) and sets the probability of accidentally confusing two distinct NVs; tracking density alongside qubit coherence properties would allow correlation of NV bath density with T1/T2 in diamond spin qubits

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Inhomogeneous optical linewidth of NV ZPL transitions in MHz
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Sets the spectral multiplexing capacity — number of individually addressable NVs per diffraction-limited spot; narrower linewidths enable denser qubit arrays without crosstalk, directly impacting scalability of diamond-based quantum processors

catchall.free_notes: Diamond A was implanted with nitrogen and annealed to 1200°C (ref 24). Statistical analysis was performed specifically on non-implanted regions where vacancies migrated from implanted zones. No systematic optical or spectral differences were observed between in-grown and implanted NVs. The cryogenic PLE imaging platform achieves sub-diffraction localization by combining spectral selectivity (~100 MHz linewidth resolution) with spatial Gaussian fitting and Bayesian emitter-assignment using both positional and blinking-dynamics metrics.

substrate_material.source: Experimental Section, Diamond crystals paragraph: 'All data were acquired using electronic-grade, CVD-grown diamond substrates sourced from Delaware Diamond Knives.'

deposition_method.source: Abstract sentence 4 and Experimental Section, Diamond crystals paragraph

annealing_temperature_C.source: Experimental Section, Diamond crystals paragraph: 'subsequently annealed up to 850 C and 1200 C' — Diamond B referenced with 850 C per citation 25

additional_measurements.description: Spatial clustering of NV centers — excess probability of multi-NV clusters relative to complete spatial randomness
additional_measurements.value: Qualitative: pronounced enhancement in n≥2 cluster probability; Ripley's K-function above CSR across broad radius range, peak near ~100 nm
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Non-Poissonian NV clustering confirmed in independently prepared sample, suggesting intrinsic growth mechanism rather than sample-specific artifact; directly relevant to reproducible engineering of proximal spin pairs for multi-qubit quantum operations

anomalous_observations.description: NV clustering behavior persists across two independently prepared samples (A and B), suggesting it is an intrinsic feature of CVD diamond growth and post-annealing defect distribution rather than a measurement artifact
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Intrinsic correlation in NV formation driven by nitrogen incorporation inhomogeneity or biased vacancy diffusion during annealing — not a sample-preparation artifact.

catchall.free_notes: Diamond B was implanted with nitrogen and annealed to 850°C (ref 25). As with Diamond A, analysis focused on non-implanted regions. The persistence of clustering in both samples with different annealing temperatures supports a growth-stage (nitrogen distribution) rather than annealing-stage (vacancy diffusion) origin, though the authors state this remains an open question requiring complementary characterization (electron microscopy, cathodoluminescence, atomistic simulations).

Diamond A.profile_notes: This sample is a diamond substrate with NV color centers — not a superconducting film — so most superconducting-qubit vocabulary dimensions are not applicable. Classified as film_only/not_applicable because no microwave or qubit device was fabricated; science focus is spatial characterization of NV defect distributions as a function of annealing conditions.

Diamond B.profile_notes: Same as Diamond A — CVD diamond substrate with NV centers annealed to 850°C; no superconducting film or qubit device present. The cross-sample comparison of NV clustering behavior maps loosely onto process_optimization but no superconducting-qubit correlations are evidenced.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Tc extracted from exponential decay of Qint vs temperature in microwave resonator measurements; distinct from DC transport Tc
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: QP activation temperature directly characterizes the superconducting gap as seen by microwave photons; a ~40% increase in Tc means thermal QPs are exponentially suppressed at qubit operating temperatures, directly extending T1 upper bound set by QP loss channel

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Metal-substrate interface surface loss tangent extracted from QTLS,0 vs surface participation ratio (pMS) plot across multiple resonators
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Surface loss tangent is the key figure of merit for TLS loss at the dominant metal-substrate interface; tracking this across material systems enables direct comparison of TLS loss channels independent of resonator geometry

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Zero-temperature upper critical field extracted from WHH model fit to magnetoresistivity vs temperature data at various applied fields
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Hc2(0) characterizes vortex pinning strength; order-of-magnitude higher Hc2 in Ta-Hf vs Ta directly explains why no vortex-motion-induced loss is observed in Ta-Hf resonators — tracking this would allow systematic attribution of loss channels across material systems

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Percentage lattice mismatch between film and sapphire substrate determined from XRD and STEM
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Lattice mismatch controls strain and disorder in the film, directly setting residual resistivity, RRR, and coherence length; the jump from 1.6% (1ks) to 20.5% (2ks) drives qualitatively different transport behavior and vortex pinning — systematic tracking would reveal the mismatch threshold for dirty-limit behavior

catchall.free_notes: This is the representative alloy film used for CPW resonator fabrication and microwave measurements. Fabrication followed identical process to Ref.[23] (Crowley et al.) for fair comparison. Device packaged in QDevil QCage.24. 7 resonators measured. Sidewalls showed porous/ridged morphology compared to smooth native Ta sidewalls, attributed to Hf-induced strain and local etch rate variations in chlorine RIE. Ta-Hf alloy confirmed as Type-II dirty-limit superconductor via WHH model fitting. Film survives 20-minute piranha exposure but degrades rapidly (< 1 minute) in BOE.

catchall.free_notes: 2ks films at different temperatures show similar disorder levels (RRR ~1.7-1.9, residual resistivity ~20-33 µΩ·cm) compared to the stronger temperature dependence seen in 1ks films, suggesting that at 2ks the lattice mismatch dominates over temperature-dependent ordering effects.

Table II Row 1: 1ks, 550°C.profile_notes: RRR of 1.53 and residual resistivity of 36.61 µΩ·cm firmly confirm dirty-limit regime. No microwave device fabricated for this condition; film_only with materials characterization focus.

Table II Row 2: 1ks, 650°C.profile_notes: RRR of 1.45 and residual resistivity of 21.72 µΩ·cm confirm dirty-limit regime. No microwave device; film characterization only.

Table II Row 3: 1ks, 750°C.profile_notes: Identified as optimal 1ks condition with highest RRR (2.21) and pure (111) orientation; Hc2(0) and coherence length measured, confirming dirty-limit. No microwave device fabricated from this condition.

Table II Row 4: 1ks, 850°C.profile_notes: RRR of 1.82 and high residual resistivity confirm dirty-limit. Structural degradation at 850°C noted; no microwave device fabricated from this condition.

Table II Row 5: 2ks, 550°C.profile_notes: RRR of 1.71 and residual resistivity of 32.55 µΩ·cm confirm dirty-limit. Large lattice mismatch (~20.5%) with sapphire distinguishes 2ks films from 1ks films; no microwave device.

Table II Row 6: 2ks, 650°C.profile_notes: RRR of 1.58 and residual resistivity of 23.64 µΩ·cm confirm dirty-limit. No microwave device fabricated from this condition.

Table II Row 7: 2ks, 750°C (resonator sa.profile_notes: Qi ~1×10^6 at single-photon power qualifies as state_of_the_art; vortex_motion included because Hc2 enhancement is explicitly cited as suppressing this channel relative to pure Ta, and quasiparticle loss is characterized via Tc extraction from microwave measurements. Surface loss tangent measured and compared to native Ta for cross-platform comparison.

Table II Row 8: 2ks, 850°C.profile_notes: RRR of 1.91 and residual resistivity ~20.89 µΩ·cm confirm dirty-limit. Authors note 2ks films show similar disorder across temperatures, suggesting lattice mismatch dominates over thermal ordering effects; no microwave device.

catchall.free_notes: Device D1 is approximately monolayer NbSe2 (AFM thickness 2 nm, Raman-derived 1.5 nm used in Fig. 4a analysis). NbSe2 encapsulated in hBN; assembled in Ar glove box with O2 and H2O below 0.1 ppm. Edge contacts formed by RIE followed by in-situ Ar ion milling (105 s) and angled Al evaporation at 30 degrees with substrate rotating at 60 rpm. Al CPW resonator is 250 nm MBE-grown Al on high-resistivity Si, patterned by photolithography and wet etching.

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that kinetic inductance scales inversely with NbSe2 layer thickness (1/d dependence) across all 12 devices
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation following approximate 1/d power law; Lk,sq ranges from 1.195 nH/sq at ~2 nm to 0.004 nH/sq at 52 nm

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that the ratio Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean increases monotonically from 0.7 to 15 as sample thickness decreases toward monolayer, indicating increasing dirty-limit contribution in thinner samples
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation; thin samples show dirty-limit dominant behavior, thick samples show clean-limit behavior

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that the self-Kerr coefficient magnitude increases with decreasing film thickness (larger Kerr nonlinearity for thinner, higher-Lk samples)
correlations_observed.nature: Positive correlation; Kerr ranges from -0.006 Hz/photon (bulk) to -14.7 Hz/photon (monolayer)

anomalous_observations.description: Microwave measurements reveal mixed clean- and dirty-limit contributions to kinetic inductance in NbSe2, whereas prior DC transport studies reported only clean-limit behavior
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The microwave response may be sensitive to the multi-band nature of NbSe2. Two superconducting bands with different gap sizes and scattering rates can coexist in clean and dirty limits simultaneously, which DC transport (probing only real conductivity) cannot resolve but complex conductivity (kinetic inductance) can.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Kinetic inductance per square (nH/sq) of the superconducting film, measured via resonator frequency shift; characterizes superfluid density and Cooper pair inertia
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Sheet kinetic inductance is the primary figure of merit for superinductor materials used in fluxonium and 0-pi qubits; tracking this across samples and materials would allow systematic comparison of 2D vdW superconductors, amorphous films, and conventional materials for quantum circuit applications. Values span 3 orders of magnitude in this dataset alone (0.004 to 1.195 nH/sq).
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table 1, column Lk,sq, page 12; Abstract sentence 3

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Self-Kerr nonlinearity coefficient K/2pi (Hz/photon) extracted from resonator frequency shift vs photon number; measures deviation from linear inductance
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: The Kerr coefficient quantifies whether a superinductor operates in the linear regime required for fluxonium shunt inductors and parametric amplifiers. Conventional Josephson junction arrays have Kerr ~MHz while NbSe2 has Kerr ~Hz, a difference of 6 orders of magnitude. Tracking this across materials would enable selection of optimal superinductor materials for specific applications.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table 1, column K/2pi, page 12; Abstract sentence 5; Conclusions paragraph 1

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Ratio of measured kinetic inductance to clean-limit kinetic inductance (Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean); quantifies where a material sits in the clean-to-dirty superconductivity continuum
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This ratio directly characterizes whether quasiparticle scattering (dirty limit) or ballistic transport (clean limit) dominates, which determines vortex behavior and decoherence mechanisms. Tracking this parameter across samples enables systematic comparison of loss channels in superconducting quantum devices.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table S3, column Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean, page 31; Layer Dependence section page 8

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Pearl length Lambda = 2*lambda^2/d (µm); characteristic length scale for magnetic field penetration in 2D superconducting films thinner than the London penetration depth
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Pearl length determines whether geometric confinement enhances kinetic inductance beyond BCS predictions; all devices in this study operate in the Pearl regime (d << lambda). If device width approaches Pearl length, kinetic inductance is further enhanced, directly affecting superinductor performance in quantum circuits. Estimated range 10-70 µm for these devices.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Supplementary Section 8, page 32; page 9 paragraph 1; Figure S6

catchall.free_notes: All 12 NbSe2 devices share the same Al CPW resonator platform: 250 nm MBE-grown Al on high-resistivity Si, patterned by photolithography and wet etching. NbSe2 bulk crystal sourced from HQ Graphene. Mechanical exfoliation and hBN encapsulation performed in Ar glove box (O2 < 0.1 ppm, H2O < 0.1 ppm). Thickness measured by AFM (tapping mode); layer number confirmed by Raman shear mode spectroscopy. Device D1 is monolayer/1.5-layer (Raman-derived thickness used in Fig. 4a). Edge contacts made by RIE (O2/Ar/CHF3), followed by 105 s in-situ Ar ion milling and 30-degree angled Al evaporation with 60 rpm substrate rotation. Measurements at ~10 mK in Bluefors XLD1000 dilution refrigerator. Residual resistance ratios for NbSe2 samples reported as R300K/RN = 5-10, confirming high-quality crystals. Al spectator resonator (no NbSe2) measured Qi = 5.985×10^6, coupling Qc = 10^5 (Figure S3). The lower Qi observed in NbSe2-terminated resonators attributed to imperfect Al-NbSe2 contacts and dielectric loss from polymer contamination from dry transfer process.

D1.profile_notes: NbSe2 is a van der Waals 2D superconductor not in the controlled vocabulary; classified as 'other'. Monolayer sample shows Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean ratio of 14.95 confirming dirty_limit; Qi of 8000 places it in early_exploration tier. Growth method is mechanical exfoliation, classified as 'other'.

D2.profile_notes: 4-layer NbSe2 with Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean ratio of 3.53 indicates dirty_limit behavior; Qi of 60000 is at the lower edge of competitive but per early_exploration threshold (<1e5 is early_exploration, this is 6e4 which is below 1e5), so early_exploration applies.

D3.profile_notes: 6-layer NbSe2 with Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean ratio of 2.39 indicates mixed clean/dirty (intermediate) behavior; Qi of 7000 places it in early_exploration tier.

D4.profile_notes: 7-layer NbSe2 with Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean ratio of 1.79 indicates transitional intermediate behavior; Qi of 24000 places it in early_exploration tier.

D5.profile_notes: 8-layer NbSe2 with Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean ratio of 1.79 indicates intermediate behavior; Qi of 12000 places it in early_exploration tier.

D6.profile_notes: Primary detailed device (9 nm, ~9 layers); Qi of 28000 is early_exploration. Authors attribute lower Qi to imperfect Al-NbSe2 contacts and dielectric loss from polymer contamination from dry transfer process.

D7.profile_notes: No Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean ratio available for D7; transport_regime assigned intermediate by interpolation with neighboring thickness samples. Qi of 21000 is early_exploration.

D8.profile_notes: 10-layer NbSe2 with Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean ratio of 0.71 (<1) indicates clean_limit behavior; vortex_motion included as the record notes clean-limit superconductors exhibit vortex motion as dominant loss channel. Qi of 7000 is early_exploration.

D9.profile_notes: No explicit Lk,sq/Lk,sq,clean ratio for D9 but thickness (12 nm) is close to D8 which showed clean_limit; record notes near-clean-limit regime. Qi of 6000 is early_exploration.

D10.profile_notes: Thickest high-performing sample at 25 nm; record describes near-bulk clean-limit behavior. Qi of 180000 (1.8e5) exceeds 1e6 threshold? No, 1.8e5 < 1e6, but exceeds 1e5 competitive threshold — however 1.8e5 is between 1e5 and 1e6 so this is competitive tier. Correction: Qi=180000=1.8e5 which is >1e5 and <1e6, placing it in competitive tier.

D11.profile_notes: 30 nm near-bulk NbSe2; clean_limit inferred from near-bulk thickness and trend established by D8-D10. Qi of 25000 is early_exploration.

D12.profile_notes: 52 nm bulk-like NbSe2; authors explicitly describe 1/d scaling of kinetic inductance across all 12 devices and clean-to-dirty limit transition. Qi of 58000 is early_exploration (<1e5). Loss attributed to imperfect contacts and polymer contamination from transfer process.

junction_present.source: Results section page 4: 'single crystal device as a comparison'

additional_measurements.description: Critical current of single crystal at T=1.8 K, positive direction
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Establishes baseline critical current of bulk Mo3Al2C single crystal; used to confirm junction critical currents in bicrystal devices are substantially reduced, as expected for weakly coupled superconductors — relevant to Josephson junction qubit design with this material

additional_measurements.description: Critical current of single crystal at T=1.8 K, negative direction
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Symmetric positive/negative critical current confirms no intrinsic diode effect in single-handedness crystal; baseline for comparison with bicrystal devices

additional_measurements.description: Second superconducting transition temperature attributed to Mo2C inclusions
additional_measurements.source: Results section page 4: 'a second transition is observed at Tc2 = 3 K due to inclusions of Mo2C'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Presence of Mo2C inclusions indicates phase impurity in single crystals; could affect quasiparticle density and junction behavior at operating temperatures near 1.8 K
Large gap sets upper bound on IcRn product (~3 mV for ideal junction) and determines quasiparticle excitation energy; relevant to operating margin and quasiparticle loss in Josephson junctions

London penetration depth determines effective junction area via magnetic flux penetration; directly enters calculation of junction width W and Josephson penetration length, affecting vortex behavior and critical current uniformity

additional_measurements.source: Results section page 4: 'Mo3Al2C undergoes a structural transition at T = 155 K to a rhombohedral structure'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Below 155 K the crystal becomes polar and chiral; this symmetry change could affect spin-selective transport properties relevant to the diode effect in Josephson junction devices

additional_measurements.description: Native oxide layer thickness on Mo3Al2C crystal surface (tunnel barrier)
additional_measurements.source: Results section page 5: 'The insulating layer of the device is the nominal oxide layer on the crystal, which is of order 10 Å [30]'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Native oxide thickness directly determines tunnel barrier transparency and IcRn product; oxide composition and uniformity affect TLS density at the interface — a key loss channel in superconducting Josephson junction qubits

Crystal chirality is the fundamental symmetry property enabling the superconducting diode effect; tracking space group assignment is essential for understanding nonreciprocal transport in Josephson junction devices

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Crystallographic space group of the superconductor, e.g. P4132 (right-handed) or P4332 (left-handed) for Mo3Al2C
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Crystal chirality (space group) determines whether a material breaks inversion symmetry in a handedness-specific way; this is the key parameter for designing superconducting diode junctions and for tracking CISS-type effects — without this field, chirality-based device properties cannot be systematically compared across samples
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Throughout paper, e.g. Abstract and Results section page 4

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Product of junction critical current and normal state resistance in mV; figure of merit for Josephson junction quality
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: IcRn directly sets the energy scale of the junction and determines switching speed and noise immunity in qubit circuits; comparing IcRn across junction types (e.g., chiral vs non-chiral, different barriers) requires a structured field

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Description of crystal handedness configuration at the junction interface, e.g. right-handed/left-handed (P4132/P4332) or right-handed/right-handed (P4132/P4132)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: The diode effect depends critically on opposite vs same handedness at the junction; tracking this as a structured field enables systematic correlation between interface chirality and nonreciprocal transport properties across a database of devices

catchall.free_notes: Device 1 is a single Mo3Al2C crystal used as a control to confirm bulk superconducting properties. Crystal grown by high-temperature flux reaction with excess Al at 1650°C for 24 hours, then cooled to 1250°C at 5°C/hour. Crystals are 100-200 microns in size with (110) plane exposed. Two-probe measurement geometry introduces contact resistance producing nonzero voltage in superconducting state. Tc=9.2 K reported from literature [24,25]; observed Tc=9 K in this device consistent with prior work.

junction_present.source: Results section: 'We fabricated Josephson junctions by pressing together the (110) planes of single crystals of Mo3Al2C'

additional_measurements.description: Critical current in positive direction at T=1.8 K, H=0
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Junction critical current sets the switching energy scale for Josephson junction qubit operation; asymmetry between Ic+ and |Ic-| is the direct measure of the superconducting diode effect

additional_measurements.description: Critical current in negative direction at T=1.8 K, H=0
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Asymmetry between |Ic+| and |Ic-| demonstrates nonreciprocal supercurrent — the superconducting diode effect — relevant to rectifier and logic elements in superconducting circuits
Rn determines the IcRn product and thermal noise floor; low Rn here may indicate relatively good interface transparency or larger junction area
IcRn = 0.26 mV is well below the ideal predicted value of ~3 mV, indicating non-ideal barrier transparency — relevant to energy gap and switching speed in qubit applications

additional_measurements.description: Estimated effective junction width W from Fraunhofer-like pattern period
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Junction width determines uniformity of supercurrent; large W relative to Josephson penetration length indicates nonuniform supercurrent distribution, affecting magnetic diffraction pattern shape and diode effect quality

additional_measurements.description: Approximate period of oscillation in Ic vs H
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Field periodicity sets effective junction area and is used to estimate junction width; shorter period implies smaller effective area
Critical current density determines Josephson penetration length and junction regime (short vs long); lower Jc here compared to devices 3 and 4 suggests nonuniform supercurrent
W/λJ ~ 1/2 places device in intermediate junction regime; relevant to uniformity of supercurrent and shape of magnetic diffraction pattern

additional_measurements.source: Results section page 7: 'maximum amplitude of about α = 5%'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Diode efficiency directly sets the rectification ratio in superconducting logic; 5% asymmetry is modest but statistically significant, motivating optimization of junction orientation

additional_measurements.description: Statistical significance of Ic+ vs |Ic-| difference at H=1 Oe
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: High statistical significance confirms the diode effect is a robust property of the junction rather than measurement noise — important for reliability in circuit applications

additional_measurements.source: Results section page 5: 'followed by a more significant drop at 6 K. The lower transition temperature in the device compared to the bulk Tc is expected behavior due to the presence of the insulating barrier'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Reduced Tc in device relative to bulk indicates proximity effect suppression at the interface; sets the actual operating margin for this junction geometry

anomalous_observations.description: Critical current vs H pattern is asymmetric about central peak with skewed peaks, despite W/λJ ~ 1/2 placing device in short-to-intermediate junction limit where more symmetric patterns are expected
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Device 2 has an estimated critical current density an order of magnitude lower than devices 3 and 4, suggesting nonuniform supercurrent distribution given its larger estimated junction size, leading to self-field-like behavior even in the intermediate junction regime

catchall.free_notes: Right-handed/left-handed (P4132/P4332) bicrystal junction. Junction formed by pressing (110) planes of two opposite-chirality Mo3Al2C crystals together using a 3D-printed ABS pressure device. Electrical contacts made using DuPont 4922N-100 silver paint or sputtered gold pads on mica substrate. Pseudo 4-probe technique used due to small crystal size. Tunnel barrier is native oxide on crystal surface (~1 nm). Diode effect direction switches as function of magnetic field, and effect is robust over two hours of repeated measurement at fixed fields.

catchall.free_notes: Second right-handed/left-handed (P4132/P4332) bicrystal junction. Closer to short junction limit than device 2. Authors explicitly compare device 3 to device 4 (same-handedness): similar critical current densities and junction sizes, but only device 3 shows asymmetry in Ic+/Ic-, attributed to intrinsic SDE from chirality rather than self-field effect. Diode effect robust over two hours of repeated measurement at multiple fixed fields.

Highest Jc of devices with Fraunhofer-like patterns; most uniform supercurrent, confirmed by more symmetric Ic vs H pattern
W/λJ ~ 1/6 firmly in short junction limit; supports interpretation that absence of diode effect is intrinsic, not a geometric artifact

catchall.free_notes: Control device: right-handed/right-handed (P4132/P4132) bicrystal junction. Shows Fraunhofer-like pattern confirming Josephson effect. No statistically significant diode effect observed at any measured field, confirming that opposite chirality at the interface is required for the SDE in Mo3Al2C. Authors use comparison of device 3 (similar Jc and W) vs device 4 to attribute asymmetry in device 3 to intrinsic SDE rather than self-field effect.

junction_present.source: Results section: junction fabricated same way as others but no Fraunhofer-like pattern observed; Table 1 reports Ic and Rn values

surface_oxide_thickness_nm.source: Results section page 5: 'The insulating layer of the device is the nominal oxide layer on the crystal, which is of order 10 Å'

Tc_K.source: Results section page 5: 'All devices show an initial decrease in resistance around Tc = 9 K'

additional_measurements.description: Critical current in positive direction at T=1.8 K, H=0
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Largest Ic of all bicrystal devices; attributed to largest junction area, consistent with W >> λJ regime

additional_measurements.description: Critical current in negative direction at T=1.8 K, H=0
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Slight asymmetry with Ic+; authors attribute this to self-field effect (extrinsic) rather than intrinsic diode effect
Lower Rn consistent with larger effective junction area
Highest IcRn of all devices, close to theoretical ideal of ~3 mV; however authors note the asymmetry is due to self-field effect, not intrinsic SDE

additional_measurements.description: Junction regime: W >> λJ (no field modulations of Ic observed)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Long junction limit results in highly nonuniform supercurrent distribution; Ic vs H shape depends on field sweep direction — this is the self-field effect, an extrinsic source of Ic+/Ic- asymmetry that must be distinguished from intrinsic SDE in qubit applications

anomalous_observations.description: Device 5 shows ~5% asymmetry in Ic+/Ic- as function of field, superficially resembling a diode effect, but this is a same-handedness (P4132/P4132) control device where intrinsic SDE is not expected
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The asymmetry in device 5 is attributed to the extrinsic self-field effect arising from the W >> λJ (long junction) regime, evidenced by: (1) no oscillations of Ic vs H are observed, (2) the central peak is skewed rather than showing horizontal shift, and (3) Ic vs H shape depends on direction of field sweep

catchall.free_notes: Right-handed/right-handed (P4132/P4132) bicrystal junction in the long junction limit (W >> λJ). No Fraunhofer-like oscillations observed in Ic vs H. Width and critical current density could not be estimated from field modulation. Used as counter-example to distinguish self-field effect from intrinsic SDE — the two phenomena produce qualitatively different shapes of Ic± vs H: self-field gives skewed peaks, intrinsic SDE gives horizontal shift between Ic+ and Ic- curves.

Device 1 (single crystal Mo3Al2C).profile_notes: Mo3Al2C is a chiral superconductor not covered by any specific vocabulary term; classified as 'other'. Device 1 is a single crystal control with no patterned microwave device and no loss measurements, so film_only and not_applicable coherence tier apply. Growth is by high-temperature flux reaction, classified as 'other'.

Device 2 (P4132/P4332, right-handed/left.profile_notes: Junction device based on pressed bicrystal Mo3Al2C single crystals with native oxide tunnel barrier; no microwave qubit or resonator measurement, so coherence_tier is not_applicable. Surface oxide is explicitly identified as the tunnel barrier and a potential TLS source. Key correlation to mean_free_path_to_coherence_length is included because W/λJ ratio (junction regime) is explicitly analyzed and determines transport behavior.

Device 3 (P4132/P4332, right-handed/left.profile_notes: Second right-handed/left-handed bicrystal junction; closest to short junction limit among opposite-chirality devices. IcRn of 2.69 mV approaches theoretical maximum, indicating high-quality native oxide tunnel barrier. No microwave coherence measurement, so not_applicable for coherence_tier.

Device 4 (P4132/P4132, right-handed/righ.profile_notes: Control device (same-handedness junction) used to confirm that the superconducting diode effect requires opposite crystal chirality at the interface. Classified as cross_platform_comparison because explicit comparison to Device 3 (opposite chirality, matched geometry) is the central scientific argument.

Device 5 (P4132/P4132, right-handed/righ.profile_notes: Long-junction (W >> λJ) same-handedness control used to distinguish intrinsic superconducting diode effect from extrinsic self-field effect; loss_mechanism_identification applies because the paper explicitly attributes the observed Ic asymmetry to the self-field mechanism rather than intrinsic chirality. No microwave coherence measurement, so not_applicable for coherence_tier.

additional_measurements.description: Microwave resonance frequency of Felix superconducting resonator
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets the operating frequency for microwave-to-optical transduction; must match optical doublet spectral gap for intra-cavity frequency matching, directly determining transducer operating point relative to qubit frequencies

additional_measurements.description: Microwave mode intrinsic loss rate κm,in for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Intrinsic microwave loss rate limits the internal quality factor of the superconducting resonator; higher loss reduces transduction efficiency η via the cooperativity expression, setting an upper bound on achievable link transmission

additional_measurements.description: Microwave mode external coupling rate κm,ex for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Determines coupling efficiency fraction κm,ex/κm in the transduction efficiency formula; optimizing this ratio relative to intrinsic loss is directly analogous to Qi/Qc optimization in resonator design

additional_measurements.description: Microwave mode total loss rate κm for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Total microwave linewidth sets the 3 dB transduction bandwidth (34.5 MHz measured for Felix); bandwidth must exceed qubit control pulse bandwidth for coherent state transfer

additional_measurements.description: Optical red sideband mode frequency ω− for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Pump laser frequency for M2O transduction; must be in telecom band for low-loss fibre transmission

additional_measurements.description: Optical red sideband mode intrinsic loss rate κ−,in for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Optical cavity intrinsic loss limits intra-cavity photon number and hence transduction cooperativity; lower loss would improve transduction efficiency

additional_measurements.description: Optical red sideband mode external coupling rate κ−,ex for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Determines pump coupling efficiency into optical cavity; affects intra-cavity pump photon number and thus enhanced coupling Geo

additional_measurements.description: Optical red sideband mode total loss rate κ− for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Total optical linewidth of pump mode; affects pump photon number and transduction cooperativity

additional_measurements.description: Optical blue sideband mode frequency ω+ for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Up-converted signal frequency; must match Albert's blue sideband for inter-cavity frequency matching enabling fridge-to-fridge coherent link

additional_measurements.description: Optical blue sideband mode intrinsic loss rate κ+,in for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Signal mode optical loss; directly reduces transduction efficiency via κ+,ex/κ+ factor in η expression

additional_measurements.description: Optical blue sideband mode external coupling rate κ+,ex for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Output coupling efficiency of up-converted optical signal; determines fraction of intracavity photons that escape to the fibre link

additional_measurements.description: Optical blue sideband mode total loss rate κ+ for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets signal mode linewidth and coupling efficiency ratio

additional_measurements.description: Electro-optic single-photon coupling rate geo for Felix
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Fundamental coupling strength between microwave and optical modes; directly enters cooperativity C = 4Geo²/(κm·κ+) and hence transduction efficiency; increasing geo is the primary route to higher efficiency

additional_measurements.description: On-chip transduction efficiency at ~4 mW optical power for Felix
additional_measurements.source: Figure 4c and page 4 paragraph 1: 'Both transducers can achieve 0.1% transduction efficiency at an optical power near 4 mW'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Directly sets the link transmission budget; 80 dB improvement over commercial EOMs means milliwatt-level optical drive is sufficient rather than watt-level, enabling co-location with superconducting qubits without exceeding refrigerator cooling power

additional_measurements.description: 3 dB M2O conversion bandwidth for Felix
additional_measurements.source: Page 4, Results section: 'The 3 dB conversion bandwidth is 34.5 MHz for Felix'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Bandwidth must exceed qubit pulse bandwidth for coherent gate operations transmitted over the photonic link; sets the clock rate of the quantum network

additional_measurements.description: Ring radius for Felix photonic molecule
additional_measurements.source: Page 4, Frequency matching section: 'the ring radius is 61.7 μm for Felix'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Determines optical FSR and hence the inter-cavity frequency mismatch with Albert via Vernier effect; critical design parameter for achieving inter-cavity frequency matching

additional_measurements.description: Nb film thickness for superconducting resonator
additional_measurements.source: Methods, Device fabrication: 'A layer of 100 nm Nb is deposited by ebeam evaporation'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Nb film thickness affects kinetic inductance, surface impedance, and microwave quality factor; also affects electrode gap geometry which controls piezoelectric loss and optical heating noise

additional_measurements.description: Optical intrinsic quality factor before Nb deposition
additional_measurements.source: Methods, Device fabrication: 'we can observe more than 4 × 10⁶ intrinsic optical quality factor'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Baseline optical quality before superconductor integration; higher optical Q increases intra-cavity photon number and transduction efficiency

additional_measurements.description: Optical intrinsic quality factor after Nb deposition and patterning
additional_measurements.source: Methods, Device fabrication: 'the optical Q drops to ~1 × 10⁶ after Nb deposition and microwave resonator pattern etching'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: 4x degradation in optical Q after Nb integration limits transduction efficiency; authors note mechanism is unclear but suggest flip-chip bonding of Nb resonator as mitigation

additional_measurements.description: Electro-optic coefficient r33 used for transduction
additional_measurements.source: Page 3, Results: 'interact with the optical transverse-magnetic (TM) modes in the AlN ring through the electro-optic coefficient r33 ≈ 1 pm V⁻¹'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Fundamental material parameter governing geo; AlN's r33 is weaker than LiNbO3 but AlN has photorefractive-free and charge-screening-free operation enabling stable cryogenic performance

additional_measurements.source: Methods, Device fabrication: 'We deposit 2.5 μm SiO2 as the cladding by PECVD'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Gap between Nb electrodes and AlN ring (~2.5 μm) is much smaller than previous work (14 μm) and is identified as causing ~10 dB higher noise; increasing cladding thickness suppresses piezoelectric loss and thermal noise from light in the ring

additional_measurements.source: Page 5, paragraph 3: 'small gap size of ~2.5 μm between the Nb electrodes and the AlN ring'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Smaller gap increases geo but also increases piezoelectric loss and optical heating of the microwave mode (thermal noise photons); authors identify this as the dominant noise source in this device

anomalous_observations.description: Added noise is ~10 dB higher than previous AlN transducer work by the same group, despite similar design
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The gap between Nb electrodes and AlN ring is ~2.5 μm, much smaller than the 14 μm gap in their previous work. Light confined in the AlN ring causes heating of the microwave mode via piezoelectric coupling. On-resonance n̄_mode is notably higher than off-resonance n̄_mode, confirming light inside the ring (not scattered light from grating couplers) is responsible.

anomalous_observations.description: Optical Q factor drops from >4×10⁶ to ~1×10⁶ after Nb deposition and resonator patterning, even with sufficiently thick cladding
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Mechanism is unclear. Authors suggest that fabricating the Nb resonator separately and bonding it to the AlN chip via flip-chip bonding could mitigate this degradation.

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that increasing electrode-to-AlN-ring gap size reduces piezoelectric loss and improves microwave quality factor, suppressing thermal noise without substantially compromising transduction efficiency (g²eo·Qm figure of merit remains approximately flat with increasing cladding thickness)
correlations_observed.measurement_b: Piezoelectric-limited microwave Qm and thermal noise occupancy n̄_mode
correlations_observed.nature: Increasing gap increases Qm (positive) and reduces noise (positive) while geo decreases (negative), but net figure of merit g²eo·Qm is approximately constant — authors state noise can be reduced without compromising efficiency

schema_promotion_candidates.description: The vacuum electro-optic coupling rate geo in Hz (or 2π×Hz), representing the coupling strength between a single microwave photon and a single optical photon in the transducer cavity
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: geo is the fundamental figure of merit for electro-optic transducers: it directly sets the cooperativity C = 4n−·geo²/(κm·κ+) and hence transduction efficiency η. Tracking geo across samples allows systematic comparison of material and geometry improvements. It is distinct from transduction efficiency (which also depends on cavity linewidths and pump power) and is the intrinsic material+geometry coupling parameter.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: The on-chip microwave-to-optical (or optical-to-microwave) transduction efficiency η in percent, measured at a specified optical pump power
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This is the key device-level figure of merit for quantum transducers enabling photonic links between refrigerators. It directly sets the link transmission budget and determines whether single-photon-level signals can be transmitted coherently. Without tracking this, it is impossible to compare transducer performance across platforms (AlN, LiNbO3, optomechanical, etc.).

schema_promotion_candidates.description: The thermal occupancy n̄_mode of the superconducting microwave resonator mode under optical pumping conditions, in units of photons
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Added noise from optical heating is the primary barrier to quantum-coherent transduction. n̄_mode under operating conditions directly sets the added noise of the transducer, which must be <<1 photon for quantum state transfer fidelity. Tracking this across samples and gap sizes enables systematic noise engineering.

catchall.free_notes: Felix is transducer 1 (M2O up-converter) with microwave frequency ~9.960 GHz and ring radius 61.7 μm. Both transducers share the same AlN-on-sapphire platform and Nb superconducting resonator architecture. The Nb is deposited by ebeam evaporation at ~10⁻⁹ torr. The SiO2 cladding (2.5 μm) serves as both optical cladding and electrode separation layer. The full fabrication uses two rounds of AlN etching (400 nm + 600 nm = 1 μm total) with Cl2/BCl3/Ar ICP. Annealing at 1000°C for 2h is applied to the SiO2 cladding. Characterization performed at 50 mK in a Bluefors LD400 dilution refrigerator. Optical pulses: 2.5 μs width, 1 kHz repetition rate.

additional_measurements.source: Page 4, Frequency matching section: '60.0 μm for Albert'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Slightly smaller than Felix (61.7 μm), creating ΔFSR = 2π×11 GHz difference that enables Vernier-effect frequency matching; asymmetric photonic molecule design is the key innovation for inter-cavity frequency matching

additional_measurements.description: Microwave mode thermal occupancy n̄_mode under on-resonance optical excitation at 1.5 mW
additional_measurements.value: ~6 photons (read from Figure 5b at 1.5 mW on-resonance)
additional_measurements.source: Figure 5b, on-resonance curve at 1.5 mW on-chip optical power
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Added noise of ~6 photons at 1.5 mW is far above the quantum limit (<<1 photon required); this is the primary barrier to quantum-coherent operation of the link. Dominated by light-induced heating via small electrode-AlN gap

additional_measurements.description: DC tuning voltage applied to Albert for frequency matching
additional_measurements.source: Page 4: 'By applying 160 V to Albert and −160 V to Felix, we are able to obtain ω+,Felix = ω+,Albert'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Large DC voltages required for frequency tuning; authors confirm no electrode breakdown observed. DC voltages anticipated to have negligible effect on remote qubits if transducer is on a separate fridge flange

anomalous_observations.description: On-resonance thermal noise (n̄_mode) is significantly higher than off-resonance noise for Albert, while previous AlN transducer work showed nearly unchanged noise between on- and off-resonance conditions
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The small ~2.5 μm gap between Nb electrodes and AlN ring (vs 14 μm in prior work) causes light circulating inside the AlN ring to heat the Nb microwave resonator via piezoelectric coupling, dominating the noise. Scattered light from grating couplers is the noise source in the off-resonance case.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Intrinsic optical quality factor of the ring resonator, derivable from κ+,in or κ−,in (Q = ω/κ_in), both before and after superconductor integration
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Optical Q directly determines intra-cavity photon number at given pump power, hence transduction cooperativity and efficiency. The 4x degradation from >4×10⁶ to ~1×10⁶ after Nb deposition is a critical processing result. Tracking optical Q before/after Nb integration across samples would identify the loss mechanism and guide process improvement.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Methods, Device fabrication paragraph; Extended Data Table 1 κ+,in and κ−,in values

catchall.free_notes: Albert is transducer 2 (O2M down-converter) with microwave frequency ~4.606 GHz and ring radius 60.0 μm. The asymmetric FSR design (Felix 61.7 μm, Albert 60.0 μm) gives ΔFSR = 2π×11 GHz, resulting in a Vernier periodicity of ~90 nm and initial frequency mismatch of only 3.8 GHz before DC tuning. Both transducers operate at 50 mK. Albert's noise characterization used a TWPA (~14 dB gain at 4.8 GHz pump) and HEMT amplifier chain. The 1-km fibre spool is Corning SMF-28e with 0.2 dB/km loss. Total fibre-to-chip coupling loss is −23.6 dB (4 grating couplers × −5.9 dB each). On-chip optical pump power for both transducers in the photonic link experiment is ~3 mW.

Felix.profile_notes: Primary superconducting element is the Nb resonator (evaporated); AlN is the electro-optic medium, not the superconductor. Coherence tier is early_exploration based on the microwave intrinsic loss rate of 2π×23.3 MHz, corresponding to a very low Qi (~430), far below 1e5. Loss mechanisms include TLS_interface (Nb/SiO2/AlN stack) and quasiparticle (optical heating of Nb via piezoelectric coupling through small electrode-AlN gap).

Albert.profile_notes: Same Nb-on-AlN-on-sapphire platform as Felix; Albert has lower intrinsic microwave loss (2π×2.4 MHz, Qi~1920) but still early_exploration tier. The correlation between electrode-AlN gap size and piezoelectric-limited Qm is explicitly evidenced by authors, but no exact key_correlation vocabulary term captures this; film_thickness_to_loss is the closest proxy for the geometry-to-loss relationship discussed.

catchall.free_notes: This paper reports a thin-film LiNbO3 microring OPO device, not a superconducting qubit device. The relevance to the superconducting qubit database is indirect: (1) the substrate stack includes a 50-nm NbN layer on Si, which is a superconducting material of direct relevance; (2) the device is fabricated with C2QA (Co-design Center for Quantum Advantage) funding, which targets quantum computing applications; (3) the squeezed light and photon pair generation capabilities are potentially relevant to qubit readout and quantum networking. The LN film is z-cut, 600 nm thick, from NanoLN commercial supplier. Poling was performed by e-beam lithography patterned Ni electrodes with 650 V pulses of 10 ms duration. Post-fabrication anneal: 200°C for 12 hours. The NbN layer in the substrate is used as part of the poling electrode structure, not as a superconducting circuit element in this work. Pump wavelength ~775 nm (TM00 mode), signal/idler ~1554 nm (TE00 mode), converting via d31 nonlinear coefficient.

DL-OPO microring resonator.profile_notes: This sample is a LiNbO3 photonic microring OPO, not a superconducting qubit device; the only superconducting material present is a 50-nm NbN layer used as a poling electrode in the substrate stack, not as a microwave or qubit element. Classification defaults to niobium_nitride as the sole superconducting material in the record, but coherence_tier is not_applicable since no superconducting device performance is measured.

additional_measurements.description: Total T-gate count for physically meaningful scalar field theory simulation (1+1D, occupation cutoff N=9 or field cutoff k=20, volume |Omega|=100)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: T-gate count is the primary resource metric for fault-tolerant quantum computation; 10^12 T-gates on a superconducting surface-code computer with 100 ns cycle time corresponds to ~1 day of runtime, directly setting the feasibility threshold for quantum field theory simulation on superconducting hardware

additional_measurements.description: Total physical qubit count for fault-tolerant scalar field theory simulation using surface code with physical error rate p ~ 10^-3
additional_measurements.source: Table II, row 'Total physical qubits including computational qubits', column p/p_th = 10^-1
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Physical qubit count directly sets the hardware scale required; comparison to best chemistry simulation results (~10^6 qubits) indicates field theory simulation requires ~28x more qubits at p~10^-3, motivating improvement of physical error rates toward 10^-4

additional_measurements.description: Total physical qubit count for fault-tolerant scalar field theory simulation using surface code with physical error rate p ~ 10^-4
additional_measurements.source: Table II, row 'Total physical qubits including computational qubits', column p/p_th = 10^-2; Section V paragraph 8
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: At p~10^-4, the simulation becomes feasible with ~4×10^6 physical qubits, placing it within striking distance of leading chemistry simulations and directly motivating improvement of superconducting qubit error rates by one order of magnitude

additional_measurements.description: Number of logical qubits required for occupation-basis Trotter algorithm (N=9, |Omega|=100)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Logical qubit count determines the error-correction overhead and code distance requirements, which set the physical qubit multiplier for superconducting surface-code architectures

additional_measurements.description: Number of logical qubits required for qubitization-based amplitude-basis algorithm (k=20, |Omega|=100)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Qubitization-based amplitude algorithm requires 2x fewer logical qubits than occupation-basis Trotter, directly reducing physical qubit overhead on superconducting hardware

additional_measurements.description: Surface code cycle time assumed for runtime estimates
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: The 100 ns surface code cycle time is the key hardware parameter linking T-gate count to wall-clock runtime; this is characteristic of current superconducting qubit platforms and directly determines the ~1 day computation time estimate

additional_measurements.description: Minimum computation time for 10^12 T-gates given 100 ns cycle time
additional_measurements.source: Section V paragraph 2; Table II row 'Total time for 10^12 A states with parallelization'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets the practical wall-clock feasibility bound for scalar field theory simulation on superconducting hardware with surface code error correction

additional_measurements.description: Number of parallel AAA magic state factories required at p ~ 10^-3
additional_measurements.source: Table II, row 'Number of parallel AAA factories', column p/p_th = 10^-1
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Magic state distillation factory count determines the space overhead of fault-tolerant computation; reducing this requires either better physical error rates or more efficient distillation protocols for superconducting qubit systems

additional_measurements.description: Number of parallel AAA magic state factories required at p ~ 10^-4
additional_measurements.source: Table II, row 'Number of parallel AAA factories', column p/p_th = 10^-2

schema_promotion_candidates.description: The physical measurement cycle time of the surface code in nanoseconds, which converts T-gate counts to wall-clock computation time
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This parameter is the key link between algorithmic T-gate counts and practical runtime on superconducting hardware; tracking it across platforms allows direct comparison of feasibility timelines and motivates hardware improvements toward shorter cycle times

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Ratio of physical gate error rate to surface code threshold error rate (dimensionless), which determines magic state distillation overhead
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This ratio is the dominant factor setting total physical qubit count for fault-tolerant computation; a 10x improvement (from 10^-1 to 10^-2) reduces total qubit requirements by ~7x for this simulation, making it the single most impactful hardware metric for fault-tolerant quantum field theory simulation

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Total number of non-Clifford T gates required to execute a quantum algorithm, which dominates the cost of fault-tolerant implementation
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: T-gate count is the primary complexity metric for fault-tolerant quantum algorithms on superconducting hardware with surface codes; tracking this across simulation targets enables systematic comparison of computational feasibility and hardware requirements

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Number of parallel AAA magic state distillation factories required to achieve target computation time, which determines space overhead
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Factory count directly multiplies the per-factory qubit cost to give total distillation overhead; it captures the space-time tradeoff in fault-tolerant computation and is sensitive to both the physical error rate and the target runtime

catchall.free_notes: This is a theoretical resource estimation paper for quantum simulation of scalar phi^4 field theory, not a materials characterization paper. It does not report any film deposition, substrate, or device fabrication data. The paper is relevant to the superconducting qubit research database only insofar as it: (1) assumes a superconducting quantum computer with surface code and 100 ns cycle time as the target hardware platform, (2) derives concrete physical qubit counts (4.2×10^6 at p~10^-4, 2.8×10^7 at p~10^-3) that set hardware targets for the field, and (3) explicitly benchmarks against chemistry simulation results. The two resource scenarios correspond to physical error rates p~10^-3 (current state of art for superconducting qubits) and p~10^-4 (near-future target). No sample-level materials data exists in this paper; the single sample entry represents the resource estimation scenario rather than a physical device.

surface-code-resource-estimate-p1e-3.profile_notes: This record contains no materials characterization data whatsoever; it is a theoretical fault-tolerant quantum computation resource estimation paper for scalar field theory simulation. All vocabulary assignments reflect the absence of any physical sample, film, or device measurement.
