relevance_json.relevance_reason: The paper explicitly acknowledges C2QA (Co-design Center for Quantum Advantage) funding, and directly covers superconducting qubit coherence, qubit-oscillator gate fidelity, bosonic error correction, and quantum circuit architectures for superconducting qubits and microwave resonators.
relevance_json.paper_type_reason: This is a tutorial/review that synthesizes results from many primary sources across superconducting, trapped-ion, and neutral-atom platforms without reporting original experimental measurements.
relevance_json.title: Hybrid Oscillator-Qubit Quantum Processors: Instruction Set Architectures, Abstract Machine Models, and Applications

extraction_json.title: Hybrid Oscillator-Qubit Quantum Processors: Instruction Set Architectures, Abstract Machine Models, and Applications

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Photon lifetime (T1) of superconducting microwave cavity oscillator modes, in microseconds
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: Oscillator lifetime directly sets the memory coherence time for bosonic error-correcting codes and is the dominant figure of merit distinguishing hybrid CV-DV architectures from qubit-only systems. The paper repeatedly emphasizes that 3D superconducting cavities achieve T1 on the order of 1-35 ms, orders of magnitude longer than transmon qubits (~100-500 us), making this the key hardware advantage of the hybrid platform. Tracking this separately from qubit T1 is essential for resource estimation.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: transmon-qubit lifetimes by an order of magnitude or more... bosonic superconducting resonators are known to offer long coherence times which exceed transmon-qubit lifetimes by an order of magnitude or more... a transmon controller with a lifetime of T1 = 110 μs has been coupled to a cavity with a lifetime of T1 = 35 ms

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Gate fidelity of parametric beam-splitter operations between two bosonic cavity modes, as a percentage
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: Beam-splitter operations are the primary entangling primitive between oscillator modes in the superconducting hybrid architecture and are used for SWAP networks enabling long-range connectivity. The paper cites ~100 ns, 99.92% fidelity as state-of-the-art, and this metric directly determines circuit depth limits for bosonic quantum error correction and simulation algorithms. It is distinct from qubit-qubit gate fidelity and has no current schema field.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: SWAP gates (~100 ns, 99.92% fidelity in the single-photon subspace [70]) that enable remote communication and entanglement of both oscillators and qubits

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Dispersive coupling strength chi between a transmon qubit and a coupled microwave cavity, in MHz (chi/2pi)
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: The dispersive shift is the key native interaction enabling conditional oscillator-qubit gates (SNAP gates, conditional rotations, parity measurements) in circuit QED. Its magnitude relative to cavity linewidth kappa and qubit decay rate gamma determines whether the system is in the strong-dispersive regime, which is required for high-fidelity photon-number-resolved operations and bosonic QEC. This parameter is central to the phase-space and Fock-space ISAs described in the paper.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: In the so-called 'strong-dispersive regime,' [238,683] where chi greatly exceeds the damping rate of the cavity and the transmon, the two resonance peaks of the cavity are spectrally well resolved.

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Type of bosonic quantum error-correcting code used to encode a logical qubit in an oscillator (e.g., GKP, cat, binomial, dual-rail)
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: The choice of bosonic code fundamentally determines the instruction set, noise model, and logical gate compilation strategy. Different codes (GKP, four-legged cat, binomial, dual-rail) have different error correction capabilities, gate overheads, and hardware requirements. Tracking this allows systematic comparison of QEC overhead and logical qubit performance across experiments and platforms.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: Particular codes are most naturally implemented using particular ISAs. For example, translation symmetric codes, such as the GKP codes shown in Fig. 16(a), are most natural in the phase-space ISA, while the rotation symmetric codes, like four-legged cat codes, shown in Fig. 16(b) are most natural in the Fock-space ISA.

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Ratio of logical qubit coherence time to the best physical component coherence time, dimensionless (G > 1 means beyond break-even)
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: The error correction gain G is the primary experimental figure of merit for bosonic QEC demonstrations, indicating whether the code actually extends coherence beyond the best available physical resource. The paper defines break-even as G = 1 and cites G ~ 2.3 for GKP semiautonomous correction. This directly maps to practical utility for quantum computing and should be tracked as a structured field to compare QEC experiments across platforms.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: Error correction of logical qubits encoded into bosonic modes has recently been achieved at and beyond the break-even point (for memory) where the quantum coherence of the error-corrected logical qubit exceeds the coherence of the best of the available uncorrected qubits in the system [63,65,68,418] so that the error-correction gain G > 1 exceeds unity.

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Maximum Fock state number Nmax used as the oscillator Hilbert-space truncation in simulations or hardware operation
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: The photon number cutoff determines the effective Hilbert space dimension of the oscillator, sets hardware energy limits, affects gate fidelity through Kerr nonlinearities, and determines qubit register size needed for classical simulation. It is a fundamental design parameter for bosonic hardware and algorithm resource estimation, directly impacting the efficiency comparison between oscillator and qubit-only implementations analyzed in Sec. VIII.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: we define a maximum allowed boson number, Nmax, to restrict the oscillator Hilbert-space dimensionality to d = Nmax + 1... If we wish to represent all occupations up to a Fock state cutoff of, e.g., K + 1 = 64, a seven-qubit register is required for the binary representation

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Single-mode squeezing achieved in a bosonic mode, expressed in decibels (dB)
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: Squeezing is a fundamental resource for CV quantum information processing, enabling GKP state preparation, entanglement generation, and sensing beyond standard quantum limits. The paper reports state-of-the-art of ~10 dB in superconducting circuits and notes Kerr effects limit maximum achievable squeezing. Tracking this enables comparison of hardware capabilities across platforms and over time.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: The current state of the art in superconducting circuits yields squeezing up to factors e^{2r} ~ 10 dB [60]

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Physical platform implementing the hybrid CV-DV processor: superconducting, trapped-ion, or neutral-atom
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: The three platforms reviewed (superconducting, trapped-ion, neutral-atom) have fundamentally different native gate sets, connectivity topologies, noise models, oscillator frequencies, and scalability properties. Platform type determines which ISA is most natural, what coherence times are achievable, and which compilation strategies are applicable. Without this field, cross-platform comparison in the database is impossible.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: Experimental realizations of this model include superconducting, trapped-ion, and neutral-atom platforms.

primary_paper_queue.citation: A. Eickbusch, V. Sivak, A. Z. Ding, S. S. Elder, S. R. Jha, J. Venkatraman, B. Royer, S. M. Girvin, R. J. Schoelkopf, and M. H. Devoret, Fast universal control of an oscillator with weak dispersive coupling to a qubit, Nat. Phys. 18, 1464 (2022).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Demonstrates the phase-space ISA experimentally on a superconducting cavity-transmon system, including echoed conditional displacement gates, conditional squeezing, and GKP state preparation. Reports key hardware parameters including dispersive shift, gate times (~24 ns qubit rotations, |alpha| µs for conditional displacements), and numerical optimization of bosonic circuits. Central experimental reference for the compilation methods described throughout the review.

primary_paper_queue.citation: V. V. Sivak, A. Eickbusch, B. Royer, S. Singh, I. Tsioutsios, S. Ganjam, A. Miano, B. L. Brock, A. Z. Ding, L. Frunzio, S. M. Girvin, R. J. Schoelkopf, and M. H. Devoret, Real-time quantum error correction beyond break-even, Nature 616, 50 (2023).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Demonstrates GKP bosonic QEC with error correction gain G > 1 beyond break-even using the semiautonomous sBs stabilization protocol. Reports oscillator and qubit coherence times, GKP state fidelity, and error correction gain. This is one of the key experimental milestones motivating the hybrid CV-DV architecture reviewed in the paper.

primary_paper_queue.citation: B. L. Brock, S. Singh, A. Eickbusch, V. V. Sivak, A. Z. Ding, L. Frunzio, S. M. Girvin, and M. H. Devoret, Quantum error correction of qudits beyond break-even, Nature 641, 612 (2025).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Extends GKP QEC beyond break-even to qudit (d=3,4 level) encodings, demonstrating the first error-corrected qudit in an oscillator. Reports coherence times and error correction gain for higher-dimensional logical systems, directly relevant to the qudit encoding and oscillator-to-DV mapping discussed in the review.

primary_paper_queue.citation: Y. Lu, A. Maiti, J. W. O. Garmon, S. Ganjam, Y. Zhang, J. Claes, L. Frunzio, S. M. Girvin, and R. J. Schoelkopf, High-fidelity parametric beamsplitting with a parity-protected converter, Nat. Commun. 14, 5767 (2023).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Demonstrates high-fidelity parametric beam-splitter gates (~100 ns, 99.92% fidelity) between superconducting microwave resonators, which is the key entangling primitive for the oscillator SWAP network and long-range connectivity in the hybrid architecture. This fidelity figure is cited prominently as defining the state-of-the-art for the superconducting hybrid platform.

primary_paper_queue.citation: B. J. Chapman, S. J. de Graaf, S. H. Xue, Y. Zhang, J. Teoh, J. C. Curtis, T. Tsunoda, A. Eickbusch, A. P. Read, A. Koottandavida, S. O. Mundhada, L. Frunzio, M. H. Devoret, S. M. Girvin, and R. J. Schoelkopf, High-on-off-ratio beam-splitter interaction for gates on bosonically encoded qubits, PRX Quantum 4, 020355 (2023).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Reports the high-on-off-ratio beam-splitter gate enabling the superconducting hybrid architecture, with detailed characterization of gate fidelity, speed, and on/off ratio relevant to SWAP network compilation and dual-rail qubit operations.

primary_paper_queue.citation: P. Campagne-Ibarcq, A. Eickbusch, S. Touzard, E. Zalys-Geller, N. E. Frattini, V. V. Sivak, P. Reinhold, S. Puri, S. Shankar, R. J. Schoelkopf, L. Frunzio, M. Mirrahimi, and M. H. Devoret, Quantum error correction of a qubit encoded in grid states of an oscillator, Nature 584, 368 (2020).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: First experimental demonstration of GKP QEC in a superconducting system, reporting oscillator coherence, GKP state preparation fidelity, and the conditional displacement gate implementation. Foundational experimental paper for the GKP QEC discussion in the review.

primary_paper_queue.citation: J. D. Teoh, P. Winkel, H. K. Babla, B. J. Chapman, J. Claes, S. J. de Graaf, J. W. O. Garmon, W. D. Kalfus, Y. Lu, A. Maiti, K. Sahay, N. Thakur, T. Tsunoda, S. H. Xue, L. Frunzio, S. M. Girvin, S. Puri, and R. J. Schoelkopf, Dual-rail encoding with superconducting cavities, PNAS 120, e2221736120 (2023).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Introduces and characterizes the dual-rail qubit encoding in superconducting cavities, which is a key bosonic QEC code discussed extensively in Sec. V. Reports logical qubit coherence, erasure detection efficiency, and gate operations relevant to the dual-rail ISA discussion.

primary_paper_queue.citation: E. Crane, K. C. Smith, A. Schuckert, T. Tomesh, A. Eickbusch, J. M. Martyn, S. Kühn, L. Funcke, N. Wiebe, M. A. DeMarco, I. L. Chuang, and S. Girvin, Hybrid oscillator-qubit quantum processors: Simulating fermions, bosons, and gauge fields, ArXiv:2409.03747.
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Companion paper providing in-depth compilation techniques for fermion-boson models and lattice gauge theories on hybrid CV-DV hardware, referenced throughout as Ref. [41]. Contains detailed gate count analysis, resource estimation, and experimental protocols for quantum simulation applications that complement the high-level discussion in the review.

primary_paper_queue.citation: O. Milul, B. Guttel, U. Goldblatt, S. Hazanov, L. M. Joshi, D. Chausovsky, N. Kahn, E. Çiftyürek, F. Lafont, and S. Rosenblum, Superconducting cavity qubit with tens of milliseconds single-photon coherence time, PRX Quantum 4, 030336 (2023).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Reports superconducting 3D cavity T1 = 35 ms, which is the state-of-the-art oscillator coherence time cited in the review to motivate hybrid architectures over qubit-only systems. Contains detailed materials and fabrication characterization relevant to superconducting cavity loss mechanisms.

primary_paper_queue.citation: A. Romanenko, R. Pilipenko, S. Zorzetti, D. Frolov, M. Awida, S. Belomestnykh, S. Posen, and A. Grassellino, Three-dimensional superconducting resonators at T = 20 mK with photon lifetimes up to tau = 2 s, Phys. Rev. Appl. 13, 034032 (2020).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Reports record 2-second photon lifetime in a 3D superconducting resonator, setting the upper bound on oscillator coherence achievable with superconducting technology and directly motivating the hybrid CV-DV architecture. Highly relevant to the oscillator lifetime as a proposed schema field.

primary_paper_queue.citation: C. Flühmann, T. L. Nguyen, M. Marinelli, V. Negnevitsky, K. Mehta, and J. P. Home, Encoding a qubit in a trapped-ion mechanical oscillator, Nature 566, 513 (2019).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: First demonstration of GKP encoding in a trapped-ion motional mode, establishing the trapped-ion platform as a viable implementation for hybrid CV-DV processing. Contains experimental characterization of sideband ISA gates and oscillator state tomography relevant to the ion-trap section of the review.

primary_paper_queue.citation: A. L. Shaw, P. Scholl, R. Finkelstein, R. B.-S. Tsai, J. Choi, and M. Endres, Erasure cooling, control, and hyperentanglement of motion in optical tweezers, Science 388, 845 (2025).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Demonstrates motional mode lifetimes exceeding qubit lifetimes in neutral-atom tweezers and hyperentanglement of qubit and motional degrees of freedom, establishing neutral atoms as a viable hybrid CV-DV platform. Directly cited as a key recent advance motivating the neutral-atom section of the review.

primary_paper_queue.citation: S. Singh, B. Royer, and S. M. Girvin, Towards non-Abelian quantum signal processing: Efficient control of hybrid continuous- and discrete-variable architectures, ArXiv:2504.19992.
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Companion theory paper developing the non-Abelian QSP framework for hybrid CV-DV systems, which is one of the key novel contributions of the review. Contains detailed derivations of cat state preparation, GKP state preparation and readout, and stabilization circuits using phase-space ISA with analytical error bounds.

primary_paper_queue.citation: R. T. Sutherland and R. Srinivas, Universal hybrid quantum computing in trapped ions, Phys. Rev. A 104, 032609 (2021).
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Develops universal hybrid CV-DV quantum computing using the sideband ISA in trapped ions, providing theoretical framework and gate sequences for the ion-trap implementation discussed in Appendix A2. Contains universal control proofs and cross-compilation between sideband and other ISAs.

primary_paper_queue.citation: T. J. Stavenger, E. Crane, K. C. Smith, C. T. Kang, S. M. Girvin, and N. Wiebe, C2QA - Bosonic Qiskit, in 2022 IEEE High Performance Extreme Computing Conference (HPEC), Waltham, MA, USA, 2022, p. 1.
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Introduces BOSONIC QISKIT, the software framework used throughout the review for hybrid CV-DV circuit simulation and demonstration. Contains description of the qumode register abstraction, CVCircuit class, and simulation examples relevant to the programming language layer of the architecture stack.

additional_measurements.source: Page 2 paragraph 1: 'Nb2O5 in particular having a loss tangent on the order of ~1 × 10−2'

additional_measurements.description: Surface oxide composition identified by XPS
additional_measurements.value: Nb2O5 (Nb5+ at ~207.23 eV 3d5/2), NbOx/NbO2 (Nb4+ at ~204.56 eV), metallic Nb0 (~202 eV), surface hydroxyl groups (~532.30 eV O1s)

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Layered oxidation with Nb2O5 at surface introduces high-loss TLS states; oxygen vacancies in Nb2O5 are a known source of TLS losses in superconducting Nb films

anomalous_observations.description: Tc of 8.15 K is below bulk Nb value of ~9.26 K despite using high-purity targets
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: attributed to oxygen incorporation during deposition, substrate-induced strain, and surface oxidation upon air exposure leading to Nb2O5 and sub-oxide formation (Section 3.4, page 8)

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Zero-temperature upper critical field μ0Hc2(0) in Tesla, estimated via WHH model from R-T measurements under magnetic field
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Hc2(0) directly determines the coherence length ξGL(0) via flux quantum relation; coherence length relative to mean free path distinguishes clean vs dirty limit, which determines whether vortex motion or other loss mechanisms dominate — tracking this across samples would allow systematic comparison of loss mechanisms

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Ginzburg-Landau coherence length ξGL(0) in nanometers, derived from Hc2(0)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Coherence length relative to mean free path determines whether film is in clean or dirty superconducting limit; this directly sets which loss channel (vortex motion vs other) dominates — critical for interpreting Qi measurements

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Average crystallite size in nm from Scherrer or Williamson-Hall XRD analysis
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Grain size controls grain boundary density which determines oxygen diffusion pathways and interface TLS density; systematically tracking crystallite size across deposition conditions would allow correlation with Tc, RRR, and loss tangent

catchall.free_notes: S1 is a bare Nb film grown at room temperature and exposed to air before characterization. Base pressure below 2×10^-9 Torr; working pressure 12 mTorr; sputtering power 150 W; 99.995% pure 2-inch Nb target. Substrate cleaned with Piranha solution (H2SO4:H2O2 = 2:1) then DI water, N2 dry, IPA rinse, N2 dry. All electrical measurements repeated on three separate samples per condition. Nb (110) peak at 2θ = 38.23°, lattice parameter 3.316 Å.

additional_measurements.source: Section 3, page 3: 'S2: Nb film capped in-situ with Pt (no air exposure) of thickness around 10 nm'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Capping layer thickness sets the tradeoff between oxygen barrier effectiveness and proximity-effect-induced pair breaking; too thin may not block oxidation, too thick may suppress superconductivity at interface

additional_measurements.description: Surface oxide suppression confirmed by XPS: Pt4f metallic peaks, attenuated O1s signal
additional_measurements.value: Metallic Pt0 at 70.84 eV (4f7/2) and 74.19 eV (4f5/2); O1s strongly attenuated; after 40 nm Ar+ sputtering Nb3d dominated by metallic Nb0 with minimal NbOx and near-absent Nb5+
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.2, pages 5-6: XPS analysis of S2
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Direct evidence of oxide suppression at Nb surface by Pt encapsulation; elimination of Nb2O5 (loss tangent ~10^-2) removes primary TLS source, which should improve resonator Qi and qubit coherence

additional_measurements.description: Nb (110) peak position shift due to Pt encapsulation
additional_measurements.value: Peak shifted from 38.23° (S1) to 38.63° (S2), corresponding to lattice contraction from 3.316 Å to 3.283 Å (~1.0% compressive strain)
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.1, page 4: 'the Nb (110) peak shifted from 38.23° (S1) to 38.63° (S2)'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Lattice strain from Pt/Nb mismatch may influence defect density and scattering, affecting mean free path and coherence length; strain-induced defects could contribute to TLS losses

anomalous_observations.description: Pt-capped sample (S2) exhibits higher Hc2(0) (5.8 T) despite having higher Tc than S1, which is counterintuitive since higher Tc typically correlates with lower Hc2 in WHH theory
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: consistent with dirty-limit superconductor behavior where increased electron scattering at Pt/Nb interface reduces coherence length, thereby increasing Hc2(0) despite higher Tc. Proximity effect from Pt (strong SOC) introduces spin-flip scattering and pair-breaking. (Section 3.4.1, page 8-9)

anomalous_observations.description: W-H analysis shows Pt-capped sample (S2) has both larger crystallite size (20.18 nm) AND higher microstrain (ε=0.0068) than S1, contrary to typical trend where larger crystallites exhibit lower strain
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: anomaly attributed to Pt encapsulation layer inducing interfacial strain from lattice mismatch (Pt FCC ~3.92 Å vs Nb BCC ~3.30 Å), plus superimposition of Pt reflections on Nb (110) peak causing apparent strain amplification in W-H analysis. Not intrinsic crystal imperfections. (Section 3.1, page 3)

correlations_observed.description: Authors state Pt encapsulation improves Tc and RRR compared to bare Nb film, attributing this to suppression of surface oxide formation
correlations_observed.nature: Positive correlation: Pt-capped S2 shows Tc=8.35 K and RRR=2.14 vs S1 Tc=8.15 K and RRR=2.05

correlations_observed.description: Authors state Pt encapsulation suppresses oxygen signal at Nb surface, with O confined beneath Pt layer in TEM/EDS and attenuated in XPS
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation: Pt cap strongly reduces surface oxidation

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Material and thickness of in-situ protective capping layer deposited on superconducting film without air break
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Noble metal encapsulation is an emerging fabrication strategy to suppress TLS-generating native oxides; tracking capping layer identity and thickness alongside Tc, RRR, and Qi would enable systematic evaluation of which encapsulants best preserve superconducting performance without excessive proximity suppression

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Boolean flag and description of proximity effect between capping/contact metal and superconducting film
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Pt and other heavy metals with strong spin-orbit coupling introduce pair-breaking effects that modify ξGL and Hc2; this tradeoff between surface protection and proximity suppression is central to encapsulation strategies and should be tracked systematically

catchall.free_notes: S2 is Nb film capped in-situ with ~10 nm Pt immediately after Nb deposition, with no air exposure between layers. Pt sputtering power 100 W; same 12 mTorr working pressure; 99.995% pure Pt target. The Pt layer is polycrystalline/nanocrystalline (confirmed by GIXRD Pt(111) and Pt(200) peaks) rather than epitaxial. HAADF-STEM shows columnar Nb grain morphology retained with conformal Pt coating. EDS confirms O confined beneath Pt layer. XPS depth profiling (40 nm Ar+ sputtering) reveals metallic Nb0 dominated signal below Pt layer. Both samples measured with 10 μA DC bias current in PPMS Dynacool; measurements repeated on 3 separate samples per condition for reproducibility. Nb lattice parameter in S2: 3.283 Å (contracted ~1% relative to S1 value of 3.316 Å).

S1.profile_notes: RRR of 2.05 unambiguously places this in the dirty limit; no patterned microwave device is fabricated so coherence_tier is not_applicable. Loss mechanisms are inferred from XPS-confirmed Nb2O5 surface oxide and its known high loss tangent, not from direct Qi measurement.

S2.profile_notes: RRR of 2.14 confirms dirty limit despite Pt capping; film_only classification applies because no microwave resonator or qubit is measured. The Pt encapsulation is framed as a process optimization strategy to suppress Nb2O5 TLS sources, supported by XPS evidence of oxide suppression correlating with improved Tc and RRR.

anomalous_observations.description: Reduction in high-frequency dielectric constant for 13 nm TaN film compared to 25 nm film, despite identical material composition and band gap
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Possible causes include screening of excitonic (Sommerfeld) enhancement due to built-in electric fields from interface charges, or TaN thickness becoming comparable to excitonic Bohr radius in TaN. Authors note optical constants of TaN are likely thickness- and process-dependent.

anomalous_observations.description: Discrepancy between ellipsometry-derived SiO2 thickness (35 nm) and TEM/standalone ellipsometry thickness (43 nm) for the same thermal oxide layer beneath TaN
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Discrepancy attributed to different decay and dispersion mechanisms of electron waves vs. light waves across an interface; authors note this is not uncommon in their experience.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Optical band gap (Tauc gap) of tunnel barrier material in eV, extracted from ellipsometry Tauc-Lorentz model; temperature range may be specified
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Band gap of Josephson junction tunnel barrier directly determines barrier height and therefore the relationship between barrier thickness and critical current density. Lower band gap allows thicker barriers for equivalent tunneling current, improving ALD process control and reproducibility. This parameter is the primary figure of merit distinguishing TaN from AlOx as a tunnel barrier candidate — tracking it across samples would allow systematic comparison of junction barrier materials and their expected qubit frequency stability.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Abstract sentence 4; page 11 paragraph 1; Conclusion page 20-21

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Atomic ratio of anion to cation in compound film (e.g., N/Ta ratio for TaN), measured by XPS depth profiling
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Stoichiometry of tunnel barrier determines its electronic phase (insulating vs. conducting), band gap, and interface quality. For TaN, N/Ta ~ 1.2 distinguishes insulating Ta5N6 from conductive TaN phases used in CMOS. Incorrect stoichiometry would produce a conducting barrier that shorts the Josephson junction. Tracking this across deposition conditions is essential for process control of ALD tunnel barriers.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Abstract sentence 7; page 19 paragraph 1; Conclusion page 20 paragraph 1

schema_promotion_candidates.description: RMS surface roughness of deposited film measured by AFM, in nanometers
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Surface roughness of tunnel barrier directly affects junction uniformity and spatial variation in barrier thickness, which translates to variation in Josephson critical current and qubit frequency spread across a wafer. Low roughness (<0.5 nm) is cited as a key advantage of ALD TaN over AlOx for achieving repeatable qubit frequencies at scale.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Standard deviation of film thickness across wafer as percentage of median, measured by XRR
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Thickness uniformity of tunnel barrier across a 300 mm wafer directly determines spread in Josephson critical current and qubit operating frequencies. For scalable qubit fabrication, tight uniformity (<1%) is needed to minimize cross-talk from frequency collisions. This is a key process metric distinguishing ALD from oxidation-based barrier formation.

catchall.free_notes: This paper characterizes ALD TaN films intended as tunnel barriers in Ta-based Josephson junctions for superconducting qubits. No qubits or junctions are fabricated in this paper — it is a materials characterization study. The authors' companion paper (Bhatia et al., arXiv:2511.20266, 2025) demonstrates integration of these films as tunnel barriers in actual Josephson junctions. The substrate stack is Si(100)/SiO2(~43 nm after sputter clean from 50 nm as-deposited)/ALD TaN. An in-situ sputter clean before TaN deposition reduced SiO2 thickness by 5-15 nm. Films deposited in 300 mm cluster tool using proprietary ALD chemistry. Conditioning wafers were used to ensure chamber stability before device wafer deposition. The ALD TaN is identified as primarily amorphous (Tauc-Lorentz model fits ellipsometry) but with crystalline inclusions of hexagonal Ta5N6 phase (from SAD/XRD). Authors explicitly note future work will quantify dielectric loss tangent of ALD TaN — a critical parameter not yet measured. The paper motivates TaN over AlOx primarily on grounds of: (1) lower band gap enabling thicker barriers at same tunneling current, (2) ALD thickness/composition control vs. oxidation variability, (3) thermal stability and resistance to aging, (4) preferential nucleation of α-Ta (desired superconducting phase) on TaN surface.

Surface roughness (RMS) of 13 nm ALD TaN implied by XRR consistency with AFM data
additional_measurements.value: Consistent with <0.5 nm (XRR roughness consistent with AFM for 25 nm sample; both thicknesses characterized)

additional_measurements.description: Absence of free-carrier absorption in 13 nm ALD TaN confirmed by spectroscopic ellipsometry
additional_measurements.value: Insulating behavior confirmed (imaginary part of dielectric function small at all temperatures)

catchall.free_notes: The 13 nm ALD TaN sample shares the same deposition process and substrate stack as the 25 nm sample. Band gap and dielectric function trend are consistent between the two thicknesses, but the high-frequency dielectric constant (below band gap) is reduced for the thinner film — authors suggest possible excitonic screening or thickness-comparable-to-Bohr-radius effects. XPS depth profiling and SAD/XRD phase identification were performed primarily on the 25 nm sample; phase assignment for the 13 nm sample is by inference from same deposition process.

ALD-TaN-25nm.profile_notes: No microwave device or junction fabricated; this is a pure materials characterization study of ALD TaN as a prospective Josephson junction tunnel barrier. Transport regime is unknown as no resistivity or RRR data are reported — the film is insulating, not superconducting, so conventional transport regime classifications do not apply.

ALD-TaN-13nm.profile_notes: Same rationale as the 25 nm sample; the 13 nm film is characterized primarily via ellipsometry and XRR with phase assignment inferred from the thicker sample. The film_thickness_to_loss correlation is included because the paper explicitly documents thickness-dependent dielectric constant differences between the two samples.

additional_measurements.value: ~1e6 order (extracted from Fig. 3b fit at representative pMS)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: QTLS,0 directly sets the upper bound on resonator Qi and therefore T1 in qubits fabricated from this film

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Surface-participation-ratio-scaled loss tangent (tan delta) extracted from QTLS,0 vs SPR fit, in units of 10^-3
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This is the primary figure of merit for TLS surface loss in CPW resonators; it captures the combined MA+MS+SA interface loss normalized to geometry and is directly comparable across samples and materials systems

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Average lateral grain size in nm measured by STEM cross-section
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Grain size correlates with RRR and Tc in this study; tracking it would allow systematic comparison of how deposition conditions affect film morphology and ultimately surface loss

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Root-mean-square roughness Sq in nm measured by AFM
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Surface roughness affects TLS density at interfaces; systematic tracking across deposition conditions would reveal whether roughness or grain size is the primary driver of loss

catchall.free_notes: This chip uses the best-performing deposition condition (200°C) with wet etch fabrication. Deposition rate 0.25 nm/s, base pressure 2.9e-10 mTorr, substrate preheated to 300°C then cooled to 200°C for deposition. Film cooled in vacuum over 12 hours post-deposition.

surface_oxide_thickness_nm.source: Table II row 2: '2.66 ± 0.07 nm'

additional_measurements.value: H2O2 treatment; C1s XPS intensity reduced by 34.7 ± 5.8%
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: H2O2 removes adventitious carbon but not fabrication photoresist residue; fabrication residue contributes more to TLS loss than adventitious carbon
Same fabrication as Row 1; allows isolation of surface treatment effect

anomalous_observations.description: H2O2 treatment reduces surface carbon by ~34.7% as measured by XPS but produces no statistically significant improvement in surface loss tangent (<1 sigma)
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: H2O2 removes adventitious carbon but not fabrication photoresist residue, and the latter is the dominant hydrocarbon contributor to TLS loss. Adventitious carbon removal alone is insufficient to reduce loss.

catchall.free_notes: This chip is the H2O2 control experiment. Same deposition and etch conditions as Row 1, differing only in post-fabrication cleaning. No improvement in loss tangent vs untreated wet-etch device (within 2 sigma).

surface_oxide_thickness_nm.source: Table II row 3: '2.73 ± 0.08 nm'

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Highest roughness among three deposition temperatures; rougher surfaces may increase effective TLS-hosting interface area
Small grains with large out-of-plane <111> misalignment indicate poor crystalline ordering; grain boundaries are potential TLS hosts

additional_measurements.source: Table II row 3; Section V.B paragraph 3
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Liftoff method yields statistically indistinguishable loss tangent from wet-etch at same deposition temperature, suggesting fabrication method (wet etch vs liftoff) is not a major loss driver

catchall.free_notes: Cryogenic deposition at -72°C using liquid N2 cold finger. Same deposition rate and thickness as 200°C film. STEM shows blurred contrast at Al-substrate interface indicating reduced heteroepitaxial quality. XRD shows ~85% reduction in <111> peak intensity relative to 200°C film.

surface_oxide_thickness_nm.source: Table II row 4: '2.70 (est.) nm'

additional_measurements.description: Fabrication method: reactive ion etch (RIE) with chlorine chemistry
additional_measurements.value: Cl2/BCl3/Ar ICP-RIE; 4 mTorr, 1.5 sccm Cl2, 3 sccm BCl3, 25 sccm Ar, 70W bias, 300W RF, 25°C, 75s
additional_measurements.source: Table II row 4; Section V.B paragraph 2
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Dry etch increases surface loss tangent by ~70% vs wet etch at same deposition conditions, suggesting chlorine chemistry introduces additional TLS-hosting surface defects or contamination

anomalous_observations.description: Dry etch fabrication produces ~70% higher TLS loss tangent than wet etch at identical deposition conditions and same oxide thickness (~2.70 nm)
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Dry etch (chlorine chemistry) introduces additional surface damage or residues not removable by solvent cleaning, creating additional TLS-hosting defects beyond the native oxide contribution.

catchall.free_notes: Oxide thickness is estimated from air exposure time rather than direct XPS measurement (noted 'est.' in Table II). This is the only dry-etch sample in the comparison. Sidewall is vertical per Fig. S5c.

surface_oxide_thickness_nm.source: Table II row 5: '1.90 ± 0.05 nm'

additional_measurements.description: Surface treatment: H2O2 pre-clean followed by 49% HF for 5 seconds
additional_measurements.value: 5s HF dip; oxide reduced from ~2.69 nm to 1.90 nm; carbon contamination greatly reduced
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: HF treatment removes surface AlOx and photoresist residue, yielding ~40% reduction in surface loss tangent. Oxide thickness reduction of ~0.8 nm is the primary driver of loss improvement.
Oxide regrowth kinetics determine the achievable oxide thickness at cooldown; 2-4 hour exposure yields 1.7-2.1 nm regrown oxide

catchall.free_notes: H2O2 pre-treatment used to saturate and homogenize oxide before HF etch, reducing non-uniformity from micro-masking by hydrocarbon residue. Device cooled down within 2-4 hours of HF treatment to minimize oxide regrowth.

surface_oxide_thickness_nm.source: Table II row 6: '3.11 ± 0.09 nm'

additional_measurements.description: Surface condition: HF-treated device remeasured after 3 months of oxide and hydrocarbon saturation in ambient
additional_measurements.value: Oxide thickness saturated at 3.11 ± 0.09 nm; hydrocarbon signal saturated (XPS confirmed); loss tangent 2.51e-3
additional_measurements.source: Table II row 6; Figure 3b red points; page 6 paragraph 2
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Used as the 90-day reference point to extract intrinsic AlOx loss tangent by differencing from 2-hour HF-treated sample; demonstrates that oxide regrowth kinetics set the long-term performance floor for HF-treated Al devices

correlations_observed.description: Authors use the difference in loss tangent between this sample (90-day saturation) and Row 5 (2-hour exposure) to isolate the intrinsic AlOx loss tangent, attributing the difference entirely to the 1.2 nm oxide thickness change
correlations_observed.nature: Positive correlation used to derive tan_delta_AlOx = (1.74 ± 0.7) × 10^-2

catchall.free_notes: This sample is the same physical device as Row 5 (HF-treated, 200°C, sapphire) remeasured after 3 months in ambient. Oxide and hydrocarbon signals confirmed saturated by XPS. Used as key reference in the loss decomposition model.

surface_oxide_thickness_nm.source: Table II row 7: '1.91 ± 0.05 nm'

additional_measurements.description: HF etch duration: 15 seconds (vs 5s in Row 5)
additional_measurements.value: 15s 49% HF; oxide thickness 1.91 nm (comparable to 5s etch at 1.90 nm)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Longer HF etch time does not significantly further reduce oxide thickness vs 5s, consistent with complete native oxide removal at 5s followed by re-exposure during rinsing

catchall.free_notes: Sapphire substrate, 200°C deposition, wet etch. Oxide thickness nearly identical to 5s HF etch (Row 5), suggesting that 5s is sufficient to fully strip native oxide and additional HF time does not help further due to regrowth during rinse and transfer.

additional_measurements.value: 10:1 DI:HF for 3 min; transferred to loadlock within 10 min

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Thin residual SiOx contributes <5% to total surface loss, confirming SA interface is not a dominant loss channel for Al-on-Si devices

catchall.free_notes: Room-temperature deposition on high-resistivity Si (>20 kOhm·cm, Siegert). Deposition rate 0.4 nm/s (faster than sapphire films). O2 ashing step (500W, 5 min) performed before HF dip to saturate oxide and remove hydrocarbons uniformly. Loss tangent is the lowest single-chip value in the table.

samples.sample_id: Table II Row 9 - RT Si 49% HF 10s (replicate B)

surface_oxide_thickness_nm.source: Table II row 9: '2.00 (est.) nm'

additional_measurements.description: Replicate of Row 8 (same deposition condition and surface treatment); oxide thickness estimated from air exposure time
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Higher loss tangent than Row 8 despite same process conditions; difference likely reflects slightly longer ambient exposure before cooldown leading to thicker regrown oxide

catchall.free_notes: Duplicate of Row 8 chip. Oxide thickness estimated from air exposure time rather than direct XPS (noted 'est.'). The 0.13 nm oxide thickness difference between replicates (1.87 vs 2.00 nm) is consistent with the rapid initial oxidation regime shown in Fig. 4b.

Table II Row 1 - Wet etch.profile_notes: RRR of 32 places this film in clean_limit for aluminum. Coherence tier is competitive based on QTLS,0 ~1e6 (Qi threshold). This is the baseline wet-etch sample against which all surface treatments are compared.

Table II Row 2 - H2O2.profile_notes: H2O2 removes adventitious carbon but produces no statistically significant improvement in loss tangent, isolating photoresist residue as the dominant hydrocarbon TLS contributor rather than adventitious carbon.

Table II Row 3 - Cryo.profile_notes: RRR of 10.41 and small grain size (15.63 nm) with high surface roughness indicate dirty-limit transport. Highest loss tangent (3.60e-3) and Qi likely below 1e6 tier boundary, assigned early_exploration.

Table II Row 4 - Dry etch.profile_notes: Dry etch produces ~70% higher loss tangent (5.43e-3) than wet etch at identical deposition conditions, pushing Qi below the competitive threshold; assigned early_exploration. The elevated loss is attributed to chlorine-chemistry surface damage, not oxide thickness change.

Table II Row 5 - H2O2 49% HF 5s.profile_notes: HF treatment reduces oxide from ~2.69 nm to 1.90 nm and yields lowest loss tangent among sapphire-substrate samples (1.86e-3), demonstrating direct oxide-thickness-to-loss correlation exploited by the authors for loss decomposition.

Table II Row 6 - 49% HF 3 months.profile_notes: This is a remeasurement of Row 5 after 3 months ambient exposure; used as the oxide-saturated reference to extract intrinsic AlOx loss tangent via differencing. The film_thickness_to_loss correlation is explicitly evidenced through the 1.2 nm oxide change mapped to 0.65e-3 loss tangent change.

Table II Row 7 - 49% HF 15s.profile_notes: 15s HF etch yields nearly identical oxide thickness and loss tangent to 5s etch (Row 5), confirming that extended HF exposure does not further reduce oxide due to regrowth during rinse and transfer; best loss tangent among sapphire samples.

Table II Row 8 - RT Si 49% HF 10s (repli.sample_id: Table II Row 8 - RT Si 49% HF 10s (replicate A)
Table II Row 8 - RT Si 49% HF 10s (repli.profile_notes: RRR of 4.82 on Si substrate indicates dirty-limit transport. Lowest overall loss tangent (1.34e-3) in the study despite poor RRR, confirming oxide removal dominates over transport regime for TLS loss. Substrate comparison (Si vs sapphire) is a primary science goal.

Table II Row 9 - RT Si 49% HF 10s (repli.sample_id: Table II Row 9 - RT Si 49% HF 10s (replicate B)
Table II Row 9 - RT Si 49% HF 10s (repli.profile_notes: Replicate of Row 8 with estimated oxide thickness (2.00 nm vs 1.87 nm); slightly higher loss tangent (1.95e-3) consistent with longer ambient exposure before cooldown, reinforcing the oxide-thickness-to-loss correlation on Si substrates.

film_thickness_nm.source: Methods, Device fabrication section, page 10: '200 nm-thick layer of tantalum'

deposition_method.value: not specified for Ta base layer; Al/AlOx/Al junction by double-angle electron-beam evaporation in custom UHV-chamber Plassys

junction_present.source: Methods, Device fabrication section, page 10: 'Josephson junctions are patterned with electron-beam lithography'

T1_us.source: Table S1, SI page 12: per-qubit T1 values at J||/J=-1.0 range from 16.9 to 42.7 µs; value here is approximate mid-range representative; individual qubit values span 16.1-51.5 µs across all coupling conditions

additional_measurements.description: Qubit anharmonicity (α = ω01 - ω12) for each of 8 qubits
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Anharmonicity sets the effective onsite interaction U in the Bose-Hubbard model (U/2π = -186.1 MHz average); determines the transmon operating regime and limits multi-photon leakage, directly affecting gate fidelity and simulation accuracy

additional_measurements.description: Qubit tunable frequency range (bare minimum and maximum) for each qubit
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Tunable frequency range determines the accessible Hamiltonian parameter space and the ability to detune qubits for readout isolation; wider range enables more flexible quantum simulation

additional_measurements.description: Readout resonator linewidth κ/2π for each qubit
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Resonator linewidth sets the measurement rate and Purcell-limited T1; narrower κ reduces Purcell loss but slows readout, creating a design tradeoff that bounds achievable qubit coherence

additional_measurements.description: Dispersive shift χ/2π for each qubit
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Dispersive shift χ determines readout SNR and speed; also contributes to ZZ crosstalk between neighboring qubits which degrades two-qubit gate fidelity and simulation accuracy

additional_measurements.description: Readout resonator frequency ωr/2π for each qubit
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Resonator frequency spacing is engineered for frequency-multiplexed simultaneous readout of all 8 qubits; connection to qubit performance indirect but readout fidelity affects measurement error rates

additional_measurements.description: T2R (Ramsey dephasing time) for each qubit at each coupling ratio condition
additional_measurements.value: Range 0.22-5.69 µs across all qubits and coupling conditions; per-qubit values tabulated for J||/J ∈ {-3.5, -2.0, -1.0, +1.0, +2.0, +3.5}
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: T2R directly limits the coherence time available for adiabatic state preparation (290 ns ramp chosen to balance adiabaticity vs T2); short T2R (~0.22-1 µs for some qubits) is identified as the primary constraint on simulation fidelity

additional_measurements.description: Average onsite interaction strength U/2π from transmon anharmonicity
additional_measurements.source: Main text, Device and Model section, page 3: 'average value U/2π = -186.1 MHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: U sets the Bose-Hubbard interaction strength; attractive U<0 requires sign-flip mapping to simulate repulsive physics; U/J ratio determines proximity to Mott insulator vs superfluid phase boundary

additional_measurements.source: Main text, Device and Model section, page 2: 'average hopping of J/2π = 6.1 MHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Hopping J sets the energy scale and coherence time requirements for the simulation; J/U ratio controls the quantum phase being simulated

additional_measurements.description: Leg hopping strength J||/2π tunable range
additional_measurements.value: Positive: 2.5 to 20.4 MHz; Negative: -17.3 to -7.0 MHz
additional_measurements.source: Main text, Device and Model section, page 2
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Tunable sign and magnitude of J|| implements synthetic magnetic flux (0 or π per plaquette), enabling exploration of chiral superfluid vs Meissner superfluid vs bond-ordered insulator phase diagram

additional_measurements.value: 70-90% for coupler-qubit configurations used in experiments; >90% at maximal qubit-coupler detuning (~3 GHz)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Readout fidelity directly limits measurement accuracy of many-body observables (current correlators, bond energies); lower fidelity introduces systematic errors in extracted order parameters

additional_measurements.description: TLS spectroscopy: frequency-dependent T1 landscape showing TLS-induced relaxation dips
additional_measurements.value: Sharp dips in qubit population (enhanced relaxation) observed at multiple frequencies between 3600-4400 MHz
additional_measurements.source: SI Section S8, Figure S6, page 17
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: TLS defects coupled to qubit modes cause frequency-dependent T1 reductions; TLS density at qubit-substrate and qubit-air interfaces is a primary loss channel limiting coherence in Ta-based transmon devices. Frequencies for ramps and operations chosen to avoid TLS hotspots.

additional_measurements.description: Al junction layer thicknesses and AlOx oxidation conditions
additional_measurements.value: First Al layer: 20 nm; AlOx grown 40 min in pure O2 at 50 mbar; second Al layer: 70 nm
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Al/AlOx/Al Josephson junction parameters determine junction resistance (hence EJ), junction quality, and sub-gap states; oxidation conditions set tunnel barrier properties affecting junction reproducibility and qubit frequency targeting

additional_measurements.value: 290 ns (for 4 unoccupied qubits ramped onto resonance)
additional_measurements.source: SI Section S9, page 18: 'We choose a ramp time of 290 ns'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Ramp time must be slow enough for adiabatic evolution (set by energy gaps ~J, J||) but fast compared to T2R; the 290 ns ramp is a non-negligible fraction of the lowest T2R times (~0.22-1 µs), directly limiting state preparation fidelity

additional_measurements.description: Chiral-current order parameter C measured vs J||/J
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: C distinguishes chiral superfluid (C>>0) from Meissner superfluid (C≈0) phases; demonstrates synthetic flux control capability of the platform. Connection to qubit performance indirect but demonstrates analog simulation fidelity.

additional_measurements.description: Bond order parameter OBO measured vs J||/J
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: OBO characterizes bond-ordered insulator phase; largest at J||/J ≈ -1 where rung bond energies alternate in sign. Connection to qubit performance indirect.

additional_measurements.source: Methods, Wiring and setup section, page 10
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Operating temperature sets quasiparticle density; at 10 mK with Ta (Tc ~4.5 K), thermal quasiparticle population is negligible, so quasiparticle-induced T1 relaxation is not a dominant loss mechanism here

anomalous_observations.description: Numerical simulations consistently overestimate measured signal amplitudes (current correlations and bond kinetic energies) even when dephasing during state preparation is included in the Lindbladian model
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Additional dissipative processes beyond simple dephasing (T2R) are present but not captured in the model. These may include T1 decay during the ramp, residual ZZ interactions, higher transmon levels, or other decoherence channels not included in the Lindblad master equation with pure dephasing collapse operators.

anomalous_observations.description: Qubit T1 lifetimes depend sensitively on qubit frequency, with sharp dips at specific frequencies attributed to strongly-coupled two-level systems (TLSs)
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Lossy TLSs in the device environment (likely at interfaces or in amorphous oxides) hybridize with the qubit mode at their resonant frequencies, causing enhanced relaxation. This is a known phenomenon in superconducting qubits and motivates frequency selection to avoid TLS hotspots.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Transmon anharmonicity α = ω01 - ω12 in MHz, setting the effective onsite Bose-Hubbard interaction U
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: In transmon-based quantum simulators, anharmonicity directly maps to the Bose-Hubbard U parameter (U ≈ -α); tracking this across devices allows systematic comparison of interaction-to-hopping ratios and operating regimes. It also sets the leakage rate to higher transmon levels, which is a key limit on simulation fidelity.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table S1, SI page 12; Hamiltonian Eq. (1) main text page 3

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Ramsey dephasing time T2R in µs, measured per qubit at the lattice operating frequency
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: T2R sets the maximum coherent evolution time available for state preparation and measurement in analog quantum simulation. It is the binding constraint on adiabatic ramp fidelity and distinguishes dephasing-limited from relaxation-limited operation. The paper explicitly identifies T2R as the key constraint on ramp time selection.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table S1, SI page 12; SI Section S9 page 18

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Whether inter-qubit couplings are implemented via direct capacitive coupling or flux-tunable transmon couplers, and the coupling sign tunability
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Tunable couplers that can access both positive and negative effective coupling (by tuning coupler frequency above or below qubit frequency) are essential for implementing synthetic gauge fields in quantum simulators. This architecture parameter determines the accessible phase diagram and is distinct from fixed-coupling designs.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text Device and Model section page 2; SI Section S4 page 14

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Single-shot readout assignment fidelity (%) for distinguishing |0> and |1> states
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Readout fidelity directly limits the accuracy of extracted many-body observables (correlation functions, order parameters) in quantum simulation experiments. The 70-90% range reported here introduces systematic measurement errors that reduce effective simulation fidelity beyond what T1/T2 alone would predict.

catchall.free_notes: This is a quantum simulation device, not a materials characterization or qubit benchmarking paper. The primary scientific outputs are many-body phase signatures (chiral superfluid, Meissner superfluid, bond-ordered insulator) rather than qubit coherence metrics. The Ta base layer fabrication follows the process from Ref. [51] (Bahrami et al., Phys. Rev. B 113, 054505 (2026)) which studied vortex motion induced losses in tantalum resonators; the junction process follows Ref. [53] (Bland et al., Nature 647, 343 (2025)) which reported millisecond lifetimes. The device uses 8 flux-tunable transmon qubits plus 6 flux-tunable transmon couplers. Aluminum airbridges are used to connect ground planes across coplanar waveguides and flux lines. The device is mounted in a Scalinq LINQER package. Operating frequencies are 3.85 GHz or 4.30 GHz depending on J||/J ratio. Josephson junctions patterned using Dolan bridge technique (Ref. [52]). T1 values in Table S1 range widely across qubits (9.4-51.5 µs) and coupling conditions; the single T1_us field in the schema cannot capture this per-qubit, per-condition variation adequately. The paper reports T1 for all 8 qubits at 6 different J||/J operating points, totaling 48 distinct T1 measurements.

T1_measurement_context.source: Backfilled from similarity_profile device_type='multi_qubit_device' by backfill_t1_context.py

8-qubit triangular ladder device.profile_notes: Transport regime is unknown as no RRR or mean-free-path data is reported; deposition method for the Ta base layer is not specified. TLS loss is explicitly identified via frequency-dependent T1 dips attributed to interface/oxide TLS defects. T1 values span 9.4–51.5 µs placing the device in the competitive tier; the primary science focus is analog quantum simulation (device_demonstration) rather than materials or coherence optimization.

additional_measurements.source: Abstract sentence 4; Section III; Table I (experimental value listed as 388 MHz, but text states 392 ± 15 MHz as the primary result)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: The hyperfine coupling between the donor electron spin and 119Sn nuclear spin directly sets the electron–nuclear spin gate speed, which governs multi-qubit register operation and nuclear spin–photon interface bandwidth. A large hyperfine interaction (392 MHz) enables fast electron–nuclear gates and potentially direct nuclear-spin–photon entanglement when the hyperfine splitting exceeds the optical linewidth.

anomalous_observations.description: Resonant excitation of I10 enhances transitions near Ga and Al two-electron satellite (TES) replicas, despite these donors having larger transition energies than I10
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The mechanism for this enhancement is unclear and beyond the scope of the present work. Authors note the TES transitions are significantly sharper than any previously reported in the literature.

anomalous_observations.description: Nuclear spin polarization PN is unequal between the two CPT dips and depends on relative pump/probe optical intensity, indicating optically driven nuclear spin polarization
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Competition between Overhauser effect (dominates off two-photon resonance) and CPT dark-state pumping (dominates at two-photon resonance) naturally explains the power-dependent polarization trends.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Isotropic hyperfine coupling constant between donor electron spin and nuclear spin, in MHz
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: The hyperfine interaction directly sets the speed of electron–nuclear spin gates and determines whether direct nuclear-spin–photon entanglement is achievable (requires hyperfine splitting > optical linewidth). Tracking this across donor species and hosts would allow systematic comparison of spin qubit register performance across platforms.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Abstract sentence 4; Section III; Table I

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Donor binding energy in meV, which sets the localization energy of the donor-bound exciton and determines thermal robustness of the optical transition
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Larger donor binding energy pushes the optical transition further from the band edge, enabling operation at elevated temperatures without thermal broadening. Systematically tracking this across donor species would allow identification of thermally robust spin-photon interfaces.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Section I, paragraph 2; Section II; Figure 1

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Energy separation between main donor-bound exciton transition (D0X) and excited state (D0X*), in meV
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This separation determines the thermal robustness of the optical linewidth: larger separation reduces phonon-mediated temperature broadening of the main transition, directly affecting optical spin-photon interface fidelity at elevated temperatures.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Optically induced nuclear spin polarization PN = (P_up - P_down)/(P_up + P_down), dimensionless
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Nuclear spin initialization fidelity via optical pumping is a prerequisite for nuclear spin qubit operation. Tracking this across systems enables comparison of initialization protocols and identification of the most efficient spin-photon platforms.

catchall.free_notes: Sample A: 119Sn (100% spin-1/2 isotope) implanted at 380 keV, fluence 1e13 ions/cm2, into 1.7 µm MBE ZnO epilayer on hydrothermal ZnO (0001) substrate (Tokyo Denpa). Post-implantation anneal: 800°C, 1 hour, oxygen atmosphere. SIMS confirms Li diffusion from hydrothermal substrate into epilayer during annealing, co-localizing with implanted Sn profile and enabling Sn-Li complex formation without explicit Li implantation. MBE growth conditions: substrate 725°C, 2 hours, Zn flux 5e-4 Pa, ozone oxidant, base pressure <3e-8 Pa. Sample B (separate sample, not detailed above): 119Sn at 380 keV, 1e12 ions/cm2, into 1.5 µm MBE epilayer; annealed 780°C 2 hours O2; then 7Li implanted through grid mask at 120 keV, 1e11 ions/cm2; annealed 600°C 20 min O2. Li-implanted regions of Sample B show clear enhancement of I10 emission vs Sn-only regions, confirming Li involvement in the complex. This paper is primarily a quantum defect/spin-photon interface study in ZnO; relevance to superconducting qubit research is indirect (donor spin qubit, not superconducting qubit). Included due to potential cross-platform relevance for hybrid quantum systems.

Sample A.profile_notes: This sample is a ZnO:Sn donor spin-photon interface study with no superconducting material, no microwave device, and no qubit coherence metrics — it falls entirely outside the superconducting qubit materials domain. All vocabulary assignments reflect the closest applicable terms given the mismatch, with 'other' for material class and 'film_only'/'not_applicable' reflecting the absence of any patterned microwave or qubit device.

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Elevated mode temperature (above quantum ground state) from optical pump absorption contributes added noise to qubit readout, limiting readout SNR and potentially causing qubit dephasing

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Pulsed operation mitigates heating and backaction on the qubit; duty cycle determines average heat load deposited at mixing chamber

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Must match the NbN microwave resonance frequency for three-mode resonance condition enabling efficient electro-optic transduction; initial mismatch of ~300 MHz required dc electrostatic tuning

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Required to bring optical splitting into resonance with microwave frequency; large bias voltages may impact reliability and integration with superconducting circuits

schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This is the primary figure of merit for microwave-optical transducers interfacing superconducting qubits to fiber networks; tracking it across samples and platforms would enable systematic comparison of loss mechanisms and progress toward quantum-state transduction

catchall.free_notes: The M2O device is a hybrid structure: 1 µm AlN optical waveguide ring resonator on sapphire, with 50 nm NbN superconducting microwave resonator deposited above it via ALD and patterned by photolithography. The AlN is etched in two steps (400 nm + 600 nm) using Cl2/BCl3/Ar ICP, with SiO2 hard masks. A 2.5 µm SiO2 cladding is deposited by PECVD and annealed at 1000°C for 2 hours before NbN deposition. Fiber array coupling with 5 dB insertion loss per facet. Device operated at 50 mK MXC stage during M2O characterization. The NbN microwave resonator uses a loop geometry enabling flux tunability via kinetic inductance modulation. The AlN electro-optic coefficient used in modeling is r33 = 1 pm/V.

additional_measurements.source: Page 2 prose: 'free spectral range (FSR) of 76 MHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: FSR determines the minimum tuning required for frequency matching between qubit readout resonators and the M2O transducer; smaller FSR would improve frequency coverage and reduce tuning requirements for heterogeneous qubit arrays

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Signal mode must match target qubit readout frequency; sets one end of the frequency-conversion chain

additional_measurements.description: M2M signal mode intrinsic loss rate (κs,in)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Internal loss rate of the NbN M2M resonator; very low internal loss (high Q) enables near-unity conversion efficiency and low added noise

additional_measurements.description: M2M signal mode external coupling rate (κs,ex)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Near-critical coupling to feedline maximizes conversion efficiency; relevant to M2M-M2O cascaded system efficiency

additional_measurements.description: M2M idler mode frequency (aligned to qubit readout resonator)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Idler mode is tuned to match qubit readout resonator frequency; demonstrates 1.7 GHz frequency translation capability

additional_measurements.description: M2M idler mode intrinsic loss rate (κi,in)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Internal loss of idler mode limits achievable M2M conversion efficiency; relevant to cascaded system SNR

additional_measurements.description: M2M idler mode external coupling rate (κi,ex)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Over-coupled idler mode (κi,ex > κi,in) optimizes power extraction to feedline for routing to M2O stage

additional_measurements.description: M2M frequency conversion efficiency (ηMM) across operating modes
additional_measurements.value: >50% for all modes; up to 84% at 7.054 GHz signal
additional_measurements.source: Figure 3(d) panels ii-vi; prose page 5: 'ηMM > 50% for all operating modes'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: High M2M conversion efficiency is essential to minimize signal loss before the M2O stage; directly sets the cascaded system efficiency floor

additional_measurements.description: M2M amplification gain (phase-sensitive, combined conversion+amplification mode)
additional_measurements.value: 30 dB with signal-to-idler internal conversion efficiency of 67%
additional_measurements.source: Page 5 prose: 'M2M stage operates as a phase-sensitive amplifier, providing 30 dB of gain with a signal-to-idler internal conversion efficiency of 67%'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: M2M amplification compensates for low M2O conversion efficiency, enabling practical qubit state readout SNR despite 1.3×10^-4 transduction efficiency

additional_measurements.description: M2M added noise upper bound (referred to M2M output)
additional_measurements.source: Supplementary Section V page 15: 'we obtain an upper bound on the added noise of nadd < 1.3 quanta'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Added noise of M2M amplifier directly impacts qubit readout fidelity and measurement backaction; near-quantum-limited performance required for high-fidelity qubit state discrimination

additional_measurements.description: M2M tuning range as fraction of center frequency
additional_measurements.source: Page 6 prose: 'M2M tuning range is limited to 0.83%, corresponding to a 78% frequency coverage around the 7.339 GHz target frequency'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Limited tuning range means not all qubit readout frequencies within the FSR can be addressed; improving coverage requires longer resonator (smaller FSR)

additional_measurements.description: M2M device total physical length (meander line)
additional_measurements.source: Page 2 prose: 'a compact meander line with a total physical length of 80 mm'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Physical length sets the FSR of the multimode resonator; longer resonator would reduce FSR and improve frequency coverage

additional_measurements.description: M2M mode temperature under operating conditions
additional_measurements.source: Page 6 prose: 'M2M mode temperature is independently calibrated to be below 1.3 quanta'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Low mode temperature confirms M2M device remains near ground state during operation, minimizing added thermal noise contribution to qubit readout

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Sheet inductance in pH/square, characterizing the kinetic inductance of thin NbN films deposited by ALD
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Sheet inductance of 105 pH/sq for 5 nm ALD-NbN determines nonlinearity strength and flux-tuning range for kinetic inductance devices; tracking this across film thicknesses and deposition conditions would enable optimization of M2M converters and parametric amplifiers for qubit interfaces

catchall.free_notes: M2M device fabricated on 500 µm high-resistivity silicon. 5 nm NbN deposited by ALD. Single e-beam lithography step using CSAR resist, CF4 plasma etch. Mounted on cold plate (not MXC) of dilution refrigerator to confine pump-induced heating to higher temperature stages. Loop structures incorporate two nanowires with intentionally asymmetric widths to break symmetry and enable three-wave mixing when flux biased. M2M device operated in two modes: (1) pure frequency conversion with pump at signal-idler difference frequency; (2) combined conversion + phase-sensitive amplification with additional pump at 2×idler frequency. M2M chip mounted in oxygen-free copper package, wire-bonded to PCB.

junction_present.source: Paper describes a transmon qubit; acknowledgments page 10: 'Rigetti Quantum Foundry Services team for design support and device fabrication of the transmon chip'

T1_us.source: Page 6 prose: 'Exponential fits yield T1^µw = 30.9 ± 0.3 µs for microwave readout'

T2_echo_us.source: Page 6 prose: 'Ramsey measurements give T2^µw = 3.1 ± 0.1 µs' — note this is T2* (Ramsey), not T2 echo; assigned medium confidence due to ambiguity between Ramsey and echo

additional_measurements.description: Qubit T1 measured via optical readout (M2M-M2O cascaded chain)
additional_measurements.source: Page 6 prose: 'T1^opt = 28.7 ± 3.1 µs for optical readout'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Agreement with microwave T1 (30.9 µs) validates optical readout as a non-destructive probe; larger error bars indicate reduced SNR due to limited M2O conversion efficiency

additional_measurements.description: Qubit T2* (Ramsey) measured via optical readout
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Slight degradation vs microwave T2* (3.1 µs) attributed to thermal noise from elevated MXC operating temperature and limited M2O efficiency; validates cascaded link for dephasing measurements

additional_measurements.source: Table S1, Supplementary Material page 11; also page 5 prose

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Readout resonator linewidth must be within the M2M signal mode bandwidth for efficient coupling; narrow linewidth relative to M2M mode linewidth (177 kHz total) places demands on frequency alignment precision
Qubit frequency determines gate drive requirements and anharmonicity; 3.4 GHz is in lower range of typical transmon frequencies

additional_measurements.description: Frequency detuning of qubit readout resonator from native M2O microwave resonance
additional_measurements.source: Abstract and page 5 prose: 'readout resonator is detuned by 1.7 GHz from the microwave resonance (5.669 GHz) of the standalone M2O device'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Demonstrates the problem the M2M stage solves: fixed-frequency M2O transducers cannot directly interface with qubits detuned by GHz-scale offsets; validates frequency-agile architecture

anomalous_observations.description: T2* is anomalously low (3.1 µs) relative to T1 (30.9 µs); T2* << 2*T1 suggests significant pure dephasing
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: 'The relatively low T2 was limited by the thermal noise in the readout resonator caused by the elevated operating temperature of the MXC' (page 6, citing refs 46, 47). MXC was heated above base temperature to provide increased cooling power during optical pump operation.

catchall.free_notes: Transmon qubit designed by D.L. Campbell and M.D. LaHaye (Air Force Research Laboratory); fabricated by Rigetti Quantum Foundry Services. Qubit operated at 50 mK MXC stage. MXC was heated during M2O operation to provide increased cooling power, which elevated thermal noise in the readout resonator and degraded T2*. Optical readout used M2M stage in combined conversion+amplification mode (30 dB gain) to compensate for low M2O efficiency (1.3×10^-4). Rabi oscillations, T1, and T2* measurements all show good agreement between optical and microwave readout within uncertainties, validating the cascaded M2M-M2O readout architecture.

M2O transducer (NbN microwave resonator.sample_id: M2O transducer (NbN microwave resonator on AlN/sapphire)
M2O transducer (NbN microwave resonator.profile_notes: NbN thin films via ALD are inherently disordered and fall in the dirty limit by convention; coherence_tier is early_exploration based on the resonator intrinsic loss rate (κm,in = 2π×31.9 MHz, implying Qi on the order of ~180, far below 1e5). The science focus is device demonstration of the M2O transducer architecture rather than loss mechanism identification.

M2M converter (NbN meander resonator on.sample_id: M2M converter (NbN meander resonator on silicon)
M2M converter (NbN meander resonator on.profile_notes: The M2M signal mode intrinsic loss rate of 2π×75 kHz at ~5.637 GHz corresponds to an intrinsic Qi of ~75,000, placing it in the competitive tier (1e5 borderline); the very thin 5 nm ALD-NbN film is strongly disordered (dirty_limit). TLS_substrate is relevant given the high-resistivity silicon substrate and measured low internal loss.

Superconducting transmon qubit (Rigetti.profile_notes: Material class assigned as aluminum (standard Rigetti transmon junction/wiring process) since no specific film material beyond junction identity is stated; transport_regime is unknown as no RRR or film characterization data are provided. T1 of 30.9 µs places this in the competitive tier; anomalously low T2* is attributed by authors to thermal noise from elevated MXC temperature, not an intrinsic material loss mechanism.

anomalous_observations.description: Ic drops abruptly at ~0.55-0.6 K, well below the bulk PtSi Tc of ~0.9 K, and above this temperature Ic no longer correlates with constriction width
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The constriction region has a weakened superconducting order parameter, causing it to return to normal at lower Tc and Hc than the bulk leads, with supercurrent transported via proximity effect

anomalous_observations.description: Ic drops to ~10% of zero-field value at 15 mT (0.4 K), despite bulk PtSi critical field of 45 mT at the same temperature
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Weakened order parameter in the constriction region suppresses both Tc and Hc relative to bulk values; proximity-effect transport in the constriction

anomalous_observations.description: Extracted Ginzburg-Landau coherence length ξGL(0) = 45 nm from SQUID interferometry, shorter than 76 nm measured from critical field of bulk PtSi thin films
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Shorter mean free path within the constriction region shortens ξGL(0) relative to the bulk film value

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Width of the superconductor-constriction-superconductor junction constriction in nanometers
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: The ratio of constriction width to coherence length is the primary determinant of CPR nonlinearity (and hence qubit anharmonicity) in constriction-type Josephson junctions — tracking this across samples enables systematic comparison of junction nonlinearity

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Length of the superconductor-constriction-superconductor junction constriction in nanometers
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Along with width, the constriction length relative to coherence length governs the CPR shape and nonlinearity coefficient a1, which directly sets qubit anharmonicity

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Ginzburg-Landau coherence length ξGL(0) at 0 K in nanometers
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: ξGL is the key material parameter controlling constriction junction CPR nonlinearity; it determines how skewed vs sinusoidal the CPR is and sets the anharmonicity of constriction-based qubits — systematic tracking across samples and fabrication conditions would enable optimization of junction design
schema_promotion_candidates.source: page 4 paragraph 4, ξGL(0) = 45 nm determined from SQUID interferometry fitting

schema_promotion_candidates.description: First nonlinear coefficient a1 = IJ'''(0)/IJ'(0) of the Maclaurin expansion of the current-phase relation; equals -1 for sinusoidal CPR
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: a1 directly quantifies the anharmonicity available from the junction, which sets the qubit frequency-dependent nonlinearity and determines suitability as a qubit oscillator element; a1 = -0.2 for the constriction vs -0.02 for the full SQUID arm shows that lead inductance severely degrades the nonlinearity available for qubit operation

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Total linear kinetic inductance Lk of the SQUID arm including constricted and non-constricted regions, in nanohenries
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Kinetic inductance of leads moderates the effective nonlinearity seen by the circuit; Lk ~ 0.12 nH found to be independent of constriction width, showing it dominates the arm inductance and dilutes qubit anharmonicity — critical parameter for circuit design with high-kinetic-inductance materials like PtSi
schema_promotion_candidates.source: page 4 paragraph 5, Lk = 0.12 nH at 0.4 K for all devices

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Oscillation depth δ = (Ic,max - Ic,min)/Ic,max of the SQUID interference pattern as a percentage
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: δ directly encodes the deviation of the CPR from sinusoidal; used here to extract coherence length via GL simulations; incomplete modulation (δ < 100%) signals nonsinusoidal CPR and nonnegligible kinetic inductance — both critical for qubit circuit design

catchall.free_notes: ScS-25 is a single constriction junction device (not a SQUID) with nominal 25 nm width × 100 nm length. Actual dimensions extend ~10 nm isotropically beyond design. Film thickness 25 nm, far below London penetration depth (~190 nm), so film is fully penetrated by magnetic field. Each Ic value averaged over 50 individual V-I sweeps due to stochastic switching fluctuations. Measurements conducted between 0.4 and 1.0 K using four-probe current-bias configuration. PtSi is described as a silicon-compatible platform. Authors note critical currents are ~2 orders of magnitude too large for transmon qubits at GHz frequencies; narrower constrictions or higher-frequency qubit designs (millimeter-wave) would be needed.
