additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Establishes the quantitative relationship between coherence length and CPR nonlinearity for PtSi constrictions — enables prediction of anharmonicity for different device geometries

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that modulation depth δ increases with coherence length ξ in Ginzburg-Landau simulations of SSQ-50
correlations_observed.nature: positive correlation: δ increases from 25% to 40% as ξ increases from 60 to 140 nm

correlations_observed.description: Authors observe stronger Ic modulation in narrower constrictions
correlations_observed.nature: negative correlation: narrower constrictions show larger δ (e.g. SSQ-25 δ=31% vs SSQ-50 δ=25% at 0.4 K)

correlations_observed.description: Authors state δ increases with temperature T in both SSQ and ASQ devices
correlations_observed.nature: positive correlation, with abrupt increase above ~0.6 K; consistent with increasing coherence length at higher T

catchall.free_notes: SSQ-50 is the primary SQUID device studied in detail. Symmetric dc-SQUID with two 50 nm nominal (actual ~70 nm × ~80 nm) constriction junctions. Rectangular loop ~1 µm² area. Vorticity diamond model analysis used to extract Lk independently of constriction width. GL simulations performed in 2D given film thickness << λL. Key finding: constriction nonlinearity (a1=-0.2) substantially degraded at device level (a1=-0.02) by lead kinetic inductance.

additional_measurements.description: SQUID Ic modulation depth δ for asymmetric SQUID with 50 nm and 100 nm nominal constrictions (from Figure 3c)
additional_measurements.value: smaller than SSQ-50 δ of 25%, increases with T
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Asymmetric SQUID geometry allows CPR characterization of individual junctions with different widths; modulation depth encodes relative CPR nonlinearity

additional_measurements.description: Phase offset between positive and negative Ic branches
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Phase offset in asymmetric SQUID arises from different CPRs and kinetic inductances of the two arms; confirms non-sinusoidal CPR in PtSi constrictions relevant to qubit junction design

catchall.free_notes: ASQ-50/100 is an asymmetric dc-SQUID with one 50 nm and one 100 nm nominal width constriction junction. The wider 100 nm constriction carries more supercurrent, making δ smaller than SSQ-50. The ~π phase offset between positive and negative bias Ic branches is a signature of the asymmetric CPRs and kinetic inductances of the two arms. Simulation reproduces the phase offset with dependence on ξ.

ScS-25.profile_notes: No microwave device measured and no coherence data reported; device is a transport-characterized constriction junction only. Growth method for PtSi silicide films is not specified in the record, so 'other' is used.

ScS-50.profile_notes: Single constriction junction with DC transport measurements only; no microwave or coherence measurements. Classified junction_only and not_applicable for coherence_tier.

ScS-100.profile_notes: Single constriction junction with DC transport measurements only; no microwave or coherence data reported.

SSQ-25.profile_notes: Symmetric dc-SQUID used for CPR characterization via interferometry; no microwave device or coherence measurements, so coherence_tier is not_applicable and device_type is junction_only.

SSQ-50.profile_notes: Primary SQUID device used to extract GL coherence length and CPR nonlinearity; no qubit or resonator fabricated. The crystal_phase_to_loss correlation is included because the paper explicitly links reduced mean free path in the constriction to modified coherence length affecting junction nonlinearity.

ASQ-50/100.profile_notes: Asymmetric SQUID used to probe CPR asymmetry between different constriction widths via DC interferometry; no microwave or coherence measurements reported.

Table 2 Row 3.profile_notes: Qi ~1×10^6 at lowest drive power meets state_of_the_art threshold. Vortex motion identified as additional loss channel from Tact < Tc discrepancy; surface/interface loss tangent and quasiparticle losses also explicitly modeled.

Table 2 Row 4.profile_notes: RRR of 1.75 and residual resistivity of 28.85 µΩ·cm confirm dirty-limit regime. No microwave device fabricated; structural degradation noted at 850°C.

Table 2 Row 5.profile_notes: RRR of 1.67 and residual resistivity of 32.55 µΩ·cm indicate dirty-limit transport. 2 ks film at 550°C; no resonator device fabricated from this sample.

Table 2 Row 6.profile_notes: RRR of 1.59 and residual resistivity of 23.64 µΩ·cm confirm dirty-limit regime. No microwave device fabricated from this sample.

Table 2 Row 7.profile_notes: Qi up to 4×10^7 is firmly state_of_the_art. Tact consistent with DC Tc (unlike 1 ks film) confirms suppressed vortex motion due to higher lattice mismatch and smaller grains; surface loss tangent and QP losses also explicitly characterized.

Table 2 Row 8.profile_notes: RRR of 2.06 and residual resistivity of 20.89 µΩ·cm confirm dirty-limit regime. No microwave device fabricated from this sample.

catchall.free_notes: D1 is a Type A (clean limit) film. Substrate is c-axis HEMEX sapphire from Crystal Systems, piranha-cleaned before deposition. Deposition uses UHV DC magnetron sputtering (AJA Orion 8), base pressure 2e-9 to 2e-8 Torr, 3 mTorr argon during deposition. Deposition temperature ranged 500-850 C; slower deposition rates yield clean-limit films (D1-D5). STEM shows ~3 nm amorphous oxide, single-crystal 111 bcc structure, Ta atoms directly bonded to Al2O3 at interface. Scanning SQUID on D1 confirms homogeneous Tc ~4.3 K with <0.01 K variation at micrometer scale, ruling out beta-Ta inclusions. XAS at Ta L3 edge confirms metallic Ta0 with alpha-Ta EXAFS features.

anomalous_observations.description: D8 grows with <110> out-of-plane orientation rather than the typical <111> orientation seen in all other devices, despite being on the same c-axis sapphire substrate
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The columnar grain structure arising from 110 growth orientation provides a high density of grain boundary defects that serve as vortex pinning sites, consistent with its dirty-limit classification and lowest RRR among all devices. Growth orientation correlated with deposition rate per phase diagram (Fig. 6).

catchall.free_notes: D8 is the most extreme Type B (dirty limit) device, with the lowest RRR (6.78), smallest coherence length (28.4 nm), largest Hc2 (0.407 T), and unique 110 out-of-plane crystal orientation. The 110 orientation produces columnar grains visible in SEM (Fig. 13b). Used as comparison device for microstructured type A film (Fig. 5b). Lattice mismatch not calculated for D8 in Table II as different crystal planes are relevant for 110 orientation.

D1.profile_notes: D1 is a clean-limit alpha-Ta film (l > xi, RRR ~39.5); vortex_motion is the identified dominant loss channel. TLS_interface is included based on surface oxide (~3 nm) characterization and participation ratio measurements mentioned in the paper.

D2.profile_notes: D2 has the highest RRR (65.1) and longest mean free path (142.3 nm) of all devices, firmly placing it in the clean limit; vortex motion is the primary identified loss mechanism.

D3.profile_notes: D3 was used both as an unmodified clean-limit film and as the microstructured (pinning hole) device; the microstructuring experiment directly confirmed vortex motion as the sole anomalous loss channel, and TLS participation ratio measurements confirmed holes do not increase TLS loss.

D4.profile_notes: D4 is a clean-limit alpha-Ta film (RRR 62.2, l=125 nm >> xi=44.5 nm) with the highest viscous drag coefficient among type A films, making vortex motion the identified dominant loss channel.

D5.profile_notes: D5 has the largest coherence length (57.4 nm) and smallest Hc2 among all devices, representing the most extreme clean-limit character; used as representative type A film for E-J and field-dependent Qi measurements confirming vortex-motion loss.

D6.profile_notes: D6 is the first dirty-limit film (xi=40.6 nm > l=20.4 nm, RRR=11.5); the high Tact (1.69 K) indicates vortex pinning suppresses vortex-motion loss relative to type A films, though vortex motion remains the investigated loss channel in the paper's framework.

D7.profile_notes: D7 is the representative dirty-limit film (xi=36.6 nm > l=12.9 nm, RRR=10.8) with Tact near Tc (4.16 K) indicating effective vortex pinning; higher surface roughness compared to type A films suggests potential TLS contribution at interfaces.

D8.profile_notes: D8 is unique as the only <110>-oriented film, with the lowest RRR (6.78), smallest coherence length (28.4 nm), and highest surface roughness (RMS 2.29 nm); the distinct crystal orientation and columnar grain structure are explicitly linked to dirty-limit behavior and superior vortex pinning, warranting crystal_phase_to_loss correlation.

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Crystallographic out-of-plane texture of the deposited film (e.g., (110), (111), polycrystalline), distinct from crystal phase
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: Nb film orientation on sapphire transitions from (110) to (111) with increasing deposition temperature, and this correlates with RRR and grain boundary density, which directly affect TLS loss and microwave dissipation. Tracking orientation separately from phase would allow systematic comparison of texture-dependent loss across deposition conditions.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: On c-plane sapphire, films grown at low temperatures (<700 K) typically adopt a (110) out-of-plane texture, whereas higher growth temperatures (>750 K) favor (111) orientation... Correspondingly, the RRR of Nb films strongly correlates with the growth temperature.

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Chemical species present in the native surface oxide layer (e.g., Nb2O5, NbO2, NbO, NbOx suboxide fractions), typically measured by XPS or SIMS depth profiling
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: The relative fractions of Nb2O5 versus suboxides (NbO2, NbO) determine the density and type of TLS defects at the metal-air interface. Disordered suboxides host oxygen vacancies and dangling bonds that are the primary source of dielectric loss in Nb qubits. Tracking oxide composition (not just thickness) would allow loss mechanism attribution across surface treatments.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: At a microscopic level, microwave dissipation in Nb thin films arises from the complex hierarchy of surface native oxide (Nb2O5) and suboxides (e.g., NbO2, NbO) that form upon air exposure... These oxides are structurally disordered and host oxygen vacancies and dangling bonds that give rise to electric-dipole TLS.

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Material used to cap or encapsulate the superconducting film surface (e.g., Ta, Au, Al, TiN, Ru, Si), including thickness in nm
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: Surface encapsulation is emerging as the primary practical strategy for suppressing native oxide TLS loss in Nb qubits. Different capping materials (Ta, Au, TiN, Ru) yield dramatically different T1 values (40–300+ µs on the same substrate). A structured field for encapsulation material and thickness would enable direct cross-study comparison of this rapidly growing body of work.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: various in situ metal capping layers Ta, Al, TiN, and Au were systematically investigated for their ability to suppress native oxide formation at the Nb–air interface... Ta-capped Nb qubits that were fabricated on Si substrate demonstrated marked improvements in coherence, with energy relaxation times (T1) exceeding 300 µs, significantly outperforming other capping strategies.

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Base pressure of the deposition chamber before film growth, in Torr
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: Nb has strong affinity for oxygen; even trace oxygen at the growth front incorporates interstitially or forms suboxides that degrade RRR and increase TLS density. Base pressure is the primary indicator of oxygen exposure during growth and directly controls film purity. Table 1 of this review already tracks this parameter across studies, confirming its importance for cross-lab comparison.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: Even trace oxygen contamination in the growth environment leads to interstitial incorporation of oxygen or formation of Nb oxides, both of which degrade superconducting properties by disrupting crystallinity and creating oxygen-rich disordered regions.

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Working gas pressure during sputtering or other PVD deposition, in Torr
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: Working pressure affects mean free path of sputtered species, film density, grain size, and columnar vs. equiaxed growth morphology — all of which influence surface roughness, grain boundary density, and ultimately TLS participation. Table 1 of this review includes this parameter, and it differs systematically between HiPIMS and DC sputtering studies.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: Table 1: Selected studies on the Nb film for superconducting qubits applications. [columns include P_Base (mTorr) and P_deposition (mTorr)]

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Mean lateral grain size of the polycrystalline film as measured by TEM or AFM, in nm
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: Grain boundaries in Nb films are oxygen diffusion pathways that create suboxide-rich disordered regions, directly increasing TLS density. HiPIMS films have mean grain size ~44 nm versus ~65 nm for DC sputtered films, and this correlates with reduced RRR and lower resonator Q. Tracking grain size would allow quantitative comparison of microstructure-loss relationships across deposition methods.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: Nb films grown by HiPIMS often display smaller grains and increased roughness compared to conventional sputtering (Figure 5), which correlates with reduced RRR and lower resonator quality factors... grain size distributions of 44.0±13.8 nm [HiPIMS] and 65.3±21.6 nm [DC magnetron].

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Boolean indicating whether an amorphous or crystalline NbSix interfacial layer was detected at the Nb-Si interface, with method of detection noted
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: NbSix interfacial layers at Nb-on-Si devices increase electric-field participation at the metal-substrate interface and degrade coherence. The presence or absence of silicide is highly process-dependent (controlled vs. uncontrolled thermal budget) and is a critical quality indicator for Si-substrate devices. Structured tracking would allow identification of fabrication conditions that suppress silicide formation.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: Interfacial silicide formation in Nb-on-Si devices introduces amorphous or polycrystalline NbSix layers that increase electric-field participation at the metal-substrate interface and degrade coherence.

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Boolean indicating whether NbHx hydride precipitates were detected in the film (typically by SIMS showing NbH- and H- signals), with processing step that introduced hydrogen noted
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: NbHx precipitates suppress the superconducting gap locally and reduce qubit performance. Hydrogen is introduced during wet processing (BOE) or plasma exposure and is not tracked by standard transport measurements. SIMS is required for detection. Given that multiple groups have independently discovered this issue, it warrants a structured field to track its occurrence and severity across fabrication protocols.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: hydrogen incorporation and hydride precipitation, arising from wet processing or plasma exposure, produce nanoscale NbHx regions that locally suppress the superconducting gap and further reduce qubit performance.

schema_evolution_proposals.description: Post-deposition surface treatment method applied to reduce TLS density (e.g., BOE etch, nitrogen plasma passivation, ANAB, CMP), distinct from encapsulation
schema_evolution_proposals.why_important: Surface passivation treatments (BOE, N2 plasma, ANAB, CMP) each modify the oxide hierarchy and hydrogen content differently, with measurable impacts on Qi and T1. A structured field would enable systematic comparison of passivation strategies across labs and allow correlation with oxide composition measurements.
schema_evolution_proposals.supporting_text: researchers have explored a range of passivation and encapsulation techniques. Buffered oxide etch (BOE) is commonly used to remove the native oxide layer prior to fabrication steps. Nitrogen plasma treatments offer oxide reduction and nitrogen-based passivation... Chemical mechanical planarization (CMP) and accelerated neutral atom beam (ANAB) processing have been shown to yield smoother surfaces and more stable suboxide layers with reduced hydrogen incorporation.

primary_paper_queue.citation: A. Premkumar, C. Weiland, S. Hwang, et al., 'Microscopic relaxation channels in materials for superconducting qubits,' Communications Materials 2 (2021): 72
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Key primary study combining transmon qubit T1 measurements with XPS depth profiling and STEM/EELS to show oxygen diffusion along grain boundaries and Nb sub-oxides directly correlate with TLS losses; reports RRR values for DC sputtered vs HiPIMS Nb films on sapphire with Tc data

primary_paper_queue.citation: J.-S. Oh, C. J. Kopas, J. Marshall, et al., 'Exploring the relationship Between deposition method, microstructure, and performance of Nb/Si-based superconducting coplanar waveguide resonators,' Acta Materialia 276 (2024): 120153
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Systematic comparison of HiPIMS vs DC magnetron sputtered Nb on Si; reports grain size distributions (44±14 nm HiPIMS vs 65±22 nm DC), cross-sectional TEM of NbSix interfacial layers, and resonator quality factors; directly links microstructure to microwave loss

primary_paper_queue.citation: M. Bal, A. A. Murthy, S. Zhu, et al., 'Systematic improvements in transmon qubit coherence enabled by niobium surface encapsulation,' Npj Quantum Information 10 (2024): 43
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Directly compares T1 for Nb qubits with Ta, Al, TiN, Au, and no capping on both sapphire and Si substrates (Table 2 of review); reports T1 ranging from 40-300+ µs with surface oxide thickness measurements; critical dataset for encapsulation strategy comparison

primary_paper_queue.citation: M. Tuokkola, Y. Sunada, H. Kivijärvi, et al., 'Methods to achieve near-millisecond energy relaxation and dephasing times for a superconducting transmon qubit,' Nature Communications 16 (2025): 5421
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Reports median T1=425 µs and T2,echo=541 µs for Nb transmons on high-resistivity Si, representing state-of-art for Nb qubit coherence; detailed fabrication and materials optimization protocol

primary_paper_queue.citation: A. A. Murthy, P. Masih Das, S. M. Ribet, et al., 'Developing a Chemical and Structural Understanding of the Surface Oxide in a Niobium Superconducting Qubit,' ACS Nano 16 (2022): 17257-17262
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Atomic-level characterization of surface oxide in Nb transmon qubits using FEM, EDS, and TOF-SIMS; identifies oxygen-deficient suboxide regions hosting oxygen vacancies as TLS; establishes link between oxide microstructure and decoherence

primary_paper_queue.citation: M. Drimmer, S. Telkamp, F. L. Fischer, et al., 'The effect of niobium thin film structure on losses in superconducting circuits,' Materials for Quantum Technology 5 (2025): 041001
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Systematic XRD study of Nb orientation transition from (110) to (111) with deposition temperature on c-plane sapphire (300-975 K); reports RRR range 2.3-12.5 and Tc=9.25±0.1 K as function of growth temperature; directly links crystallographic texture to transport properties

primary_paper_queue.citation: M. V. P. Altoé, A. Banerjee, C. Berk, et al., 'Localization and Mitigation of Loss in Niobium Superconducting Circuits,' PRX Quantum 3 (2022): 020312
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Wafer-scale XPS, EELS, STEM analysis of Nb/Si coplanar resonators showing atomically sharp Nb-Si interface achievable under optimized conditions; demonstrates process-induced oxides as dominant TLS source; reports effect of BOE treatment on oxide structure and resonator Q>10^6

primary_paper_queue.citation: A. A. Murthy, J. Lee, C. Kopas, et al., 'TOF-SIMS analysis of decoherence sources in superconducting qubits,' Applied Physics Letters 120 (2022): 044002
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: TOF-SIMS demonstration that lithographic patterning and plasma processing foster silicide formation at Nb-Si boundaries; provides depth profiles of oxide evolution between as-deposited and processed Nb films; critical for understanding process-induced degradation

primary_paper_queue.citation: K. Zheng, D. Kowsari, N. J. Thobaben, et al., 'Nitrogen plasma passivated niobium resonators for superconducting quantum circuits,' Applied Physics Letters 120 (2022): 102601
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Demonstrates nitrogen plasma passivation creating ~5 nm NbN surface layer that suppresses oxide regrowth; reports improved Qi and reduced TLS participation after aging; Tc unaffected; practical scalable passivation method

primary_paper_queue.citation: J.-S. Oh, R. Zaman, A. A. Murthy, et al., 'Structure and Formation Mechanisms in Tantalum and Niobium Oxides in Superconducting Quantum Circuits,' ACS Nano 18 (2024): 19732-19741
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: EELS characterization of NbOx-Nb interface showing progressive reduction of oxidation states from Nb5+/Nb4+ at surface to metallic Nb; directly characterizes graded oxide structure responsible for TLS hosting

primary_paper_queue.citation: Z. Sung, A. Cano, A. Murthy, et al., 'Direct observation of nanometer size hydride precipitations in superconducting niobium,' Scientific Reports 14 (2024): 26916
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Direct SIMS and microscopy evidence of NbHx hydride precipitates in superconducting Nb devices; shows H- and NbH- signals confirming hydrogen trapping during processing; hydrides suppress superconducting gap below 1.3 K

primary_paper_queue.citation: S. K. Karuppannan, D. Huang, N. M. Kommanaboina, et al., 'Improved Interface of Niobium Superconducting Resonator With Ruthenium as a Capping Layer,' ACS Applied Electronic Materials 6 (2024): 7372-7379
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Ru encapsulation reduces Nb oxide thickness from ~5.3 nm to ~0.6 nm; reports >80% loss reduction and improved low-photon-regime performance; demonstrates effectiveness of thin noble-metal-like capping with minimal oxide formation

primary_paper_queue.citation: H. Wang, T. Banerjee, T. Farinha, et al., 'Impact of etches on thin-film single-crystal niobium resonators,' arXiv: 2402.18051 (2024)
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Epitaxial MBE-grown single-crystal Nb on c-plane sapphire; reports RRR 20-40 and Tc 7.9-9.2 K; studies effect of etch processing on resonator performance; provides baseline for highest-quality Nb films achievable

primary_paper_queue.citation: S. Kar, C. Weiland, C. Zhou, et al., 'Engineering of niobium surfaces Through accelerated neutral atom beam technology for quantum applications,' Journal of Applied Physics 134 (2023): 025301
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: ANAB processing of Nb produces controlled oxide layers 2.2-5.5 nm with well-defined Nb2O5/suboxide stratification; XPS and TEM characterization; demonstrates room-temperature CMOS-compatible oxide engineering for TLS reduction

primary_paper_queue.citation: K. M. Ryan, C. G. Torres-Castanedo, D. P. Goronzy, et al., 'Characterization of Nb films for superconducting qubits using phase boundary measurements,' Applied Physics Letters 121 (2022): 202601
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: HiPIMS Nb on Si(001), a-plane and c-plane Al2O3; reports RRR~5 and Tc 8.5-9.0 K; also reports anomalously low Tc=7.01 K and RRR=2.59 for HiPIMS on a-plane Al2O3 at 1273 K; phase boundary measurements as film quality metric

primary_paper_queue.citation: J. A. Dhas, E. Bhatia, K. P. Koirala, et al., 'Tailoring the Physicochemical Properties of Nb Thin Films via Surface Engineering Methods,' ACS Applied Materials & Interfaces 17 (2025): 24502-24512
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: TOF-SIMS 3D mapping of hydrogen species (NbOH-, NbH-, H-) in native, CMP-treated, and ANAB-treated Nb films; quantifies hydrogen exclusion (NbOH- drops from 0.16% to 0.06-0.09%); directly links surface treatment to hydrogen-related TLS reduction

primary_paper_queue.citation: M. P. Bland, F. Bahrami, J. G. C. Martinez, et al., 'Millisecond lifetimes and coherence times in 2D transmon qubits,' Nature 647 (2025): 343-348
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Ta-based qubits achieving T1 surpassing 1 ms, used in review as comparison benchmark for Nb; important for understanding the materials gap between Nb and Ta performance and the role of native oxide stoichiometry

primary_paper_queue.citation: B. Abdisatarov, D. Bafia, A. Murthy, et al., 'Direct measurement of microwave loss in Nb films for superconducting qubits,' Applied Physics Letters 125 (2024): 124002
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Direct microwave loss measurement in Nb films; relevant to connecting film-level materials characterization to resonator Qi; provides loss data for HiPIMS and conventionally sputtered Nb

primary_paper_queue.citation: A. K. Anbalagan, R. Cummings, C. Zhou, et al., 'Revealing the Origin and Nature of the Buried Metal-Substrate Interface Layer in Ta/Sapphire Superconducting Films,' Advanced Science 12 (2025): 2413058
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: XRR and XPS study of buried interfacial layers in Ta/sapphire films; directly relevant to Nb/sapphire interface characterization methods and buried layer formation mechanisms discussed in this review; from same BNL group as review authors

primary_paper_queue.citation: R. D. Chang, N. Shumiya, R. A. McLellan, et al., 'Eliminating Surface Oxides of Superconducting Circuits With Noble Metal Encapsulation,' Physical Review Letters 134 (2025): 097001
primary_paper_queue.why_relevant: Noble metal encapsulation of superconducting circuits to eliminate surface oxides; addresses sidewall oxidation problem during etching that is flagged in review as a remaining challenge for conformal passivation

DW factor determines the fraction of photons emitted into the ZPL (well-defined quantum state); higher DW factor improves single-photon emitter efficiency for quantum networks

catchall.free_notes: This is a computational (first-principles DFT) study, not an experimental deposition/fabrication study. No physical film is deposited; the 'sample' is a modeled point defect in a Si supercell. The paper proposes the (CN)Si carbon-nitrogen complex as a hydrogen-free alternative to the T center [(CCH)Si] in Si for quantum information applications. A second configuration, CSi(NSi)Si, is also studied but has DW factors below 1% and is largely ruled out for single-photon emitter applications. All calculations use 512-atom (4x4x4) and up to 1000-atom (5x5x5) supercells with HSE and PBE0 hybrid functionals. The paper is primarily relevant to quantum networking (single-photon emitters) rather than superconducting qubit hardware, explaining medium relevance designation.

[12]). The T center is noted to be sensitive to (de)hydrogenation, motivating the hydrogen-free CN alternative.

(CN)Si.profile_notes: This is a purely computational DFT study of a point defect (C-N complex) in silicon, with no physical film deposition, no superconducting material, and no microwave device. It targets quantum networking single-photon emitters, not superconducting qubit hardware; all vocabulary assignments default to not-applicable or unknown accordingly.

(CCH)Si T center (reference).profile_notes: Reference computational sample for the T center defect in Si; same reasoning as (CN)Si applies — purely theoretical, no superconducting film, no device, outside the scope of superconducting qubit materials characterization.

additional_measurements.description: Superconducting gap difference delta_Delta/h between junction leads
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Gap difference directly controls the Arrhenius suppression of QP tunneling rate; larger delta_Delta exponentially reduces quasiparticle-induced T1 degradation

additional_measurements.description: Superconducting gap Delta/h of the film
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets the Cooper pair binding energy and Tc, determining the thermal quasiparticle density at operating temperature and the energy scale for radiation-induced pair-breaking

additional_measurements.description: Resident nonequilibrium QP density x_QP^ne (in units of Cooper pair density)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Nonequilibrium QP density directly sets the steady-state parity-switching rate and contributes to T1 loss; tracking this parameter reveals how well a device is protected from ambient QP poisoning
Background contribution to parity switching from stray pair-breaking photons; sets a floor on coherence even in the absence of QP bursts

additional_measurements.source: Page 4, paragraph 2: 'burst detection rate in the big-delta device (0.57 min^-1)'

additional_measurements.description: QP burst excess relaxation rate decay time constant
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets the duration of elevated T1 degradation after a radiation impact; longer burst duration means more errors per event in a quantum error correction context

additional_measurements.description: Qubit temperature Tq during burst (effective chip/phonon temperature)
additional_measurements.value: ~90 mK peak, decaying exponentially with ~6 ms time constant
additional_measurements.source: Figure 2(e) inset and page 5 paragraph 2: 'qubit temperature Tq surges to about 90 mK...time constant of about 6 ms'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Elevated phonon/QP temperature after radiation impact limits effectiveness of gap engineering; kB*Tq must be much less than delta_Delta - h*fq for gap engineering to suppress QP tunneling

additional_measurements.description: Film thickness pair for junction leads (thin/thick)
additional_measurements.source: Experimental setup paragraph: 'film thickness pairs: 17 and 83 nm'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Film thickness difference controls superconducting gap difference delta_Delta, which is the primary tuning parameter for quasiparticle tunneling suppression in gap-engineered transmons

anomalous_observations.description: Gap engineering reduces burst detection rate by only factor ~5, whereas thermalized QP model predicts ~10^4 suppression for big-delta device at base temperature 25 mK
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Chip substrate phonon temperature rises to ~90 mK during burst due to slow phonon escape through chip clamps, so (delta_Delta - h*fq)/kB ~ 200 mK only exceeds burst temperature by factor ~2, giving modest suppression rather than the exponentially large suppression expected at 25 mK base temperature

anomalous_observations.description: Qubit temperature Tq remains elevated for ~6 ms after burst, nearly 10x longer than the 0.7 ms decay of nonequilibrium QP density xQP
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Persistent elevated temperature reflects slow escape of hot phonons from the chip through the two chip clamps, pointing to a dominant role of substrate heat capacity and thermal link in determining post-burst recovery

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that larger gap difference delta_Delta yields lower burst detection rate
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation: big-delta device (8.2 GHz) has 0.57 min^-1 vs small-delta device (0 GHz) at 2.76 min^-1, factor ~5 reduction

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Gap difference delta_Delta between the two leads of the Josephson junction, in frequency units (GHz). Controlled by film thickness ratio.
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This is the primary design parameter for gap-engineered transmons: it sets the Arrhenius activation barrier for QP tunneling and directly determines both steady-state QP-induced T1 loss and suppression of radiation-induced burst events. Tracking this across samples would enable systematic comparison of gap-engineering effectiveness across devices and fabrication centers.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table I; Experimental setup paragraph; throughout paper

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Resident nonequilibrium quasiparticle density in units of Cooper pair density, extracted from temperature-dependent parity-switching rate fits
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: x_QP^ne directly sets the steady-state parity-switching rate and quasiparticle contribution to T1 decay. It is independent of temperature (unlike equilibrium QPs) and reflects long-lived nonequilibrium QP populations from stray radiation and other sources. Tracking this systematically would quantify how well different substrates, packaging, and shielding strategies protect qubits from ambient QP poisoning.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Rate of detected quasiparticle burst events per minute, measured by identifying non-Poissonian excess relaxation events above a threshold in continuous monitoring data
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Directly quantifies how frequently radiation impacts cause correlated error bursts; this is the key figure of merit for evaluating mitigation strategies (gap engineering, substrate choice, shielding) relevant to quantum error correction scalability.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Exponential time constant for decay of excess qubit relaxation rate after a radiation-induced QP burst, in milliseconds
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Sets the duration of elevated error rate per burst event; longer recovery time means more errors per event and longer temporal correlations that violate QEC assumptions. Comparing this across substrate materials and chip geometries would guide phonon thermalization improvements.

catchall.free_notes: 3D transmon geometry (cavity QED). All three devices (big, medium, small delta) mounted together in a multiplexed package to ensure identical electromagnetic and radiation environment. Chips anchored to package by only two isolated clamps each — authors identify this as likely limiting phonon escape rate and thus burst recovery. Fridge base temperature 25 mK. Measurement protocol: continuous monitoring with readout every 5.7 µs, qubit reset to |1> after each readout for parity-switching detection. ~10 hours of data collected per device. Medium-delta device results not fully reported in main paper due to complications from gap difference being comparable to qubit energy.

Big-delta device.profile_notes: Transport regime is unknown as no RRR or mean free path data are reported; the paper focuses on gap engineering via film thickness to suppress quasiparticle tunneling. film_thickness_to_loss is supported by the explicit correlation between film thickness ratio and burst detection rate via the gap difference.

Medium-delta device.profile_notes: No T1 value is reported for this device in the main text; coherence_tier set to not_applicable due to absent performance data. Results deferred to supplemental material due to gap difference being comparable to qubit energy, complicating interpretation.

Small-delta device.profile_notes: Serves as the control device with near-zero gap difference; T1 ~100 µs places it at state_of_the_art tier. film_thickness_to_loss is supported by comparison with the big-delta device showing that larger thickness difference yields lower burst detection rate.

film_material.source: Page 5, reference [26]: 'Reaching 10 ms single photon lifetimes for superconducting aluminum cavities' cited as basis for demonstrated qumode coherence time

Qi_internal_quality_factor.source: Page 4 Section VI, finesse F = 5e5 cited for QuGrav toy detector; Q ~ F for a high-finesse resonator; this is an inferred lower bound, not directly stated

additional_measurements.description: Qumode coherence time (single photon lifetime) for superconducting aluminum microwave cavities
additional_measurements.source: Page 4, paragraph beginning 'At present, the demonstrated qumode coherence time already exceeds 10 ms for both microwave [26]...'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Qumode coherence time sets the window within which the qumode must be rebuilt via qubit coupling and QND measurement performed; directly analogous to T1 in qubits — longer coherence enables higher-n qumode states and thus better gravitational wave strain sensitivity scaling as (n+1)^(-1/2)

additional_measurements.description: Cavity finesse F for QuGrav toy detector
additional_measurements.source: Page 4, Section VI, 'currently accessible parameters B0 = 10 T, ε = 1, D = 1 μHz, L = 1 m, F = 5×10^5'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Cavity finesse directly determines the narrow-band frequency resolution and the SNR scaling for gravitational wave detection; higher finesse superconducting cavities directly improve strain sensitivity — tracks microwave cavity quality relevant to bosonic qubit systems

additional_measurements.description: Operating temperature of superconducting qubit-coupled microwave cavities for qumode detector
additional_measurements.source: Page 4, paragraph 2 of Section VI: 'cavities coupled to superconducting qubits operate at millikelvin temperatures'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Cryogenic operation required to suppress thermal photon noise at GHz frequencies (~10 mK photon energy); same operating regime as superconducting qubits — confirms compatibility with existing qubit infrastructure

additional_measurements.description: Narrow-band GW strain sensitivity achievable with n=100 qumode at 1-10 GHz
additional_measurements.source: Page 4-5, Eq. (19) and surrounding text: 'h_noise ≈ 6.3 × 10^{-29} S^{-1}' with S=1 for n=100
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Benchmark for sensitivity enabled by current superconducting cavity and qumode technology; connection to qubit performance: higher Qi (lower loss) cavities directly improve S and thus strain sensitivity

additional_measurements.description: Photon number n demonstrated in optical domain for qumode states
additional_measurements.source: Page 4, 'we benchmark our estimates with n = 100, already demonstrated in the optical domain [17]'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets near-term target for microwave qumode occupation number; in superconducting systems steady progress in cavity QED noted — directly relevant to bosonic quantum computing and qubit-cavity systems

additional_measurements.description: Sensitivity improvement factor from n-photon qumode versus single-photon detector
additional_measurements.source: Page 4, Eq. (18) and surrounding text: 'h^single_noise / h^Qu_noise = (n+1)^{-1/2}'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Bose-Einstein enhancement of photon generation rate — same physics as stimulated emission relevant to qubit readout fidelity and signal amplification in circuit QED

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Single photon lifetime (T1) of the bosonic mode in a superconducting cavity used as a qumode, in milliseconds
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Qumode coherence time sets the operational window for qumode-based sensing and computation; it is the bosonic analog of qubit T1 and directly limits the achievable photon occupation number n before decoherence, which in turn sets the sensitivity enhancement factor (n+1)^(-1/2) for both gravitational wave detection and bosonic quantum error correction — tracking this across cavity materials and geometries is essential for optimizing bosonic qubit platforms
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Page 4: 'the demonstrated qumode coherence time already exceeds 10 ms for both microwave [26] and optical cavities [27]'

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Finesse F of the superconducting microwave resonator, dimensionless (related to but distinct from Q; F = Q * FSR / resonant_frequency for Fabry-Perot type)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Cavity finesse directly determines the narrow-band SNR for both gravitational wave detection and qubit readout; it appears explicitly in the sensitivity formula Eq. (16) and in the S parameter Eq. (20) — tracking finesse alongside Qi would allow separation of coupling and internal loss contributions in superconducting cavity characterization
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Page 4, Section VI: F = 5×10^5 for QuGrav toy detector; Eq. (16) and Eq. (20)

catchall.free_notes: This is a theory/proposal paper with no experimental samples fabricated or measured. The paper proposes using n-photon qumode states in superconducting microwave cavities (coupled to superconducting qubits) for gravitational wave detection via the inverse Gertsenshtein effect. The relevance to the superconducting qubit materials database is indirect: the proposal relies critically on (1) superconducting aluminum cavity T1 > 10 ms (citing Reagor et al. 2013, Appl. Phys. Lett. 102, 192604), (2) millikelvin operation of qubit-cavity systems, and (3) anticipated improvements in microwave cavity quality. The 'QuGrav' toy detector parameters (B0=10 T, L=1 m, A=1 m^2, F=5e5, operating at 1-10 GHz) are theoretical benchmarks, not fabricated devices. No materials characterization data (RRR, Tc, deposition conditions, etc.) are reported. The paper explicitly states 'No data were created or analyzed in this study.'

QuGrav toy detector (microwave).profile_notes: This is a theory/proposal paper with no fabricated samples or experimental characterization; all parameters are theoretical benchmarks citing prior work. Coherence tier is assigned state_of_the_art based on the cited >10 ms T1 for aluminum cavities, but confidence is low given the indirect sourcing. Loss mechanisms and growth method are not determinable from the record.

film_material.source: Main text page 2, Results and Discussion paragraph 1

film_thickness_nm.source: Supplementary Figure 3, Flake 1 cross-section line profile showing height ~130 nm

additional_measurements.description: Er3+ ion implantation energy and fluence
additional_measurements.value: 10 keV, ~1e13 cm-2, 7 deg from surface normal
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets Er defect concentration and depth distribution within CrSBr; implantation damage and Er lattice site determine optical linewidth and spin coherence properties relevant to telecom spin-photon interface applications

additional_measurements.value: mean ~102-106 nm, consistent with PL saturation thickness ~100 nm
additional_measurements.source: SI Section I.b, paragraphs 2-3 and Supplementary Figure 1b-c
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Determines whether Er centers reside within the magnetic host vs. substrate; depth relative to flake thickness controls sensing locality and coupling to magnetic sublattices

additional_measurements.value: prominent features near 1520, 1540, 1560 nm with additional lines to ~1600 nm (4I13/2 to 4I15/2 manifold transitions)
additional_measurements.source: Figure 1c caption and main text page 2 paragraph 3
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Telecom-band emission enables fiber-compatible spin-photon interfaces; wavelength and linewidth determine compatibility with quantum network infrastructure

additional_measurements.description: Er3+ excited-state lifetime at room temperature, bulk region, B=0
additional_measurements.source: Figure 2b right-hand insets, bottom panel (Flake 3 bulk area)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Long ms-scale lifetime sets the timescale over which Er senses quasi-static local magnetic fields; relevant for spin coherence and sensing bandwidth in magnon-photon transduction

additional_measurements.description: Er3+ excited-state lifetime at room temperature, near edge, B=0
additional_measurements.source: Figure 2b right-hand insets, top panel (Flake 2 near edge)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Edge-enhanced lifetime reflects LDOS modification; photonic environment affects radiative rate and thus quantum efficiency of the telecom spin-photon interface

additional_measurements.description: Er3+ excited-state lifetime at room temperature, near edge, B=0.3 T
additional_measurements.source: Figure 2b right-hand insets, top panel (Flake 2 near edge)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Field-induced lifetime reduction confirms radiative rate suppression mechanism rather than non-radiative quenching; important for interpreting magnetic readout fidelity

catchall.free_notes: Flake 1 is shown only in Figure 1b for room-temperature characterization (reflected light, PL map, AFM). Thickness ~130 nm estimated from Supplementary Figure 3. All flakes in the study were implanted simultaneously in a single broad-beam step. The main text flakes (1-6) are all non-annealed. Room-temperature PL shows homogeneous emission throughout this flake consistent with uniform thickness. CrSBr crystal geometry is rectangular, elongated along a-axis.

film_material.source: Main text page 2, Results and Discussion

film_thickness_nm.source: Supplementary Figure 3, Flake 2 cross-section line profile showing height ~0.5 mm scale bar, estimated ~500 nm from profile

additional_measurements.description: PL intensity change under 0.3 T in-plane field (a-axis)
additional_measurements.source: Main text page 3, paragraph 2: 'the presence of magnetic field reduces the PL intensity by about 20%'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Quantifies magnetic sensitivity of Er readout; ~20% contrast at 0.3 T sets the signal-to-noise floor for magnetic state readout in CrSBr-based spin-photon devices

additional_measurements.description: Er3+ excited-state lifetime, bulk region, B=0 T, room temperature
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Baseline lifetime for magnetic sensing; millisecond timescale sets sensitivity to quasi-static fields relevant for magnon-photon transduction

additional_measurements.description: Er3+ excited-state lifetime, bulk region, B=0.3 T, room temperature
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Field-induced lifetime increase anti-correlated with PL decrease confirms radiative rate reduction mechanism

additional_measurements.description: Er3+ excited-state lifetime, near edge, B=0 T, room temperature
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Edge LDOS enhancement extends lifetime; photonic geometry affects sensing fidelity at crystal boundaries

additional_measurements.description: Er3+ excited-state lifetime, near edge, B=0.3 T, room temperature
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Larger field-induced lifetime change at edges consistent with photonic transduction amplification near crystal boundaries

catchall.free_notes: Flake 2 used in Figure 2 for field-dependent PL mapping. PL suppression under B=0.3 T is strongest near perimeter. Field effect is selective to in-plane components (strongest for B||a-axis, weaker for B||b-axis, negligible for B out-of-plane per SI Section III.a). Effect vanishes in flakes thinner than ~50 nm.

additional_measurements.description: PL anisotropy under field: out-of-plane vs in-plane
additional_measurements.value: B||c (out-of-plane): no discernible PL change; B||a: largest dimming; B||b: weaker dimming
additional_measurements.source: SI Section III.a, paragraph 1 and Supplementary Figure 4
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Directional selectivity of Er readout to in-plane field components matches CrSBr's intrinsic in-plane magnetic anisotropy; critical for interpreting Er as a probe of layered AFM order where interlayer moments are aligned antiferromagnetically

catchall.free_notes: Flake 3 used in Figure 2 (field-dependent PL) and Supplementary Figure 4 (field orientation dependence). PL suppression under B=0.3 T extends to selected bright regions. Field orientation study performed by toggling between B=0 and 0.3 T for three orientations (B||a, B||b, B||c). Used in polarization study (SI Section III.b) showing Er excitation maximized with pump along a-axis and strongly linearly polarized emission.

additional_measurements.description: Temperature of Er3+ PL minimum (proxy for local AFM transition temperature)
additional_measurements.source: Figure 3b and caption: 'minimum centered near TN'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Er PL minimum tracks the Neel transition of the host magnetic material; establishes Er as quantitative local probe of magnetic phase transitions relevant to magnon-photon transduction

additional_measurements.description: Thermal hysteresis in Er3+ PL vs temperature on cooling vs warming
additional_measurements.value: Cooling curve sits above warming curve at same temperature near TN; hysteresis window spans ~90-160 K
additional_measurements.source: Figure 3b and caption, main text page 4 paragraph 5
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Thermal hysteresis reflects domain wall pinning-depinning dynamics and slow magnetic reconfiguration; relevant to reproducibility and stability of magnetic state readout in spin-photon devices

additional_measurements.description: Er3+ excited-state lifetime at TN (132 K), Flake 4
additional_measurements.value: ~9 ms (maximum in lifetime vs temperature curve)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Lifetime maximum at TN anticorrelated with PL minimum confirms local field sensing mechanism; long lifetime at transition reflects maximum local uncompensated moment from domain walls and AFM fragments

additional_measurements.description: Er3+ excited-state lifetime at 3.5 K and 300 K, Flake 4
additional_measurements.value: ~6 ms at 3.5 K and ~300 K (below peak)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Shorter lifetime at low T and room T consistent with improved sublattice cancellation deep in AFM phase and absence of local fields in paramagnetic phase

anomalous_observations.description: Er PL remains comparatively flat across 30-40 K with no distinct anomaly, despite bulk CrSBr reportedly showing a hidden-order/dynamic magnetic crossover in this range
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: near-site sublattice cancellation is largely preserved through the 30-40 K hidden-order window, meaning the local order sensed by Er remains effectively unchanged at the atomic scale even while bulk measurements detect a crossover

catchall.free_notes: Thin flake (~70 nm); PL recovery deep in AFM phase (below TN) is modest compared to thicker flakes, consistent with less bulk-like magnetic order and shorter correlation lengths. No external magnetic field during temperature sweep experiments of Figure 3.

additional_measurements.description: Temperature of Er3+ PL minimum (proxy for local AFM transition temperature)
additional_measurements.source: Figure 3c, dip near TN labeled at 132 K
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Consistent with Flake 4 result; confirms Er PL minimum tracks bulk TN across flake thicknesses

additional_measurements.description: Thermal hysteresis training: number of cycles to reach reproducible loop
additional_measurements.value: 3 full temperature cycles required before PL-T traces converge to reproducible hysteretic loop
additional_measurements.source: Figure 3c and main text page 5 paragraph 1
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Slow domain reconfiguration and pinning-depinning more pronounced in thicker flakes; history dependence must be controlled for reproducible magnetic state readout in device applications

additional_measurements.description: High-temperature shoulder extent of reduced PL above TN
additional_measurements.value: Residual AFM signatures extend further above TN in thicker flake than in Flake 4 (~70 nm)
additional_measurements.source: Main text page 4, paragraph 4: 'the high temperature shoulder extends further in the thicker flake'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Longer correlation lengths and greater metastability in thicker flakes; suggests Er probes genuine nanoscale magnetic order surviving above bulk phase boundary

catchall.free_notes: Thick flake (~600 nm); shows more bulk-like AFM order with stronger PL recovery deep in AFM phase compared to Flake 4. The training effect (first warmup defines baseline, subsequent cycles differ until convergence) is especially pronounced in this thicker flake. Used in Figure 3c for multiple thermal cycles.

additional_measurements.description: AFM transition temperature at B=0 (PL minimum)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Baseline transition temperature for field-dependent comparison

additional_measurements.description: AFM transition temperature shift under B=0.3 T in-plane field (B||a-axis)
additional_measurements.value: Shift from ~132 K to ~140 K (+8 K) under 0.3 T
additional_measurements.source: Figure 4b-c caption and main text page 5 paragraph 1: 'The PL minimum shifts from 132 K at B=0 to ~140 K at B=0.3 T'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Field-induced shift of apparent transition temperature is anomalous for an AFM; authors interpret as field-biased ferromagnetic correlations rather than true TN shift; relevant to field-tunable magnon-photon coupling in CrSBr-based devices

anomalous_observations.description: Applied in-plane B=0.3 T raises the apparent AFM transition temperature by ~8 K, opposite to the expectation for a simple antiferromagnet where field should suppress TN via Zeeman-driven collapse
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: the applied field does not raise TN per se, but biases critical fluctuations to promote ferromagnetic-like correlations locally, seeding finite FM puddles that Er registers as deeper PL suppression extending to higher apparent temperature; the shift reflects field-polarized short-range FM correlations rather than a true change in long-range AFM ordering temperature

catchall.free_notes: Used in Figure 4 for field-dependent temperature sweep. Three curves shown: B=0 T warm-up, B=0.3 T cool-down, B=0.3 T warm-up. PL intensity normalized to value at 300 K and 0 T. Field approximately parallel to crystalline a-axis. This flake was thermally cycled after stabilization (training cycles completed before field measurements).

Flake 1.profile_notes: CrSBr is a van der Waals magnetic semiconductor with Er3+ implants — not a superconducting material. Classified as 'other' throughout. Film_only and not_applicable apply because no superconducting device or microwave measurement is performed; this is optical/magnetic characterization of an exfoliated flake.

Flake 2.profile_notes: CrSBr with Er3+ implants is not a superconducting material; no superconducting device fabricated or measured. PL and lifetime measurements under applied magnetic field are optical characterization of a magnetic host, outside the superconducting qubit materials domain.

Flake 3.profile_notes: Same material system as other flakes — mechanically exfoliated CrSBr with Er3+ implants. Not a superconducting material; characterized optically for magnetic anisotropy and polarization properties only.

Flake 4.profile_notes: Thin (~70 nm) exfoliated CrSBr flake with Er3+ implants; study focuses on temperature-dependent PL tracking the AFM Neel transition. No superconducting properties or microwave device measurements present.

Flake 5.profile_notes: Thick (~600 nm) exfoliated CrSBr flake with Er3+ implants; characterization focuses on thermal hysteresis and thickness-dependent magnetic domain behavior via optical PL. Not a superconducting material or device.

Flake 6.profile_notes: Exfoliated CrSBr flake (~100 nm) with Er3+ implants; characterized for field-dependent shift of the apparent AFM transition temperature via Er PL. Outside the superconducting qubit materials domain entirely.

catchall.free_notes: Cr(III) in wurtzite GaN occupies a tetrahedral (Td) site, reducing to C3v symmetry, with Jahn-Teller distortion further reducing to C1h for neutral charge state. Authors conclude substitutional Cr(III) cannot explain the ~1.78 eV PL emission observed experimentally in GaN. Calculations performed using HSE hybrid functional with 29% mixing parameter fitted to reproduce experimental band gap of GaN (3.48 eV). 96-atom and 360-atom supercells used; Table values from 360-atom supercells.

anomalous_observations.description: Experimental PL peak at 1.807 eV in AlN was attributed to Cr(III) 2E->4A2 transition, but calculated ZPL is 0.756 eV — a discrepancy of over 1 eV
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Experimental assignment was based on superficial similarity to ruby (Cr in octahedral Al2O3). In wurtzite AlN, Cr occupies a tetrahedral site, making ground and excited states fundamentally different from octahedral Cr(III) in Al2O3. The emission is more likely due to Co(II) (d7 tetrahedral, equivalent to d3 octahedral by particle-hole symmetry).

catchall.free_notes: AlN host allows larger range of stable Cr charge states due to wider band gap (6.16 eV calculated vs 3.55 eV for GaN). Calculations use HSE with 31% mixing parameter. The experimentally assigned 1.807 eV ZPL in AlN (Ref [17]) is conclusively shown not to arise from substitutional Cr(III).

Authors propose Co(II) as the most likely candidate for the experimentally observed ~1.78 eV emission in GaN and as a promising SPE/spin qubit. Formation energy is relatively low, enhancing the possibility of unintentional incorporation. No Jahn-Teller distortion since e orbitals are fully filled in both spin channels.

Cr(III) in GaN [CrGa0].profile_notes: This sample is a DFT-computed substitutional defect (Cr impurity in GaN), not a superconducting film or device. All vocabulary assignments reflect that this is a theoretical materials characterization study of a spin qubit candidate defect with no microwave device or coherence measurement.

Cr(III) in AlN [CrAl0].profile_notes: DFT-computed substitutional Cr impurity in AlN; no superconducting film or device measured. Science focus includes defect identification and ruling out Cr(III) as source of experimentally observed PL emission.

Cr(IV) in GaN [CrGa+].profile_notes: DFT-computed Cr(IV) defect in GaN identified as a promising spin qubit candidate with very small Huang-Rhys factor; no superconducting material or microwave device involved. Device_demonstration reflects identification of a viable qubit platform rather than actual device fabrication.

Cr(IV) in AlN [CrAl+].profile_notes: DFT-computed Cr(IV) defect in AlN; same favorable spin-flip qubit properties as Cr(IV) in GaN. No superconducting film or microwave device; all assignments reflect theoretical characterization of a spin qubit candidate.

Co(II) in GaN [CoGa-].profile_notes: DFT-computed Co(II) substitutional defect in GaN proposed as the source of ~1.78 eV experimental emission and as a spin qubit candidate; no superconducting material or microwave device present in this record.

Co(II) in AlN [CoAl-].profile_notes: DFT-computed Co(II) substitutional defect in AlN with favorable optical properties for spin qubit applications; no superconducting film or device measurement involved.

Ni(III) in GaN [NiGa0].profile_notes: DFT-computed Ni(III) substitutional defect in GaN; authors reassign the 1.047 eV PL transition from a spin-conserving to a spin-flip character. No superconducting material or microwave device involved.

Ni(III) in AlN [NiAl0].profile_notes: DFT-computed Ni(III) substitutional defect in AlN confirming the ~1.05 eV PL assignment; no superconducting film or microwave device measurement present.

Cu(IV) in GaN [CuGa+].profile_notes: DFT-computed Cu(IV) substitutional defect in GaN; explicitly ruled out as a useful quantum emitter or spin qubit candidate due to mid-IR ZPL and large Huang-Rhys factor. No superconducting material or device involved.

Cu(IV) in AlN [CuAl+].profile_notes: DFT-computed Cu(IV) substitutional defect in AlN with the worst phonon coupling properties of all studied impurities; not a viable spin qubit candidate. No superconducting material or microwave device involved.

catchall.free_notes: This paper studies negatively charged nitrogen-vacancy (NV-) centers in bulk diamond using photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectroscopy at 7 K. It is not about superconducting qubits or thin-film materials. The schema fields for film deposition, Tc, RRR, Qi, T1, T2, etc. are entirely inapplicable. The only tangential connection to superconducting qubit research is reference [29] (Monge et al., Nano Lett. 2023) which studied spin dynamics of a solid-state qubit in proximity to a superconductor, but that work is not the subject of this paper. No schema fields can be meaningfully populated.

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Connection to qubit performance unclear; this characterizes the solid-state environment of an NV spin qubit in diamond, not a superconducting qubit. Local strain splits excited-state orbital energies and affects optical transition frequencies relevant to photon-mediated entanglement schemes.

additional_measurements.description: Optical inhomogeneous linewidth reduction under resonant microwave drive (dressed-state protection)
additional_measurements.value: Linewidth reduced from 98 MHz to 62 MHz (factor of 1.6 improvement)
additional_measurements.source: Page 2, paragraph 3: 'This linewidth then decreases from 98 to 62 MHz, an improvement by a factor of 1.6'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Connection to qubit performance unclear for superconducting qubits; relevant to NV-center spin qubit dephasing and photon indistinguishability for photon-mediated entanglement.

additional_measurements.description: Transverse electric dipole moment of NV- excited state (experimental)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Connection to qubit performance unclear for superconducting qubits; determines coupling strength between NV excited-state orbitals and electric fields, relevant to electric-field-mediated entanglement schemes between proximal NV centers.

additional_measurements.description: Transverse electric dipole moment of NV- excited state (quantum embedding calculation)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Connection to qubit performance unclear for superconducting qubits; ab initio benchmark for NV excited-state properties.

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Connection to qubit performance unclear for superconducting qubits; determines Stark shift of optical transitions relevant to tuning NV emission frequency.

additional_measurements.source: Page 4, paragraph 1 and Figure 4 caption: 'Lorentzian linewidths of about 58 MHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Connection to superconducting qubit performance unclear; characterizes NV excited-state spin coherence relevant to spin qubit gate fidelity in diamond-based quantum computing.

additional_measurements.source: Page 1, paragraph 4: 'Our experiments take place at 7 K within a closed cycle cryostat'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Connection to superconducting qubit performance unclear; operating temperature for NV excited-state spectroscopy, not a superconducting qubit operating point.

NV-center-bulk-diamond.profile_notes: This sample is bulk diamond studied via optical spectroscopy of NV- centers — it has no relevance to superconducting qubit materials or devices. All vocabulary assignments reflect inapplicability; 'other' is used for material_class as diamond/color-center systems have no matching term, and coherence_tier is not_applicable as no superconducting device was fabricated or measured.

film_material.source: Page 3, Section II: 'In all presented calculations, we use the superconducting gap for (bulk) aluminum: 2Δ/h = 90 GHz'

junction_present.source: Page 2, Section II: 'we explain our approach using the specific case of a single-junction transmon qubit under a monochromatic charge drive'

additional_measurements.description: Superconducting gap energy for bulk aluminum used in all calculations
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: The gap energy sets the threshold for pair-breaking transitions; the rate of photon-assisted QP generation is exponentially sensitive to 2Δ̄/ℏωd, directly linking gap magnitude to quasiparticle-induced T1 loss

additional_measurements.description: Transmon qubit parameters used in charge-drive parity switching rate simulation (Fig. 2c)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: These parameters define the qubit operating point and determine matrix elements for pair-breaking transitions, setting the rate of QP generation and thus the charge-parity lifetime which bounds T1

additional_measurements.description: Constant ac-Stark shift used to compare transition rates at different drive frequencies (Fig. 2d)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Normalizing by ac-Stark shift allows comparison of QP generation rates across drive frequencies at equivalent effective drive strengths, relevant for gate and readout drive design

additional_measurements.description: Quasiparticle fraction (xqp) in typical superconducting devices
additional_measurements.source: Page 3, Section II, paragraph after Eq. (5)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: xqp directly sets the rate of QP tunneling across Josephson junctions, causing qubit relaxation, heating, and dephasing; this is the baseline against which photon-assisted QP generation must be compared

additional_measurements.description: Charge-parity lifetime at Floquet 0-π operating point (ϕac = ϕ*ac)
additional_measurements.source: Page 9, Section IV.B, paragraph 2: 'At exactly ϕac = ϕ*ac (not shown on the plot), the parity lifetimes are on the order of Tα ~ 0.1 μs'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: A 0.1 μs charge-parity lifetime sets a hard upper bound on T1 for Floquet-engineered qubits operating at this drive amplitude; this is far below typical transmon T1 values of ~100 μs, indicating strong flux-drive-induced QP generation is a serious concern for Floquet qubit designs

additional_measurements.description: Number of Cooper pairs used in steady-state QP fraction conversion
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Used to convert QP generation rate Γα into steady-state QP fraction x*qp = sqrt(cg/cr), directly quantifying impact on qubit coherence

additional_measurements.description: QP recombination coefficient used in steady-state QP fraction conversion
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets the equilibration timescale for photon-generated QPs; faster recombination reduces steady-state QP population and associated T1 degradation

additional_measurements.description: Steady-state QP fraction corresponding to millisecond-level parity lifetimes in high-frequency readout simulation
additional_measurements.source: Page 7, Section III.B, paragraph 1: 'millisecond-level parity lifetimes then correspond to QP fractions of x*qp ~ 10^-5'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: x*qp ~ 10^-5 is at the upper end of observed nonequilibrium QP densities in superconducting devices; this level of QP poisoning causes significant qubit relaxation and dephasing errors

anomalous_observations.description: Photon-assisted QP generation can occur even when microwave drive frequency is well below 2Δ/ℏ, via multiphoton absorption
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Sufficiently strong drives enable n-photon pair-breaking processes where n drive photons collectively supply energy ≥2Δ; the rate exhibits steplike threshold behavior at ωd = 2Δ/nℏ for integer n, and is exponentially sensitive to the ratio 2Δ̄/ℏωd

anomalous_observations.description: Pair-breaking rates for Floquet states localized at ϕ=π (|gπ⟩, |eπ⟩) consistently exceed those for states at ϕ=0 (|g0⟩, |e0⟩)
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: ϕ=π is an unstable equilibrium of an undriven JJ; inverting the effective potential from -cos(ϕ̂) to +cos(ϕ̂) via strong modulation is inherently unstable, producing larger dressed matrix elements of the pair-breaking operators for π-localized states

anomalous_observations.description: At resonator frequency 25 GHz with ~100 photons, a feature in Tg appears corresponding to a conventional MIST process coinciding with photon-assisted QP generation
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Both MIST and QP generation rely on JJ nonlinearity; as readout frequency increases, MIST is suppressed but QP generation is enhanced, creating a fundamental trade-off in high-frequency readout design

correlations_observed.description: Parity lifetime depends strongly on readout resonator frequency; higher frequency resonators require fewer photons for pair-breaking and thus cause QP generation at lower photon numbers
correlations_observed.measurement_b: Charge-parity lifetime Tg and Te vs photon number n̄
correlations_observed.nature: Higher resonator frequency causes onset of QP generation at lower photon numbers; the number of photons required for pair-breaking equals ⌈2Δ/ℏωr⌉

correlations_observed.description: Authors state pair-breaking transition rate is exponentially sensitive to ratio 2Δ̄/ℏωd, suggesting higher gap materials suppress photon-assisted QP generation
correlations_observed.nature: Exponential suppression of pair-breaking rate with increasing gap-to-drive-frequency ratio; authors suggest Ta or Nb (higher gap than Al) would suppress this mechanism

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Lifetime Tα = 1/Γα for the qubit charge parity state, in microseconds; the inverse of the total parity switching rate summed over all final qubit states
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Charge-parity lifetime directly bounds T1 via QP tunneling across the Josephson junction; for Floquet qubits this can be as short as 0.1 μs under strong driving, making it the dominant error channel. Tracking this parameter across samples and drive conditions would allow systematic comparison of QP-induced decoherence mechanisms and guide readout/drive parameter design
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Pages 4-9, Sections II-IV, defined after Eq. (11) and reported for all simulated devices

schema_promotion_candidates.description: The superconducting gap energy 2Δ/h in GHz; sets the threshold energy for Cooper pair breaking
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: The pair-breaking rate is exponentially sensitive to 2Δ̄/ℏωd; using higher-gap materials (Ta, Nb vs Al) can suppress photon-assisted QP generation by orders of magnitude. Tracking gap energy alongside T1 and film material would enable quantitative prediction of QP generation rates under microwave drives
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Page 3, Section II: '2Δ/h = 90 GHz for (bulk) aluminum'; Page 9, Conclusion: 'rate of pair-breaking transitions is exponentially sensitive to the ratio 2Δ̄/ℏωd'

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Josephson energy EJ/h in GHz; characterizes the tunnel coupling across the Josephson junction and sets the overall scale of pair-breaking rates via Γph = 16EJ/h
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: EJ appears as a prefactor in the pair-breaking rate expression (Eq. 9), directly scaling QP generation rates; tracking EJ alongside T1 would allow deconvolution of material from circuit design contributions to QP-induced decoherence
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Page 4, Eq. (9): 'prefactor here is Γph = 16EJ/h'; Figure 2 caption: EJ/h = 3.025 GHz; Figure 3 caption: EJ/h = 12.85 GHz

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Single-electron charging energy EC/h in MHz; sets the ratio EJ/EC that determines transmon regime and matrix elements for pair-breaking transitions
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: EC together with EJ determines the transmon spectrum and the matrix elements of cos(ϕ̂/2) and sin(ϕ̂/2) that mediate pair-breaking; tracking EC allows reconstruction of QP generation rates from circuit parameters
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Figure 2 caption: EC/h = 56 MHz; Figure 3 caption: EC/h = 218 MHz; Figure 4 caption: EC/h = 10 MHz

catchall.free_notes: This is a purely theoretical/numerical paper with no fabricated samples. The 'samples' extracted here represent the simulated circuit configurations studied in the paper. Three distinct transmon/SQUID configurations are analyzed: (1) charge-driven transmon with EJ/h=3.025 GHz, EC/h=56 MHz for parity switching rate maps (Fig. 2); (2) transmon with EJ/h=12.85 GHz, EC/h=218 MHz coupled to high-frequency readout resonators at 20-46 GHz (Fig. 3); (3) symmetric SQUID Floquet 0-π qubit with EJ/h=81.6 GHz, EC/h=10 MHz, driven at 10 GHz (Fig. 4). All calculations use aluminum BCS gap 2Δ/h=90 GHz. The paper concludes that photon-assisted QP generation can be suppressed by using thinner Al films (to increase gap) or by switching to higher-gap materials such as Ta or Nb, providing direct materials guidance for qubit design.

transmon-charge-drive-simulation.profile_notes: This is a purely theoretical simulation paper with no fabricated samples; aluminum is assigned based on the BCS gap value used in all calculations. Coherence tier is early_exploration because the simulated charge-parity lifetime at the Floquet operating point is ~0.1 µs, far below competitive T1 values, and growth_method is 'other' as no deposition was performed.

substrate_material.source: Table I caption and Section II paragraph 5

loss_tangent_substrate.source: Section II paragraph 5: 'At omega/(2pi) = 4.5 GHz, the frequency relevant for qubits, we have tan(delta) = 9.0 x 10^-9'

additional_measurements.description: Calculated bulk loss tangent at 4.5 GHz due to magnetic dipole transitions at Cr3+ impurities in HEMEX sapphire
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Bulk loss tangent directly contributes to dielectric loss in sapphire substrates used for superconducting qubits; comparable in magnitude to experimentally measured bulk loss of 19e-9 (Read et al.), suggesting magnetic loss from Cr impurities may be a co-limiting factor in qubit T1

additional_measurements.description: Cr3+ impurity concentration in HEMEX sapphire
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Impurity density directly scales the absorption coefficient and thus the bulk loss tangent; higher Cr concentration leads to proportionally higher magnetic loss and shorter qubit T1

additional_measurements.description: Zero-field splitting transition frequency for Cr3+ in sapphire
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets the resonance frequency of the magnetic dipole loss mechanism; off-resonance tail at 4.5 GHz (qubit operating frequency) still contributes measurable loss tangent ~9e-9

additional_measurements.description: Spin-lattice lifetime (inverse linewidth) of Cr3+ excited state in sapphire
additional_measurements.source: Section II paragraph 6: 'Spin-lattice coupling reduces the lifetime gamma^-1 of these states to a value of 37 ns (gamma = 27 MHz)'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Lorentzian linewidth controls how broadly the resonance peak spreads; a narrow linewidth (long lifetime) would concentrate loss near resonance, while broader linewidth spreads loss toward qubit operating frequencies

additional_measurements.description: On-resonance bulk loss tangent for Cr3+ in HEMEX sapphire
additional_measurements.source: Section II paragraph 5: 'On resonance with the transition, the bulk loss tangent can be as high as approximately 10^-3'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Establishes the severity of the loss mechanism at resonance; any qubit design whose operating frequency overlaps with impurity ZFS transitions would experience catastrophic loss

additional_measurements.description: Experimentally measured bulk loss tangent of HEMEX sapphire at 4.5 GHz (reference value from Read et al.)
additional_measurements.source: Section III paragraph 1: 'For HEMEX sapphire at 4.5 GHz they found a bulk loss tangent of 19 x 10^-9'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Benchmark against which the calculated magnetic loss (9e-9 for Cr alone) is compared; authors conclude magnetic loss is comparable in magnitude and should not be neglected as a contribution to qubit decoherence

anomalous_observations.description: ZFS-based magnetic loss mechanism mimics TLS power and temperature dependence, potentially causing misidentification of loss mechanism in standard qubit characterization
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Off-resonant ZFS transitions do not saturate with power (unlike resonant TLSs), but authors suggest that cavity effects or local heating of off-resonant TLSs could produce apparent power saturation, meaning standard TLS identification via power saturation may not distinguish this mechanism

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Concentration of paramagnetic impurities (e.g., Cr, Fe, V) in the substrate, in cm^-3
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Impurity concentration linearly scales the magnetic dipole absorption coefficient and thus the bulk loss tangent; tracking this across substrate suppliers and growth methods would allow systematic prediction of magnetic loss contribution to qubit T1
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table I and Section II paragraph 5

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Zero-field splitting energy of paramagnetic defect/impurity expressed as transition frequency in GHz
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: ZFS determines the resonance frequency of the magnetic loss mechanism; knowing ZFS for impurities in a given substrate allows calculation of off-resonance loss at qubit operating frequency and identification of which impurities are most harmful for a given qubit frequency

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Bulk magnetic loss tangent contribution from paramagnetic impurity ZFS transitions, dimensionless
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Separates the magnetic dipole loss channel from electric dipole (TLS) loss in the substrate; current schema loss_tangent_substrate conflates all mechanisms. Tracking magnetic vs electric contributions separately would enable targeted substrate purification strategies

catchall.free_notes: This is a theoretical/computational study — no physical samples were fabricated. The 'samples' here represent calculated loss for HEMEX sapphire with known impurity concentrations. Three impurities are modeled: Cr (S=3/2, ZFS=11.45 GHz, Ndef=1e17 cm^-3), Fe (S=5/2, ZFS=12.03 GHz, Ndef=1e17 cm^-3), and V (S=3/2, multiple hyperfine transitions 8.68-10.40 GHz, Ndef=1e16 cm^-3). The calculated loss tangents at 4.5 GHz are in the range 1e-9 to 1e-8 for all three impurities combined. The study proposes that ZFS magnetic loss may be a co-limiting mechanism alongside TLS electric dipole loss in crystalline sapphire substrates for superconducting qubits. Separate entries for Fe and V impurities are not created as they represent computed curves for the same HEMEX sapphire material system, not distinct physical samples.

substrate_material.source: Table I caption and Section II
