additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Impurity density directly scales magnetic dipole absorption and bulk loss tangent contribution from Fe impurities

additional_measurements.description: Zero-field splitting transition frequency for Fe3+ (S=5/2) in sapphire
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets resonance frequency for Fe magnetic loss; off-resonance tail contributes to loss at 4.5 GHz qubit operating frequency

additional_measurements.description: Calculated bulk loss tangent at 4.5 GHz from Fe3+ magnetic dipole transitions in HEMEX sapphire
additional_measurements.source: Figure 2, orange curve at 4.5 GHz vertical dashed line
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Fe contributes to bulk magnetic loss in sapphire substrates used for superconducting qubits, potentially co-limiting qubit T1 alongside Cr and V impurities

catchall.free_notes: Theoretical calculation only. Fe has ground-state spin S=5/2 and g-factor ge=2.02. Dominant Fe isotopes have zero nuclear spin so no hyperfine splitting. See Cr-in-HEMEX-sapphire entry for full study context.

substrate_material.source: Table I caption and Section II

additional_measurements.description: V2+ impurity concentration in HEMEX sapphire
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Impurity density scales magnetic dipole absorption; V is one order of magnitude less concentrated than Cr and Fe in HEMEX sapphire

additional_measurements.description: Zero-field splitting transition frequencies for V2+ (S=3/2) with hyperfine coupling (nuclear spin 7/2) in sapphire
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Multiple hyperfine transitions spread the magnetic loss over a wider frequency range (8.68-10.40 GHz); the distributed nature means significant off-resonance loss tails extend toward the 4.5 GHz qubit operating frequency

additional_measurements.description: Calculated bulk loss tangent at 4.5 GHz from V2+ magnetic dipole transitions in HEMEX sapphire
additional_measurements.source: Figure 2, green curve at 4.5 GHz vertical dashed line
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: V contributes to bulk magnetic loss in sapphire substrates; hyperfine splitting creates multiple closely spaced resonances that collectively contribute to off-resonance loss at qubit frequencies

catchall.free_notes: Theoretical calculation only. V has ground-state spin S=3/2. The dominant V nuclear isotope has spin 7/2, leading to hyperfine splitting on top of ZFS, producing 8 distinct transitions listed in Table I. The lowest-frequency transition is at 8.68 GHz. See Cr-in-HEMEX-sapphire entry for full study context.

Cr-in-HEMEX-sapphire.profile_notes: This is a purely theoretical study with no fabricated sample or device; material_class is 'other' because the subject is paramagnetic impurities (Cr3+) in a sapphire substrate, not a superconducting film. Growth_method is 'other' as no deposition occurred. The loss mechanism identified is magnetic dipole absorption from ZFS transitions, mapped to dielectric_substrate and TLS_unattributed since the paper argues this mechanism can mimic TLS signatures.

Fe-in-HEMEX-sapphire.profile_notes: Theoretical calculation only for Fe3+ impurity loss in HEMEX sapphire; no physical sample or device fabricated. Same classification rationale as Cr-in-HEMEX-sapphire entry.

V-in-HEMEX-sapphire.profile_notes: Theoretical calculation only for V2+ impurity loss in HEMEX sapphire with hyperfine splitting producing multiple ZFS transitions; no physical sample or device fabricated. Same classification rationale as Cr-in-HEMEX-sapphire entry.

additional_measurements.source: Section II, page 3: 'a linewidth κ/2π = 7.20 MHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Determines photon shot noise contribution to qubit dephasing and sets the dispersive readout bandwidth; κ relative to χ governs multi-state discrimination fidelity

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: χ/κ ~ 1/6 chosen to allow multi-state discrimination; dispersive shift mediates photon-shot-noise-induced dephasing limiting T2

additional_measurements.source: Appendix A, page 12: 'The measured total thermal population of the transmon excited states is 1.6%'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Residual thermal population contributes to readout errors and sets a floor on state preparation fidelity; indicates effective qubit temperature at operating point

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: EC equals the transmon anharmonicity magnitude; directly sets the spacing of noncomputational levels and the onset of multiphoton drive-induced transitions

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: TLS dissipation rate relative to coupling strength g determines whether qubit-TLS interaction causes exponential decay (g << Γ1) or coherent oscillation (g >> Γ1); directly governs qubit T1 degradation mechanism

additional_measurements.description: TLS-transmon coupling strength (weakly dissipative TLS, transition P)
additional_measurements.source: Appendix G, page 20: 'coupling rate g = 1.6 µs⁻¹ to the transmon'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Coupling strength sets the rate of coherent energy exchange between qubit and TLS; when g >> Γ1, non-Markovian dynamics degrade qubit coherence in ways not captured by simple T1 models

additional_measurements.description: Drive frequency range covered in pump-probe spectroscopy
additional_measurements.source: Section II, page 3: 'ranging approximately from 2.8 to 11.8 GHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Demonstrates that drive-induced transitions occur across a 9-GHz span; electromagnetic environment up to 28 GHz (EM9 mode) must be engineered to suppress mechanism C transitions

additional_measurements.description: Transmon energy levels measured (E_j1 through E_j5)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Full transmon level structure determines all multiphoton resonance conditions (mechanism B); deviation from cosine potential predictions at higher levels indicates Josephson harmonic content affecting DUST onset

anomalous_observations.description: Some intrinsic multiphoton transitions (E, F, M) appear significantly broadened and 'fuzzy' at higher drive power, more than power broadening alone can explain
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The broadening arises from temporal fluctuations of the offset charge (ng) on the transmon island, which shifts the multiphoton resonance conditions. Higher excited levels have exponentially larger charge dispersion, so at stronger drives where multiple noncomputational states participate, the resonance condition becomes highly ng-sensitive and appears smeared in ensemble-averaged data.

anomalous_observations.description: Transition B (TLS-assisted inelastic scattering) appears as a broken line in the spectroscopy landscape, switching between two frequency positions
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: A strongly coupled TLS near 8.17 GHz switches telegraphically between two well-defined frequencies separated by 15 MHz. The switching timescale of several minutes matches slow TLS behavior observed near the qubit frequency, confirming TLS origin rather than a stable electromagnetic mode.

anomalous_observations.description: Package mode EM9 near 28 GHz (far above qubit frequency of 4.5 GHz) is responsible for resonant feature N in the DUST spectrum
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The transmon's coupling to the electromagnetic environment is not limited to modes near the qubit frequency. Any mode satisfying the wave-mixing resonance condition can mediate inelastic scattering, regardless of frequency distance from the qubit. This motivates broadband rf engineering of the entire package.

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that transitions with quasihorizontal dependence (O, P) in drive-frequency vs drive-power space arise exclusively from ac-Stark-shift-induced resonance with TLS, independent of drive frequency
correlations_observed.measurement_b: mechanism classification (TLS resonance vs multiphoton vs inelastic scattering)
correlations_observed.nature: Quasihorizontal features (no explicit drive-frequency dependence, only ac-Stark shift dependence) uniquely identify mechanism A (TLS resonance)

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that transitions appearing in Floquet simulation of isolated transmon Hamiltonian are identified as intrinsic multiphoton excitations (mechanism B), while transitions absent from this simulation but present in experiment are attributed to extrinsic modes (mechanism C)
correlations_observed.measurement_b: experimentally observed transition features in pump-probe spectroscopy
correlations_observed.nature: Agreement between simulation and experiment identifies mechanism B; discrepancies identify mechanism C

schema_promotion_candidates.description: The transition frequency of the qubit (ω01/2π) in GHz
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Qubit frequency determines which drive frequencies activate multiphoton transitions and inelastic scattering processes; it is a primary design parameter for frequency allocation in multi-qubit systems and for avoiding DUST windows

schema_promotion_candidates.description: The transmon anharmonicity (αq/2π) in MHz, equal to -(EC/h)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Anharmonicity sets the scale for all drive-induced nonlinear processes: it determines the power at which ac-Stark shift induces TLS resonance (mechanism A), the onset of multiphoton transitions (mechanism B), and appears in all resonance conditions; tracking this systematically enables cross-sample comparison of DUST susceptibility

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Frequency of the readout resonator (ωr/2π) in GHz
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: The readout resonator is the primary spurious mode mediating inelastic scattering transitions (mechanism C); its frequency relative to the qubit and drive frequencies determines which drive operations activate readout-mediated DUST transitions (K, L, D)

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Frequencies of parasitic electromagnetic modes (package modes, higher cavity harmonics) coupled to the qubit, in GHz
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Spurious modes at frequencies far from the qubit (including EM9 at 28 GHz) mediate inelastic scattering transitions that limit gate and readout fidelity; systematic tracking of these modes across devices would enable database-driven rf engineering to suppress DUST

catchall.free_notes: This paper studies a single fixed-frequency 3D transmon (the second qubit-cavity system in a multiplexed readout setup from Ref. [92]), housed in a 3D rectangular waveguide cavity. The fundamental TE101 mode serves as readout. A dedicated weakly-coupled drive port with reduced attenuation enables broadband spectroscopy from ~2.8-11.8 GHz. The device is operated at dilution refrigerator temperatures with 20 dB attenuation at the mixing chamber stage. Data for drive frequencies above and below the qubit frequency were acquired in two successive cooldowns. The paper's primary contribution is the classification framework for drive-induced unwanted state transitions (DUST) into three mechanisms: (A) TLS resonance via ac-Stark shift, (B) intrinsic multiphoton excitations predicted by Floquet simulation, and (C) inelastic scattering via spurious electromagnetic or TLS modes. The open-source 'floquet' package [78] implementing the Floquet simulation methods is publicly available. The TLS environment near the qubit frequency was monitored for ~22 hours, revealing TLS switching on timescales from minutes to hours. The paper explicitly calls out reducing TLS densities across wide frequency ranges as a crucial materials challenge.

T1_measurement_context.source: Backfilled from similarity_profile device_type='transmon' by backfill_t1_context.py

3D transmon (fixed-frequency).profile_notes: Material class assigned as niobium based on standard Yale 3D transmon architecture (low confidence per record); transport regime is unknown as no RRR or mean free path data are provided. The paper's primary focus is classifying drive-induced unwanted state transitions (DUST) into TLS, multiphoton, and inelastic scattering mechanisms rather than optimizing material or film properties. T1 ~85 µs places this in the competitive tier.

catchall.free_notes: This paper is primarily a photonics/nanoscience paper reporting a new synchrotron-based near-field technique (SYSTEM) applied to MoO3/hBN/Au van der Waals nanocavities. It is not a superconducting qubit paper. The relevance to the superconducting qubit database is indirect: (1) the acknowledgments explicitly cite the DOE Co-design Center for Quantum Advantage (C2QA), suggesting the authors view this work as relevant to quantum information science; (2) reference 48 (Narimanov and Demler, arXiv 2024) proposes 'hyperbolic quantum processors' using these polariton modes; (3) the MoO3 crystal was grown by flame-growth and transferred onto hBN-on-gold without lithographic patterning. Sample #1 (Figures 1–3): MoO3 88 nm / hBN 83 nm / Au 100 nm on glass. Sample #2 (Figures S2–S4, Supporting Information): thicker MoO3 crystal showing higher group velocity, used to verify thickness-velocity relationship — not fully characterized in main text. The hBN used is isotopically pure h11BN grown from metal solution (ref 57). Au substrate is commercial template-stripped (Platypus Technologies). Spatial scan step size 30 nm; 1200 reference mirror steps over 1500 µm total (dt = 8.3 fs step size) for sample #1.

Sample #1 — MoO3/hBN/Au nanocavity (Figu.profile_notes: This sample is an alpha-MoO3/hBN/Au van der Waals nanocavity studied via THz near-field nanoscopy; it is not a superconducting qubit or microwave device. All controlled vocabulary assignments reflect the closest applicable terms, but this record has no meaningful connection to superconducting qubit materials science — it is a photonics paper tangentially linked to quantum information via the C2QA acknowledgment and a cited proposal for hyperbolic quantum processors.

schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: In hybrid integrated platforms (unlike monolithic), this overlap factor cannot be independently maximized from Q/V and is a distinct design parameter; tracking it quantifies how much of the cooperativity gap between hybrid and monolithic devices is due to geometry vs fabrication quality

catchall.free_notes: This paper reports a GaP photonic crystal nanobeam cavity heterogeneously integrated on a (100) diamond substrate containing near-surface SiV centers implanted at ~20 nm depth. The GaP film (200 nm thick) is grown by GSMBE on Al0.8Ga0.2P sacrificial layer on GaP(001) substrate, patterned by EBL and ICP-RIE, released by HF undercut, and then dry-stamped onto the diamond. The primary device analyzed (set(1,1)dev(3,3)) is a drop-coupled PhC nanobeam with Nmir=6, Ncav=12. Device resonance at ~735.68 nm (room temperature) blueshifts ~5 nm at 4K and is tunable ~2 nm by xenon gas condensation. The paper demonstrates first high-cooperativity (C > 1) coupling in a planar hybrid nanophotonic platform. Two SiV centers (SiV 1 and SiV 2) are simultaneously coupled to one cavity. T1 measurements were performed at three magnetic field alignments: severely misaligned (~70 deg, T1 ~1.34 µs), partially aligned (~4 deg, T1 ~97 µs for SiV 1, ~38 µs for SiV 2), and optimally aligned (<1 deg, T1 = 419 µs for SiV 1). The diamond sample is the same as used in prior Chakravarthi et al. Nano Letters 2023 work. SiV 2 was not characterized further after the near-axis field measurements due to its reduced T1 and DIT contrast. A total of 637 devices were stamped across 16 sets on a 2-mm diamond chip.

set(1,1)dev(3,3) - primary device (SiV 1.profile_notes: This sample is a GaP photonic crystal nanobeam cavity heterogeneously integrated on diamond with SiV color centers — not a superconducting qubit device. The controlled vocabularies do not cover this photonic/spin-qubit platform; 'other' is used for material_class, transport_regime and loss_mechanisms are not applicable in the superconducting sense, and device_type has no matching term. Coherence_tier is assigned state_of_the_art based on T1 = 419 µs for SiV 1, which exceeds the 100 µs threshold, though this is a spin coherence time rather than a superconducting qubit T1.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Zero-temperature upper critical field Hc2(0) in Tesla, extracted from WHH model fit to magnetoresistivity data
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Hc2(0) is an order-of-magnitude higher in Ta-Hf than dirty-limit Ta; this directly controls vortex pinning strength and suppresses vortex-motion-induced microwave loss — a dominant loss channel in Ta resonators. Tracking Hc2(0) across samples would allow systematic comparison of vortex loss susceptibility.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Ginzburg-Landau/BCS coherence length ξ(0) in nm at zero temperature
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Coherence length relative to mean free path determines clean vs. dirty superconducting limit, which controls whether vortex motion or TLS is the dominant loss channel. Shorter ξ in Ta-Hf explains enhanced vortex pinning and absence of vortex-motion loss in resonators.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Extracted surface loss tangent tan(δ) from participation ratio analysis of multiple resonators
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Surface loss tangent directly quantifies TLS contribution from metal-substrate and metal-air interfaces; this is the dominant loss channel at single-photon powers in superconducting resonators and qubits. Separate from bulk loss tangent field.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Percentage lattice mismatch between film and substrate
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Lattice mismatch drives residual resistivity and reduced RRR in Ta-Hf films; 1.6% mismatch for 1ks vs 20.5% for 2ks films produces measurably different transport properties. Tracking this parameter would allow systematic comparison of substrate-film compatibility across alloy systems.

catchall.free_notes: This is the resonator device sample: CPW resonators fabricated from 2ks/750C film and measured at millikelvin temperatures. Device fabrication followed same process as Ref. [23] (Crowley et al., PRX 2023). RIE with chlorine chemistry used for patterning. Sidewalls showed porous morphology distinct from native Ta. After BOE treatment, Tc drops to ~5K and surface roughness increases from 1.08 to 2.0 nm, consistent with Hf leaching. Film is a dirty-limit type-II superconductor (confirmed by WHH model fit). No vortex-motion-induced loss observed, attributed to Hf-induced defects providing vortex pinning sites.

Table II Row 3: 1ks, 750C.profile_notes: Authors explicitly identify 750C as optimal deposition temperature for 1ks series; WHH model yields coherence length 10.86 nm confirming dirty-limit. No resonator fabricated from this specific sample.

Table II Row 4: 1ks, 850C.profile_notes: RRR of 1.82 and residual resistivity ~29 µΩ·cm confirm dirty-limit. Structural degradation at 850C noted by authors despite higher temperature, making 750C the optimum.

Table II Row 5: 2ks, 550C.profile_notes: RRR of 1.71 and residual resistivity ~33 µΩ·cm confirm dirty-limit; large lattice mismatch (~20.5%) with sapphire dominates disorder in 2ks series.

Table II Row 6: 2ks, 650C.profile_notes: RRR of 1.58 and residual resistivity ~24 µΩ·cm confirm dirty-limit. Film-only sample; no microwave device fabricated.

Table II Row 7: 2ks, 750C (resonator sam.profile_notes: Qi reaches ~1e6 at single-photon power (state_of_the_art threshold); vortex_motion is listed as a loss mechanism investigated and explicitly suppressed by Hf-induced pinning rather than being absent from analysis. Surface loss tangent (16.9e-4) and quasiparticle activation both measured and attributed.

Table II Row 8: 2ks, 850C.profile_notes: RRR of 1.91 and residual resistivity ~21 µΩ·cm confirm dirty-limit; nearly identical transport properties to 750C 2ks sample, consistent with lattice-mismatch-dominated disorder masking temperature-dependent improvements.

additional_measurements.description: Ginzburg-Landau coherence length ξGL(0) derived from Hc2
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Coherence length relative to mean free path determines clean vs dirty limit; short ξGL consistent with low RRR dirty-limit film where vortex motion may be a significant loss channel

additional_measurements.description: dHc2/dT slope near Tc used in WHH analysis
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Input parameter for WHH model coherence length and Hc2 estimation; characterizes superconducting pair-breaking rate

additional_measurements.description: Nb 3d XPS: dominant Nb2O5 (Nb5+) peak at ~207.23 eV (3d5/2), with NbOx/NbO2 at ~204.56 eV and metallic Nb0 at ~202 eV
additional_measurements.value: Stratified native oxide: Nb2O5 at surface, suboxides below, metallic Nb beneath
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.2, paragraph 1: 'the Nb 3d spectrum displays characteristic doublets at ~207.23 eV (3d5/2) and ~209.98 eV (3d3/2), corresponding to Nb5+ in Nb2O5'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Native oxide composition (Nb2O5, NbO2, suboxides) directly sets TLS density at the film surface; Nb2O5 loss tangent ~1e-2 is a primary decoherence source in Nb-based qubits

additional_measurements.description: O1s XPS: lattice oxygen (O2-) in Nb2O5 at ~530.5 eV and hydroxyl groups at ~532.30 eV
additional_measurements.value: Strong O1s signal with two components indicating surface oxidation and hydroxylation
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.2, paragraph 1: 'exhibiting a strong peak composed of lattice oxygen (O2−) in Nb2O5 at ~530.5 eV and a higher binding energy component (~532.30 eV) attributed to surface hydroxyl groups'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Surface hydroxylation is a known contributor to TLS density; hydroxyl groups at the metal-vacuum interface introduce dipole fluctuators that cause T2 dephasing

additional_measurements.description: Average crystallite size from Scherrer analysis

additional_measurements.value: 2θ = 38.23°, corresponding to lattice parameter a = 3.316 Å
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.1, paragraph 4 and Table 1: 'the Nb (110) peak shifted from 38.23° (S1)'; lattice parameter calculated from Bragg's law in same paragraph
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Lattice parameter close to bulk Nb (3.30 Å) confirms minimal strain in bare film; deviation in S2 attributed to Pt-induced compressive strain

additional_measurements.description: Columnar grain morphology with grain boundaries confirmed by HAADF-STEM and EDS
additional_measurements.value: Columnar grain growth with significant oxygen penetration near surface (from Supplementary Fig. SI5)
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.3, paragraph 3: 'HAADF-STEM image and corresponding EDS elemental maps confirm the columnar grain growth of Nb, with clearly visible grain boundaries and significant oxygen penetration near the surface'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Grain boundaries act as enhanced diffusion pathways for oxygen, worsening surface oxidation and TLS density; grain boundary TLS are a known loss mechanism in polycrystalline Nb films

anomalous_observations.description: Tc of 8.15 K is below bulk Nb value of ~9.26 K despite good crystallinity
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: reduction attributed to oxygen incorporation during deposition, substrate-induced strain, and surface oxidation upon air exposure leading to Nb2O5 and sub-oxide formation

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Zero-temperature upper critical field μ0Hc2(0) in Tesla, estimated via WHH model from R-T measurements under varying magnetic field
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Hc2(0) sets the operating magnetic field margin and is directly related to coherence length and mean free path; tracking this across samples enables systematic comparison of dirty-limit vs clean-limit behavior and vortex-related loss channels

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Ginzburg-Landau coherence length ξGL(0) in nanometers, derived from Hc2(0)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Coherence length relative to mean free path determines clean vs dirty superconducting limit, which governs whether vortex motion or other mechanisms dominate microwave loss; essential for loss mechanism attribution in Nb qubit films

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Chemical speciation of native Nb oxide (Nb2O5, NbO2, NbOx fractions) from XPS deconvolution
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Different Nb oxide phases have different loss tangents and TLS densities; Nb2O5 in particular has loss tangent ~1e-2; tracking oxide composition across processing conditions allows direct attribution of TLS losses to specific chemical species

catchall.free_notes: S1 is a bare Nb film deposited at room temperature and exposed to ambient air. Deposition used 99.995% pure 2-inch Nb target at 150 W sputtering power, 12 mTorr working pressure, base pressure <2e-9 Torr. Substrate pre-cleaned with Piranha solution (H2SO4:H2O2 = 2:1), DI water rinse, IPA rinse. All electrical measurements repeated on three separate samples per condition for reproducibility. Working pressure 12 mTorr for all depositions.

substrate_material.source: Section 2.1, paragraph 1: 'Single-side polished C-cut sapphire substrates (CrysTec) were used as the growth platform'

substrate_orientation.source: Section 3.1, Figure 1 caption: 'XRD pattern of the Al2O3 (0001) substrate'

film_material.source: Section 3, paragraph 1: 'S2: Nb film capped in-situ with Pt (no air exposure) of thickness around 10 nm'

film_crystal_phase.source: Section 3.1, paragraph 1: 'both samples exhibit a body-centered cubic (bcc) structure of niobium, with a dominant Nb (110) diffraction peak observed near 2θ ≈ 38°'

film_thickness_nm.source: Section 3, paragraph 1: 'Nb thin films of approximately 120 nm thickness were deposited on C-plane sapphire substrates'

deposition_method.source: Section 2.1, paragraph 1: 'Niobium (Nb) and platinum (Pt) thin films were deposited using a radio frequency magnetron sputtering system (AJA International)'

deposition_temperature_C.source: Section 3, paragraph 1: 'room temperature growth of niobium (Nb) thin films'; room temperature interpreted as ~25°C

additional_measurements.description: Upper critical field μ0Hc2(0) estimated using WHH model
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Higher Hc2 compared to S1 despite slightly higher Tc indicates increased electron scattering from Pt/Nb interface, consistent with dirty-limit behavior; sets operating magnetic field margin for qubit applications

additional_measurements.description: Ginzburg-Landau coherence length ξGL(0) derived from Hc2
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Shorter coherence length than S1 (8.0 nm) indicates enhanced scattering from Pt/Nb interface proximity effects; shorter ξ in dirty limit implies vortex motion may be relevant loss channel

additional_measurements.description: dHc2/dT slope near Tc used in WHH analysis
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Steeper slope than S1 (0.95 T/K) consistent with increased scattering at Pt/Nb interface modifying pair-breaking rate

additional_measurements.source: Section 3, paragraph 1: 'S2: Nb film capped in-situ with Pt (no air exposure) of thickness around 10 nm'

additional_measurements.description: Pt4f XPS: metallic Pt0 peaks at ~70.84 eV (4f7/2) and ~74.19 eV (4f5/2), spin-orbit splitting ~3.35 eV, no Pt oxide detected
additional_measurements.value: Metallic Pt with no oxide formation confirmed
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.2, paragraph 1: 'the Pt4f spectrum of S2 displays well-defined metallic Pt0 peaks at ~70.84 eV (4f7/2) and ~74.19 eV (4f5/2)'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Absence of Pt oxide confirms Pt does not introduce additional oxide-based TLS at the cap surface, unlike Al or Ta caps which form lossy AlOx or Ta2O5; critical for evaluating net benefit of noble metal encapsulation

additional_measurements.description: O1s XPS signal markedly attenuated compared to S1; O confined below Pt layer per EDS mapping
additional_measurements.value: Strongly suppressed O1s signal; O absent from Pt surface region
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.2: 'S2 shows a markedly attenuated O 1s signal'; Section 3.3: 'oxygen (Fig. 5e, blue) is confined mostly beneath the Pt layer and is absent from the surface region'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Oxygen suppression at the Nb surface directly reduces Nb2O5 and suboxide formation, which are the primary TLS sources in Nb qubits with loss tangent ~1e-2; this is the key mechanism by which Pt encapsulation is expected to improve qubit coherence

additional_measurements.description: Nb 3d XPS after ~40 nm Ar+ sputtering through Pt cap: dominant metallic Nb0 with minimal suboxide and near-absence of Nb5+ peak
additional_measurements.value: Residual NbOx at ~203.7 eV only; no significant Nb2O5 detected beneath Pt
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.2: 'After ~40 nm of Ar+ sputtering, the Nb 3d signal re-emerges, dominated by the metallic Nb0 component with minimal suboxide presence and the near absence of the Nb5+ oxide peak'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Confirms Pt cap prevents native oxide formation at Nb surface; preserved metallic Nb surface without Nb2O5 should yield lower TLS density and improved Qi in resonators and qubits

additional_measurements.description: Average crystallite size from Scherrer analysis
Nb (110) peak position from XRD; lattice parameter contraction due to Pt-induced compressive strain
additional_measurements.value: 2θ = 38.63°, lattice parameter a = 3.283 Å (~ 1.0% contraction vs S1)
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.1, paragraph 4: 'the Nb (110) peak shifted from 38.23° (S1) to 38.63° (S2)...lattice parameter for S1 was calculated as 3.316 Å, which contracts to 3.283 Å in S2'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Lattice contraction from compressive Pt-Nb interfacial strain may influence defect density and scattering; correlated with reduced coherence length ξGL(0) in S2, pointing to interface-driven pair-breaking

additional_measurements.description: Williamson-Hall analysis: crystallite size and microstrain for Pt-coated sample
additional_measurements.value: Crystallite size 20.18 nm, microstrain ε = 0.0068 (larger crystallite and higher strain than S1)
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.1, paragraph 3: 'The W–H analysis reveals that the Pt-coated Nb sample (S2) exhibits both a larger crystallite size (20.18 nm) and higher microstrain (ε = 0.0068) compared to the uncoated sample (S1)'

additional_measurements.description: Pt layer morphology from GIXRD and TEM: nanocrystalline/disordered, conformally coating Nb surface
additional_measurements.value: Polycrystalline Pt with (111) and (200) reflections; conformal ~10 nm coating
additional_measurements.source: Section 3.3, paragraph 1: 'Pt overlayer, mapped in cyan, is continuous across the surface and closely conforms to the topography of the underlying Nb'; Section 3.1 GIXRD shoulder at 2θ ≈ 39.8° and peak at 46.2°
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Conformal Pt coverage is critical for complete surface passivation; gaps or discontinuities in the cap would allow local oxide formation and local TLS hotspots that degrade Qi

anomalous_observations.description: S2 exhibits higher μ0Hc2(0) = 5.8 T and shorter ξGL(0) = 7.5 nm despite having higher Tc than S1. Typically higher Tc would imply longer coherence length and lower Hc2 in the same material.
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: This is consistent with dirty-limit superconductor behavior where the Pt/Nb interface introduces additional electron scattering, reducing the mean free path and thus ξGL(0) while increasing Hc2. Proximity effects from Pt's strong spin-orbit coupling may also contribute pair-breaking at the interface.

anomalous_observations.description: W-H analysis shows S2 has larger crystallite size (20.18 nm) but also higher microstrain (ε=0.0068) compared to S1, contrary to the typical trend where larger crystallites exhibit lower strain.
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The anomaly is attributed to the Pt encapsulation layer inducing interfacial strain due to lattice mismatch between Pt (FCC, ~3.92 Å) and Nb (BCC, ~3.30 Å), and to peak convolution effects from overlapping Pt and Nb reflections in the W-H analysis rather than intrinsic crystal imperfections.

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that Pt encapsulation (S2) leads to improved Tc, enhanced RRR, and higher μ0Hc2(0) compared to bare Nb (S1), attributed to suppression of surface oxidation
correlations_observed.nature: Positive correlation: Pt capping improves all three superconducting parameters relative to bare air-exposed Nb

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Material and thickness of in-situ deposited surface protection layer (e.g., Pt 10 nm, Au, TiN)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Surface encapsulation is a rapidly growing strategy for preventing TLS-inducing native oxide formation in Nb and Ta qubits; tracking capping layer identity and thickness as a structured field would enable systematic comparison of noble metal (Pt, Au, Au-Pd) vs reactive metal (Al, TiN, Ta) capping strategies across the literature

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Boolean or qualitative flag indicating whether a normal metal or heavy metal cap introduces measurable proximity-induced pair-breaking at the superconductor interface
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Pt and other heavy metals with strong spin-orbit coupling introduce spin-flip scattering that locally suppresses the superconducting order parameter; this creates a tradeoff between oxidation suppression and proximity-induced Tc/coherence degradation that must be tracked to optimize capping strategies

catchall.free_notes: S2 is identical to S1 in deposition conditions but capped with ~10 nm Pt immediately after Nb deposition without any air exposure (in-situ). Pt deposited at 100 W from 99.995% pure 2-inch Pt target. Pt exists as nanocrystalline/disordered overlayer (not epitaxial), consistent with room-temperature deposition. Authors note Nb2O5 loss tangent ~1e-2 from literature. Authors cite that removing native oxide can improve Q of Nb resonators from ~1e6 to 7e6 but improvement is short-lived due to rapid regrowth; Pt encapsulation is proposed as a durable alternative. All measurements performed on PPMS Dynacool with 4-probe DC at 10 μA. Three samples per condition measured for reproducibility.

S1.profile_notes: RRR of 2.05 firmly places S1 in the dirty limit; no patterned microwave device was fabricated, so coherence_tier is not_applicable. Loss mechanisms are inferred from XPS characterization of Nb2O5 and surface hydroxylation, which authors explicitly connect to TLS decoherence, but no microwave measurement was performed.

S2.profile_notes: RRR of 2.14 confirms dirty_limit; the Pt cap is a surface treatment strategy to suppress Nb2O5 TLS sources, and authors explicitly correlate Pt encapsulation with improved Tc and suppressed oxidation. No resonator or qubit device was fabricated, so coherence_tier is not_applicable.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Width of the PtSi constriction forming the Josephson junction, in nanometers
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Constriction width directly controls critical current, critical phase, and coherence length ratio — the primary design parameter for PtSi constriction JJs; tracking this enables systematic comparison of CPR nonlinearity across devices
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Device naming convention ScS-50 implies 50 nm constriction width; Figure S5 caption and Figure S3 caption referencing '100-nm-wide PtSi wire'

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Critical phase of the constriction junction CPR in units of pi, above which the junction switches to resistive state
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Critical phase phi_c > pi indicates highly nonsinusoidal CPR, which affects anharmonicity and flux noise sensitivity in qubit designs using constriction JJs; phi_c determines whether the junction operates in the topological or trivial CPR regime

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Kinetic inductance Lk of PtSi SQUID arms in picohenries, extracted from vorticity diamond fits
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Kinetic inductance sets the flux sensitivity and participation ratio in qubit circuits; Lk increasing with T reflects decreasing superfluid density, which is a direct proxy for quasiparticle density at operating temperature
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Figures S12b, S13b, S14b; prose page 6: 'Lk is around 0.12 nH at 0.4 K for all devices'

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Superconducting coherence length xi(0) at zero temperature in nanometers
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Coherence length relative to constriction width determines the number of conducting channels and whether the junction is in the short or long junction limit — critical for predicting CPR shape and thus qubit anharmonicity

catchall.free_notes: ScS-50 is a single constriction junction (ScS = superconductor-constriction-superconductor) with 50 nm constriction width. PtSi is formed by silicidation: ~10 nm Pt sputtered onto Si, then RTP at 600°C for 10 min in 4% H2/Ar. Substrate pre-cleaned with 100:1 BOE for 90s before Pt deposition. Excess Pt removed by aqua regia at 50°C. Devices further cleaned in piranha. The PtSi channel is partially embedded in the Si wafer due to Si consumption during silicidation. All electrical measurements performed in PPMS with He-3 insert (base ~0.4 K). Each V-I curve is an average of 50 sweeps due to stochastic switching.

additional_measurements.description: Kinetic inductance Lk of SQUID arms vs temperature for ASQ-50/100
additional_measurements.value: Lk1 approximately 120 pH, Lk2 approximately 120-190 pH at 0.4 K, both increasing toward 0.6 K
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Kinetic inductance asymmetry between arms contributes to asymmetric SQUID interference pattern; Lk participation ratio in qubit affects flux noise sensitivity

additional_measurements.description: Numerical simulation of modulation depth delta vs coherence length xi for ASQ-50/100
additional_measurements.value: delta increases from ~19% at xi=60 nm to ~32% at xi=140 nm
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Modulation depth of asymmetric SQUID depends on coherence length relative to constriction dimensions; constrains achievable flux tunability range for qubit designs using asymmetric constriction SQUIDs

anomalous_observations.description: ASQ-50/100 shows pronounced phase asymmetry: phi_c2/pi (2.8-2.9) is significantly larger than phi_c1/pi (1.7-2.0) across all temperatures, more than expected from simple width ratio
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Asymmetry consistent with differing junction dimensions (50 nm vs 100 nm constriction widths); wider junction carries more supercurrent and accumulates larger critical phase

catchall.free_notes: ASQ-50/100 is an asymmetric SQUID with arm 1 having 50-nm constriction and arm 2 having 100-nm constriction. Has the highest critical currents of all devices. The naming convention encodes the constriction widths of the two arms. Linear inductance Lk ~0.12 nH at 0.4 K consistent with SSQ devices, confirming that Lk is controlled by SQUID arm geometry rather than constriction width. Critical phase clearly depends on constriction width.

ScS-50.profile_notes: PtSi is formed by RTP silicidation of sputtered Pt; no microwave qubit device is measured, only DC transport of the constriction junction, so coherence_tier is not_applicable. Transport regime cannot be determined without RRR or mean-free-path data for PtSi.

ScS-100.profile_notes: Identical fabrication to ScS-50 but with 100-nm constriction width; no microwave device measurement, DC transport only. Transport regime unknown due to absence of RRR or coherence-length vs mean-free-path comparison for PtSi.

SSQ-25.profile_notes: Symmetric SQUID with two 25-nm constriction arms; measurements are DC transport (Ic, Lk, phi_c) with no microwave resonator or qubit coherence measurement, so coherence_tier is not_applicable. Quasiparticle loss is noted as a suspected channel via kinetic inductance temperature dependence.

SSQ-50.profile_notes: Symmetric SQUID with two 50-nm constriction arms; larger critical phase (~2pi) than SSQ-25 consistent with wider constriction. No microwave or coherence measurement; coherence_tier is not_applicable.

ASQ-50/100.profile_notes: Asymmetric SQUID with 50-nm and 100-nm constriction arms; pronounced phi_c asymmetry between arms is the key finding. No microwave device fabricated or measured; all characterization is DC transport, so coherence_tier is not_applicable.

additional_measurements.value: (9.2 ± 1.4) × 10^6, corresponding to T1 = 292 ± 44 µs at 5 GHz

additional_measurements.description: Re surface loss factor Γsurf at single-photon power
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Surface loss factor directly sets Qi limit via piΓi; limits Qint to ~25 × 10^6 for Re pads, making surface the dominant loss channel and the primary target for improvement

additional_measurements.description: Al surface loss factor Γsurf at single-photon power
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Al surface near junction limits Qint to ~24 × 10^6 for that region; despite smaller area, high participation ratio makes it a co-dominant loss contribution with Re surface

additional_measurements.description: Sapphire bulk loss factor Γbulk at single-photon power
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Bulk dielectric loss limits Qint to ~40 × 10^6; secondary loss channel after surface; consistent with HEM sapphire measurements in prior work [23]
Re-Al interface loss limits Qint to ~486 × 10^6, confirming negligible contribution; validates that Re can be integrated with conventional Al junction processes without sacrificing coherence

additional_measurements.description: Relative loss contribution breakdown for transmon: Re surface / Al surface / Bulk / Package
additional_measurements.value: Re surface 36%, Al surface 38%, Bulk 23%, Package <1%, Re-Al interface 2%
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Loss budget identifies surfaces (metal-air, metal-substrate, substrate-air combined) as dominant loss channels (74% total), guiding material improvement priorities for next-generation devices

anomalous_observations.description: Re and Ta surface loss factors are nearly identical despite Re lacking a native oxide, while Ta has a ~3 nm native oxide
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Either (1) the MA interface contribution is minimal compared to MS and SA in a planar geometry, so removing the oxide has little effect on the total surface loss factor; or (2) the dominant loss mechanism at the MA interface is organic surface contamination rather than oxide thickness, and since Re cannot survive piranha cleaning (unlike Ta), the Re surface may retain more organic contaminants despite the absence of native oxide.

correlations_observed.description: Authors note that the near-junction aluminum region has disproportionately high surface participation relative to its physical footprint, and attribute larger Qint temporal fluctuation to this
correlations_observed.measurement_a: Surface participation ratio of near-junction Al region
correlations_observed.nature: Authors state disproportionately high surface participation in near-junction region amplifies detrimental effect of TLS fluctuators, leading to larger Qint fluctuation

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Dimensionless surface loss factor Γsurf extracted from participation matrix analysis; represents the effective dielectric loss tangent of the combined MA+MS+SA interface region at single-photon power
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Γsurf is the primary material figure of merit for on-chip superconducting circuits — it is geometry-independent (unlike Qi), transferable across device designs, and directly identifies which interface is limiting T1; tracking this across materials enables systematic comparison of Re, Ta, Nb, Al platforms

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Ramsey decoherence time T2R in microseconds, measuring combined dephasing from energy relaxation and pure dephasing
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: T2R captures low-frequency noise (e.g. TLS charge noise, flux noise) not captured by T1 alone; ratio T2E/T2R quantifies pure dephasing contribution; critical for gate fidelity in longer pulse sequences

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Boolean or thickness value (nm) indicating absence or presence of native oxide at the metal-air interface, confirmed by TEM/EDS
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Presence/absence of native oxide at MA interface is a key hypothesis for TLS loss at MA interface; tracking this alongside surface loss factor Γsurf across different materials would allow systematic testing of whether oxide removal reduces MA loss

catchall.free_notes: Best-performing device. Transmon 5 operates at 5.06 GHz. Figure 1(b) shows T1 temporal trace over 24 hours with individual traces up to 451 µs observed. The Re film is deposited in a Kurt J. Lesker CMS-18 system reaching 10^-9 Torr base pressure. Sapphire wafers are 4-inch c-plane HEM, piranha-treated then annealed at 1200°C for 1 hour in O2. Re deposition: 900°C substrate temperature, 30 min, ~0.8 Å/s, 2 mTorr Ar. Wafer allowed to thermalize at 900°C for 15 min prior to deposition. Re patterned by RIE (SF6, 20 sccm, 10 mTorr, 50 W RF). Al junctions: Plassys UMS 300 e-beam evaporation at 10^-10 Torr, Dolan bridge, ±45° Ar ion mill 20s each, 20 nm at -25°, 30 Torr oxidation 10 min, 30 nm at +25°, 50 Torr oxidation 5 min. Junction inductance LJ ≈ 9 nH, junction dimensions 300 nm × 230 nm.

Transmon 1.profile_notes: RRR=19 is intermediate for rhenium (not clearly clean or dirty limit); T1=297 µs and Qi=9.3e6 are state_of_the_art. Loss decomposition identifies Re surface, Al surface, and sapphire bulk as dominant channels.

Transmon 2.profile_notes: Same film process as Transmon 1; T1=277 µs and Qi=9.5e6 are state_of_the_art. T2R much shorter than T2E indicating strong low-frequency TLS dephasing noise near the junction.

Transmon 3.profile_notes: T1=201 µs and Qi=6.9e6 qualify as state_of_the_art. T2R comparable to T1 here, suggesting dephasing is energy-relaxation limited, in contrast to other transmons in the set.

Transmon 4.profile_notes: T1=205 µs and Qi=6.8e6 are state_of_the_art. Residual dephasing beyond energy-relaxation limit attributed to TLS noise in junction region.

Transmon 5.profile_notes: Best device with T1=407 µs (traces up to 451 µs) and Qi=13e6; TEM/EDS confirms absence of native oxide at Re MA interface. Loss budget breakdown (Re surface 36%, Al surface 38%, bulk 23%) explicitly evidenced in the record.

Re film characterization sample (4mm x 3.profile_notes: Film-only R vs T and TEM/EDS sample; no patterned microwave device. RRR=19 is intermediate and does not clearly place Re in clean or dirty limit by standard thresholds.

Tripole 1.profile_notes: Tripole stripline resonator used for participation-matrix loss decomposition; D1 Qi=1.7e6 is state_of_the_art. Three-mode geometry separates surface, bulk, and package loss contributions.

Tripole 2.profile_notes: Second tripole resonator; D1 Qi=1.6e6 consistent with Tripole 1, confirming reproducibility of Re surface loss factor across devices.

Tripole 3.profile_notes: D1 Qi=1.9e6; surface loss factor (3.3±0.2)e-4 at low end of tripole range, confirming Re surface loss reproducibility.

Tripole 4.profile_notes: D1 Qi=2.2e6 is the highest among tripoles; lowest surface loss factor (3.0±0.1)e-4, confirming the 3.0–4.3e-4 range for Re surface loss.

Segmented 1.profile_notes: Segmented stripline resonator with 311 Re-Al interface pairs designed to isolate Re-Al interface loss; Qi=2.0e6. Re-Al interface loss confirmed negligible, validating Re integration with Al junction processes.

Segmented 2.profile_notes: Second segmented resonator; Qi=1.7e6 and Re-Al interface loss factor (3.5±0.9)e-12 Ω·m consistent with Segmented 1, confirming reproducibility of negligible Re-Al interface loss.

additional_measurements.description: Kinetic inductance sheet inductance of NbN film
additional_measurements.source: Appendix A: 'The kinetic inductance of the thin NbN film is characterized as L_k = 105 pH/□'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sheet kinetic inductance directly determines the nonlinearity (Kerr coefficient K) and characteristic current I*, which set the pump power required for frequency conversion; higher kinetic inductance enables stronger three-wave mixing with lower pump power, reducing thermal occupation and added noise in the conversion process

additional_measurements.description: Average free spectral range (FSR) of the multimode ring resonator
additional_measurements.source: Section II, paragraph 3: 'we determine the average FSR over the measured range to be approximately 76 MHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: FSR sets the minimum bandwidth requirement for co-operating quantum modules; a smaller FSR would allow matching to narrower-bandwidth microwave-to-optical converters (~20 MHz), making this parameter critical for hybrid quantum network design

additional_measurements.description: Average coupling efficiency (ratio of external to total coupling rate, eta)
additional_measurements.source: Section III, paragraph 1: 'The average coupling efficiency is 94%'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Coupling efficiency directly upper-bounds the maximum achievable on-chip conversion efficiency via |t|^2 <= eta_s * eta_i; at 94% average, the theoretical maximum conversion efficiency is ~88%, consistent with the measured 91% average

additional_measurements.description: Maximum on-chip transmission (conversion) efficiency between two specific modes (fs=4.85 GHz, fi=6.61 GHz)
additional_measurements.source: Section III, paragraph 2: 'the maximum transmission coefficient is 98.5% ± 0.8%'

anomalous_observations.description: Internal quality factor Qin increases with applied magnetic field strength (above 0.1 mT), contrary to expectation that magnetic fields degrade quality factor
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The frequency shift induced by the magnetic field reduces coupling to spectrally and spatially localized TLSs originating from surface oxides and interfaces. Both the mode profile and operating frequency change, modifying TLS coupling. Authors note this has been described as 'nontrivial' dependence in prior literature (Ref. [37]).

anomalous_observations.description: Measured FSR dispersion is much larger than predicted by the two-segment circuit model (Appendix B)
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Coupling between resonator modes and parasitic package modes leads to mode hybridization, causing irregular (non-monotonic) FSR variations rather than the expected small monotonic decrease from the designed two-segment structure.

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that conversion efficiency increases with increasing magnetic field strength up to a peak, then decreases at higher fields, correlating with the observed increase in internal quality factor
correlations_observed.measurement_b: On-chip conversion efficiency and internal quality factor Qin
correlations_observed.nature: Positive correlation up to ~0.14 mT (peak ~92% efficiency at 4.853 GHz), then negative correlation at higher fields attributed to higher-order nonlinearity

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that applying a TLS saturation tone improves internal quality factor at single-photon probe power, which in turn increases coupling efficiency and conversion efficiency
correlations_observed.measurement_b: Internal quality factor and on-chip conversion efficiency at single-photon level
correlations_observed.nature: Positive correlation: efficiency increases from 26% to 93% as saturation tone power increases to -98 dBm

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Sheet kinetic inductance of the superconducting film in pH per square, characterizing the kinetic inductance contribution per unit aspect ratio
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Sheet kinetic inductance directly determines the Kerr nonlinearity K, the characteristic current I*, and thus the pump power required for parametric processes; higher Lk enables stronger three-wave-mixing with lower pump power, reducing added noise — tracking this across NbN and other high-kinetic-inductance films (NbTiN, TiN) would allow systematic comparison of parametric device performance
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Appendix A: 'The kinetic inductance of the thin NbN film is characterized as L_k = 105 pH/□'

schema_promotion_candidates.description: On-chip microwave-to-microwave frequency conversion efficiency (|t|^2) as a percentage, measured via bidirectional conversion protocol
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This is the primary figure of merit for parametric frequency converter devices used to bridge frequency gaps between superconducting qubits, quantum memories, and microwave-to-optical transducers; tracking this alongside material parameters would allow identification of which film and resonator properties most strongly limit conversion efficiency
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Section III: 'maximum transmission coefficient is 98.5% ± 0.8%'; Section III: 'average conversion efficiency is 91%'

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Added noise of parametric conversion process in quanta (photon number units), measured at near-unity conversion efficiency via variable-temperature stage calibration
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Added noise directly determines whether a frequency converter can be used in quantum (single-photon) information processing; a converter adding >1 quantum destroys the quantum state being transduced — this is a key figure of merit alongside conversion efficiency for quantum networking applications
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Appendix D: 'added noise for the signal and the idler modes are 0.59 ± 0.04 and 0.79 ± 0.08 quanta, respectively'

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Fractional recovery in conversion efficiency at single-photon level when a detuned TLS saturation tone is applied, expressed as the ratio of saturated to unsaturated efficiency
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: TLS-induced loss at single-photon levels is a fundamental challenge in superconducting quantum circuits; quantifying how much efficiency is recoverable via saturation tones (vs. intrinsic material improvement) helps separate extrinsic (saturable) TLS loss from intrinsic material loss — critical for guiding film deposition and surface treatment improvements
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Appendix E and Section III: efficiency improves from 26% to 93% with TLS saturation, indicating ~3.6x recovery

catchall.free_notes: Device is a closed-loop multimode ring resonator (meta-ring) comprising a chain of 3200 asymmetric microloops, total length ~80 mm, folded for compact footprint. Nanowire width ratio gamma = w2/w1 = 0.5 (thinner wire is half the width of wider wire), which is the key design parameter enabling the three-wave-mixing Hamiltonian. Patterning uses single electron-beam-lithography step with CSAR resist followed by fluorine dry etching. Device operates in a dilution refrigerator at base temperature ~10 mK for quantum experiments. Pulsed pump scheme (duty cycle 1/100, pulse duration 10 µs) used to mitigate heating. Measurement bandwidth limited to 8.5 GHz by Zurich Instruments SHFSG/SHFLI instruments. Preliminary amplification screening performed at 3 K before dilution refrigerator measurements. The device simultaneously supports TWM (with applied magnetic field) and FWM (without or with small field) amplification regimes. TLS loss is identified as a key limitation at single-photon probe levels, attributed to oxide layers from fabrication, consistent with literature on NbN surface oxides.

NbN kinetic inductance meta-ring resonat.profile_notes: Qi at high power (~3.93e5) is competitive, but single-photon Qi (~1e4) is early_exploration; coherence_tier assigned as competitive based on the best reported Qi. TLS loss attributed explicitly to surface oxides from fabrication, corroborated by TLS saturation tone recovery and magnetic-field-dependent Qi anomaly.

film_material.source: Appendix A, page 8: 'The aluminum strip [bright pattern in Fig. 3(b)], along with an array of M = 10 SNAILs embedded in line, is fabricated with a single-step electron-beam lithography and liftoff process.'

deposition_method.source: Appendix A, page 8: 'fabricated with a single-step electron-beam lithography and liftoff process'

junction_present.source: Appendix A, page 8: 'Each SNAIL consists of three large Josephson junctions with critical current Ic = 10.1 µA in parallel with one small junction with critical current Ic = 0.8 µA, all formed by the Dolan bridge process'

additional_measurements.source: Section III page 4: 'a resonant mode at ω0/2π ≈ 5 GHz'; Fig. 4 showing tunable range
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Resonant frequency determines the signal band for qubit readout; tunable range must overlap with qubit transition frequencies (typically 4–8 GHz) for use as a readout amplifier

additional_measurements.source: Section III page 4: 'linewidth κ0/2π ≈ 100 MHz'; Fig. 4 showing measured range ~100–180 MHz
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Linewidth sets the amplifier bandwidth for multiplexed qubit readout; broader bandwidth enables simultaneous readout of more qubits

additional_measurements.description: Output signal 1-dB compression power (Pout_1dB) of F-SPA at 20 dB gain
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Compression power sets the dynamic range of the amplifier; higher Pout_1dB allows faster, high-fidelity single-shot qubit readout without saturating the amplifier

additional_measurements.description: Required pump power for 20 dB gain on F-SPA
additional_measurements.source: Fig. 5(a), green dots, page 6; Section IV: 'F-SPA requires 20 to 25 dB less pump power than the C-SPA'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Lower pump power reduces heat load on dilution refrigerator base plate (cooling power ~10–100 µW at 20 mK), directly enabling scaling to larger arrays of JPAs for multiplexed qubit readout

additional_measurements.source: Section IV, page 5: 'the F-SPA achieves an average power efficiency of ηp = 3 × 10^−3, which is 30 dB better than the C-SPA'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Power efficiency directly determines pump heat load per amplifier; 30 dB improvement enables ~1000× more JPAs to operate simultaneously within the same cryostat cooling budget

additional_measurements.description: Pump leakage power at signal port of F-SPA
additional_measurements.source: Section IV, page 5: 'pump leakage power...is on average −113 dBm for the F-SPA'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Pump leakage propagating toward qubits causes dephasing via heating and photon-number fluctuations; 36 dB reduction compared to C-SPA reduces need for additional isolators/circulators and their associated insertion loss

additional_measurements.description: Added noise temperature Tadd of F-SPA at 20 dB gain
additional_measurements.value: at most twice the standard quantum limit (ℏω/2kB), approximately 0.1–0.3 K across operating band
additional_measurements.source: Fig. 5(c) and Section IV, page 5: 'Both devices have added noise that is at most twice the quantum limit for phase-preserving amplification'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Near-quantum-limited added noise is essential for high-fidelity single-shot qubit readout; added noise directly degrades readout SNR and measurement fidelity

additional_measurements.description: Signal-band noise transmission coefficient from pump port to signal output (linear coefficient of pump-port noise injection experiment)
additional_measurements.source: Section V, page 7 and Fig. 6(b): 'the F-SPA is characterized by a fitted linear coefficient of 0.30, while the C-SPA exhibits a higher coefficient of 3.6'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Lower noise transmission from pump port means qubit dephasing from pump-line thermal noise is reduced; F-SPA maintains near-quantum-limited performance with up to 4 K noise at pump port, allowing reduced pump-line attenuation and lower heat load

additional_measurements.description: Threshold pump-band noise temperature at which F-SPA output noise increases nonlinearly
additional_measurements.source: Section V, page 7: 'output noise temperature of the F-SPA starts to increase drastically...at a pump-band noise temperature of 10^3 K'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Connection to qubit performance unclear for this specific threshold, but confirms F-SPA is insensitive to realistic pump-line thermal noise (<<1000 K) at standard operating conditions

additional_measurements.description: SNAIL array composition: number of SNAILs and junction critical currents
additional_measurements.value: M=10 SNAILs; each SNAIL has 3 large junctions (Ic=10.1 µA) in parallel with 1 small junction (Ic=0.8 µA)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Junction critical current sets the Josephson inductance LJ and nonlinearity coefficient c3, which together determine the operating frequency, dynamic range, and three-wave mixing strength of the amplifier

additional_measurements.description: Josephson participation ratio pJ_a of resonant mode
additional_measurements.source: Appendix D, page 13: 'the mode has a resonance frequency ωa/2π = 5.15 GHz and linewidth κa/2π = 137 MHz, with a Josephson participation ratio pJ_a = 0.36%'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Josephson participation ratio determines the ratio of nonlinear to linear energy storage; low participation ensures the stiff-pump approximation holds and limits Kerr self-interaction, directly affecting dynamic range and noise performance

additional_measurements.description: Product pJ_a × Q_a (Josephson participation × quality factor)
additional_measurements.source: Appendix D, page 13: 'This corresponds to pJ_aQa = 13.5'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: pQ product > 1 is the necessary condition for parametric amplification; this value confirms the device operates well above threshold, with margin determining robustness of gain

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Ratio of output signal power at 1-dB compression to pump power (ηp = Pout_1dB / Ppump), dimensionless
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Power efficiency directly sets the heat load contributed by each JPA to the dilution refrigerator base plate; as qubit arrays scale up, the aggregate pump heat load becomes a hard limit on the number of simultaneously operated JPAs. Tracking this across devices would allow systematic comparison of pump coupling strategies and their impact on cryostat scalability.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Section II Eq. (1), page 2; Section IV page 5

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Pump tone power measured at the signal port of the JPA during normal operation at specified gain, in dBm
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Pump leakage propagating toward qubits is a direct source of qubit dephasing and readout-chain heating; quantifying this at device level enables comparison of isolation strategies and their impact on qubit coherence without relying on external circulators

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Linear coefficient relating pump-port input noise temperature to amplifier output noise temperature increase, dimensionless
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This coefficient determines how much pump-line thermal noise degrades qubit readout fidelity; a low coefficient allows reduced pump-line attenuation at the millikelvin stage, significantly reducing cryostat heat load while maintaining quantum-limited noise performance

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Inductive participation ratio of Josephson element in the resonant mode (pJ = La/LJ), dimensionless, typically expressed as a percentage
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Josephson participation ratio sets the balance between nonlinearity and linewidth; it determines Kerr coefficient, dynamic range, and whether the pQ amplification condition is satisfied. Tracking this across designs would allow systematic optimization of amplifier operating point.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Appendix C Eq. (C9), Appendix D page 13

catchall.free_notes: This paper reports two devices: the filter-coupled SPA (F-SPA, the primary device) and a capacitor-coupled SPA (C-SPA, the control device). Both use identical SNAIL arrays and are designed with nominally identical resonance frequency, linewidth, and three-wave mixing strength for fair comparison. The C-SPA linewidth (60–90 MHz) was ~2x smaller than F-SPA (~100–180 MHz) due to fabrication uncertainty, which gives C-SPA a ~6 dB inherent pump power advantage. Despite this, F-SPA still shows 20–25 dB lower pump power requirement. The microstrip ground plane is a 2-µm silver layer on the back of the 300-µm Si substrate. The device architecture is a microstrip with on-chip three-stage hairpin bandpass filter (pump port, passes ~10 GHz, rejects ~5 GHz) and three-stage radial-stub lowpass filter (signal port, creates stopband near ~10 GHz, places amplifier mode in transition band). Fabrication follows the process of Frattini et al. (Ref. 8) and Sivak et al. (Ref. 15). Measurements were performed in three separate cooldowns: Cooldown 1 (F-SPA), Cooldown 2 (C-SPA), Cooldown 3 (SNTJ noise calibration). The film_thickness_nm field extracted above (2000 nm) refers to the silver ground plane, not the aluminum circuit layer — the Al layer thickness is not explicitly stated and should be treated as unreliable; this field should ideally be removed or flagged.

F-SPA (Filter-coupled SNAIL Parametric A.profile_notes: This device is a SNAIL parametric amplifier — not a qubit or resonator loss study — so coherence_tier is not_applicable and no standard qubit loss mechanisms are attributable. Device type does not map cleanly to the vocabulary (closest would be 'resonator' but it is functionally an amplifier); 'unknown' is used to avoid misclassification. Growth method inferred as evaporation from the liftoff process context typical for Al Dolan-bridge junctions, though not explicitly stated.

loss_tangent_interface.source: Table I: Bare Ta tan δ = (15.9 ± 0.7) × 10^-4, cited from reference [18]

additional_measurements.description: Surface Ta2O5 oxide present, confirmed by XPS Ta4f spectrum showing oxide peaks at 27 and 29 eV
additional_measurements.value: Ta2O5 peaks present at 27 and 29 eV binding energy
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Native Ta2O5 surface oxide hosts two-level systems (TLS), which are a dominant source of dielectric loss limiting Qi and qubit T1 coherence times in tantalum-based devices

catchall.free_notes: This is the reference sample from prior work (Crowley et al., Phys. Rev. X 13, 041005 (2023)). Base pressure of UHV chamber: 5×10^-9 Torr. Substrate thickness: 550 μm.

substrate_material.source: Page 2 paragraph 1: '200-nm-thick α-Ta films grown on 550-μm-thick c-plane sapphire substrates'

deposition_method.value: dc magnetron sputtering (Ta layer); in situ dc magnetron sputtering at room temperature (Au layer)
deposition_method.source: Page 2 paragraph 1: 'The Au (AuPd) encapsulation layer is sputtered in situ at room temperature after the Ta film growth is completed'

deposition_temperature_C.source: Page 2 paragraph 1: Au encapsulation 'sputtered in situ at room temperature'

surface_oxide_thickness_nm.source: Page 2 paragraph 2 and Figure 1(b)/(c): 'The Ta2O5 peaks are not present in the binding energy spectrum of Au-encapsulated Ta, demonstrating that the amorphous surface oxides of Ta have been suppressed'; EELS map shows oxygen-free Ta-Au interface

loss_tangent_interface.source: Table I: Au encapsulation tan δ = (19.5 ± 2) × 10^-4

Qi_internal_quality_factor.source: Page 3 paragraph 4: 'encapsulated resonators maintain a high quality factor (≈10^7) at base temperatures and high powers' for encapsulation thicknesses 3 to 26 nm

additional_measurements.description: Minimum Au encapsulation thickness required to fully suppress Ta2O5 formation
additional_measurements.source: Page 2 paragraph 3 and Figure 1(d): '2.5 (1) nm of encapsulating Au (AuPd) is enough to fully suppress the tantalum oxide'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets the minimum encapsulation thickness needed to eliminate the TLS-hosting surface oxide, which is the dominant loss channel in state-of-the-art Ta qubits

additional_measurements.description: Effective superconducting minigap in Au encapsulation layer as fraction of bulk Ta gap, for ~3 nm Au
additional_measurements.source: Page 3 paragraph 4: 'the effective gap energy is still greater than 80% of the bulk Ta gap' for ~3 nm Au encapsulation
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: The minigap in the proximitized noble metal sets the thermal quasiparticle population at operating temperature, which directly affects resonator loss and qubit T1

additional_measurements.description: Electron interface transparency at Ta-Au interface, fitted from Usadel equation model
additional_measurements.source: Page 3 paragraph 2: 'electron interface transparency, which we fit to a value of t = 0.24 +0.17/-0.08'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: High interface transparency confirms Ohmic contact between Ta and Au, meaning proximitization is efficient and normal-metal-induced losses are minimized; low transparency would cause unpaired quasiparticle loss

additional_measurements.description: Sidewall oxide participation fraction of total metal-air interface electric field energy
additional_measurements.source: Page 3 paragraph 6 and Figure 3(d): 'The sidewall oxide dominates the total participation ratio, accounting for more than 90% of electric field energy participation along the metal-air interfaces across all device geometries'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Demonstrates that subtractive-etch resonators have loss dominated by sidewall oxide TLS even when top surface is encapsulated; implies additive fabrication is needed to achieve full oxide elimination and maximize Qi

additional_measurements.description: Encapsulation thickness range over which high Qi is maintained at base temperature
additional_measurements.source: Page 3 paragraph 4: 'ideal range of encapsulation thicknesses from 3 to 26 nm over which encapsulated resonators maintain a high quality factor (≈10^7) at base temperatures and high powers'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Defines the practical process window for Au encapsulation thickness compatible with both oxide suppression and high Qi; relevant for process targeting and yield

anomalous_observations.description: Despite complete suppression of top-surface Ta2O5, no improvement in surface TLS loss tangent was observed; encapsulated devices showed slightly higher loss tangents than bare Ta
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Subtractive etch process leaves unencapsulated sidewalls which grow native oxide during patterning; sidewall oxide accounts for >90% of metal-air interface participation, dominating the total TLS loss. Additional roughness from the multi-step etch process may also increase surface participation ratio and fitted loss tangent.

anomalous_observations.description: For Au encapsulation thicknesses below 2.5 nm, Ta2O5 fraction is anomalously higher than bare Ta
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Sub-threshold Au islands catalyze oxidation of the Ta surface (oxidation catalysis effect from small Au islands), leading to enhanced oxide formation before the film fully covers the Ta surface

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that increasing Au encapsulation thickness leads to a decrease in the effective superconducting gap (minigap) of the resonator
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation; gap decreases monotonically from ~1.0 at 5 nm to near 0 at 323 nm, fitted by Usadel proximity effect model

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Material of the noble metal encapsulation layer deposited on top of the superconductor (e.g., Au, AuPd)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Encapsulation material determines the oxide suppression threshold thickness, proximitization efficiency (minigap), and interface transparency — all of which directly set the loss floor for encapsulated resonators; tracking this enables systematic comparison across encapsulation strategies

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Thickness of the noble metal encapsulation layer in nanometers
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This parameter directly controls both oxide suppression (requires minimum threshold) and the effective superconducting gap via proximity effect (decreases with thickness); it is the primary tuning parameter for the encapsulation strategy and must be tracked to compare samples

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Effective superconducting gap of the heterostructure expressed as a fraction of the bulk superconductor gap Δ0, extracted from temperature-dependent microwave measurements
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: The effective gap sets the thermal quasiparticle density at operating temperature, which is a direct loss channel in resonators and qubits; a reduced gap means more quasiparticles at 10-20 mK operating temperature and lower T1

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Fraction of total metal-air interface electric field participation attributable to the unencapsulated sidewall oxide, from FEM simulation
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: This parameter quantifies how much of the TLS loss comes from sidewall vs. top-surface oxide, enabling rational design of encapsulation and etching strategies; in subtractive-etch devices it is >90%, explaining why top-surface encapsulation alone does not improve Qi

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Electron interface transparency t at the superconductor–normal metal interface, dimensionless (0–1), fitted from Usadel equation model of minigap vs. encapsulation thickness
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Interface transparency directly determines how efficiently the superconducting order parameter is induced in the normal metal layer; low transparency leads to a weaker minigap and higher quasiparticle loss; tracking this across in-situ vs. ex-situ deposition conditions would allow systematic comparison of interface quality

catchall.free_notes: Both Ta and Au layers deposited in the same UHV chamber (base pressure 5×10^-9 Torr) without breaking vacuum. Au encapsulated resonators fabricated by photolithography + multistep etch (first etch encapsulation layer, then Ta layer). Resonators are CPW quarter-wave design, same geometry as prior work (Crowley et al. 2023). For characterization of loss, resonator geometries were varied to change surface participation ratio (SPR). Loss tangent extracted from QTLS,0 vs SPR fitting. The substrate thickness was 550 μm.

deposition_method.value: dc magnetron sputtering (Ta layer); in situ dc magnetron sputtering at room temperature (AuPd layer)

deposition_temperature_C.source: Page 2 paragraph 1: AuPd encapsulation 'sputtered in situ at room temperature'

surface_oxide_thickness_nm.source: Page 2 paragraph 3 and Figure 1(d): '1 nm of AuPd is enough to fully suppress the tantalum oxide'

loss_tangent_interface.source: Table I: AuPd encapsulation tan δ = (25.8 ± 0.8) × 10^-4

additional_measurements.description: Minimum AuPd encapsulation thickness required to fully suppress Ta2O5 formation
additional_measurements.source: Page 2 paragraph 3 and Figure 1(d): '1 nm of AuPd is enough to fully suppress the tantalum oxide'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: AuPd achieves oxide suppression at a thinner critical thickness than pure Au, suggesting it forms a more continuous film at lower thickness; thinner encapsulation means less proximity effect gap reduction and lower quasiparticle loss

anomalous_observations.description: AuPd encapsulation has a higher surface loss tangent (25.8×10^-4) than Au encapsulation (19.5×10^-4), despite both suppressing the surface oxide
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Not explicitly stated for AuPd specifically; generally attributed to unencapsulated sidewall oxide dominating loss and potentially increased sidewall roughness from the multi-step etch. The Pd content of the alloy may also introduce additional loss mechanisms not present in pure Au.

catchall.free_notes: AuPd is a gold-palladium alloy; exact composition not specified in the main text (referred to supplemental material). Both Ta and AuPd layers deposited in the same UHV chamber without breaking vacuum. Same CPW resonator geometry and fabrication process as Au-encapsulated samples.

Bare Ta (reference).profile_notes: This is a reference sample from prior work (Crowley et al. 2023); Qi ~10^7 and alpha-Ta phase support state_of_the_art tier and clean_limit assignment. Loss tangent and XPS-confirmed Ta2O5 surface oxide directly link surface oxide to TLS loss.

Ta + Au encapsulation.profile_notes: Qi ~10^7 maintained for 3–26 nm Au range, placing this in state_of_the_art tier. Quasiparticle loss is included because the paper explicitly models proximity-effect-induced minigap reduction and its impact on quasiparticle density at operating temperature. Sidewall oxide dominates residual TLS loss despite top-surface oxide suppression.

Ta + AuPd encapsulation.profile_notes: AuPd suppresses Ta2O5 at a thinner critical thickness (1 nm vs 2.5 nm for Au) but yields a higher fitted loss tangent, attributed to sidewall oxide domination and possible Pd-related loss; coherence_tier assigned state_of_the_art by analogy with Au-encapsulated samples in the same fabrication run, as the paper does not report a separate Qi value for AuPd.

catchall.free_notes: This paper studies a single Purcell-protected transmon device tested under four readout configurations (τro = 240, 160, 120, 100 ns), each independently optimized for maximum fidelity at a given readout duration. The device is embedded in a multimodal circuit at Yale. A SNAIL parametric amplifier (SPA) with 3 dB bandwidth of 8.3 MHz is used in the readout chain. The four readout settings represent operating points on the same physical device, not separate samples. Qubit initialization uses preselection. The π pulse used in RILB is 100 ns duration. Fabrication supported by YINQE and Yale SEAS Cleanroom.
