additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: At 7.76% leakage per readout, this operating point would cause rapid qubit corruption in QEC cycles despite >99.5% binary fidelity; illustrates critical failure mode missed by standard metrics

anomalous_observations.description: All four readout settings achieve >99.5% binary fidelity and ~99% repeatability, yet leakage rates span 0.12% to 7.76%—a factor of ~65x variation. Standard metrics fail to detect this large variation in leakage.
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Binary thresholding of IQ data cannot distinguish leakage states from computational states, especially when leakage states overlap with |g> or |e> blobs or when a phase-sensitive amplifier erases the orthogonal quadrature information. The RILB technique exploits the correlated, persistent nature of leakage errors across repeated measurements to detect what single-shot metrics cannot.

correlations_observed.description: Authors show that increasing readout speed (shorter τro, higher n̄r) leads to dramatically increased leakage rates while fidelity and repeatability remain approximately constant
correlations_observed.nature: Strong negative correlation: leakage increases from 0.12% to 7.76% as τro decreases from 240 ns to 100 ns; fidelity and repeatability do not track this trend

Purcell-protected transmon, τro=240ns.profile_notes: Material class assigned as aluminum by default for transmon junction devices where no primary film material is specified; the paper focuses on readout-induced leakage characterization rather than materials properties, so most material-science dimensions are not determinable. Gate fidelity >99.5% and the device context place this in state_of_the_art tier.

Purcell-protected transmon, τro=160ns.profile_notes: Same physical device as τro=240ns sample measured at a different readout operating point; material and transport assignments follow the same reasoning as the 240ns sample.

Purcell-protected transmon, τro=120ns.profile_notes: Same physical device measured at a faster readout operating point; leakage rises to 2.14% but binary fidelity remains >99.5%, illustrating the limitation of standard metrics that this paper investigates.

Purcell-protected transmon, τro=100ns.profile_notes: Fastest readout point with 7.76% leakage per readout despite >99.5% binary fidelity; noise_characterization added here because this sample's data anchors the paper's core finding about the dissociation between fidelity metrics and leakage rates, and the RILB protocol is explicitly framed as a new characterization technique.

additional_measurements.description: Kerr nonlinearity K of the SNAIL mode
additional_measurements.source: page 3, paragraph 3: 'TK = 272 ns corresponding to K/2π = 0.93 ± 0.03 MHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Kerr nonlinearity sets the energy level spacing and the time for the σx(π/2) Kerr-cat gate; it directly determines the KCQ gate speed and the noise bias suppression of bit-flip errors

additional_measurements.description: KCQ coherent state lifetime TZ (Z-basis, bit-flip lifetime) at α²=4
additional_measurements.source: page 3, paragraph 3: 'the KCQ has a coherent state lifetime TZ = 147 ± 4 μs'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Bit-flip lifetime of the Kerr-cat qubit ancilla; longer TZ reduces ancilla bit-flip errors that would propagate to the logical qubit during syndrome measurements

additional_measurements.description: KCQ cat state lifetime TX,Y (X/Y-basis, phase-flip lifetime) at α²=4
additional_measurements.source: page 3, paragraph 3: 'a cat state lifetime TX,Y = 2.32 ± 0.04 μs'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Phase-flip lifetime of the Kerr-cat qubit; sets the dominant error channel of the KCQ ancilla, which must be transparent to bosonic code syndrome measurements

additional_measurements.description: KCQ noise bias (bit-flip to phase-flip lifetime ratio) at α²=4
additional_measurements.source: Calculated from page 3, paragraph 3 values: TZ = 147 μs, TX,Y = 2.32 μs
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Noise bias quantifies exponential suppression of bit-flip errors; a bias of ~63 here (lower than the ~1000 reported in refs 28-30) indicates room for improvement before fault-tolerant syndrome measurement is achievable

additional_measurements.description: Storage cavity (3D Al cavity) T1 without KCQ squeezing
additional_measurements.source: page 4, paragraph 3: 'the decay of 〈Z(t)〉 tells us T1 = 204 ± 9 μs'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: T1 of the high-Q storage cavity sets the upper bound on logical qubit coherence time for bosonic codes; limiting loss mechanisms determine the achievable logical error rate

additional_measurements.description: Storage cavity T2 without KCQ squeezing (bare SNAIL present)
additional_measurements.source: page 4, paragraph 3: 'the ring-downs of 〈X(t)〉 and 〈Y(t)〉 tell us T2 = 381 ± 8 μs'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: T2 < 2T1 indicates excess dephasing from SNAIL thermal population coupling via cross-Kerr interaction; this dephasing channel limits logical qubit coherence and must be mitigated for fault-tolerant operation

additional_measurements.description: Storage cavity pure dephasing rate from direct T1/T2 measurement
additional_measurements.source: page 4, paragraph 3: 'corresponding to a pure dephasing rate Γφ = (5.7±2.8 ms)⁻¹'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Dephasing rate of the storage cavity directly limits coherence time and logical qubit performance; attributed to SNAIL thermal population via cross-Kerr interaction

additional_measurements.description: SNAIL thermal population nth measured by state-swap method
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Thermal population of the ancilla (SNAIL/KCQ) couples to the storage cavity via cross-Kerr interaction, causing dephasing that limits storage coherence; reducing nth is required for high-fidelity bosonic code operations

additional_measurements.description: Storage cavity pure dephasing rate inferred from SNAIL thermal population
additional_measurements.source: page 4, paragraph 3: 'corresponding to an induced dephasing rate of Γφ = (7.3±1.3 ms)⁻¹'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Independent confirmation that SNAIL thermal population is the dominant dephasing source for storage cavity; consistent with direct T2 measurement

additional_measurements.description: Cross-Kerr interaction strength between SNAIL and storage cavity
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Cross-Kerr coupling between ancilla and storage cavity is the primary mechanism by which ancilla thermal population dephases the storage qubit; minimizing χab reduces idle dephasing while maintaining control via driven parametric interactions
CD gate rate must exceed all decoherence rates to achieve coherent entangling operations; also sets the on-off ratio gCD/χres > 6000 demonstrated in this work

additional_measurements.description: Upper bound on residual cross-Kerr interaction with FSD active
additional_measurements.source: page 5, paragraph 3: 'we can bound χres/2π < 1 kHz'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Residual entangling interaction between KCQ and storage during idling time limits the on-off ratio of control and sets the floor for idle dephasing of the storage qubit

additional_measurements.description: On-off ratio of cavity control (driven CD rate vs residual interaction)
additional_measurements.source: page 5, paragraph 3: 'yielding a bound on the on-off control ratio of gCD/χres > 6000'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: High on-off ratio ensures the ancilla only interacts with the storage when actively driven; critical for fault-tolerant syndrome measurement where idle dephasing must be minimized

additional_measurements.description: Upper bound on residual storage dephasing rate with FSD active

additional_measurements.description: Storage cavity T2 with FSD active (at various α²)

additional_measurements.source: page 5, paragraph 2 and Figure 4b caption: 'the active cooling on the KCQ improves T2 of the storage cavity to a level consistent with zero dephasing'; '435 ± 17 μs'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Demonstrates that FSD eliminates idle dephasing of the storage cavity, recovering the intrinsic T1-limited coherence — a necessary condition for fault-tolerant bosonic code operation

additional_measurements.description: KCQ excited state energy gap at α=2
additional_measurements.source: page 5, paragraph 1: 'We calibrate the energy gap ωgap/2π = 12.5 MHz at α = 2'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Energy gap between ground and excited manifolds of KCQ determines the frequency of the FSD drive; sets the selectivity of the cooling process that eliminates storage dephasing

additional_measurements.source: page 3, paragraph 1: 'the on-chip SNAIL mode (ωa = 2π × 4.00 GHz)'

additional_measurements.source: page 3, paragraph 1: 'the fundamental mode of the 3D storage cavity (ωb = 2π × 7.02 GHz)'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Operating frequency of the bosonic code storage mode; higher frequency reduces thermal population at base temperature

additional_measurements.source: page 3, paragraph 1: 'the first harmonic of the 3D readout cavity (ωr = 2π × 9.36 GHz)'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Readout cavity frequency used for CQR and as dissipation channel for FSD; relevant for designing multi-mode superconducting circuit architectures

anomalous_observations.description: Storage cavity T2 fails to recover to T2 = 2T1 as KCQ cat size α increases, even though the cross-Kerr shot noise dephasing is exponentially suppressed. A second, unexpected dephasing channel prevents full recovery.
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: The residual dephasing is due to heating of the KCQ into excited states outside the computational manifold. These excited states have different average photon numbers than the ground states and therefore cause different Stark shifts of the storage cavity, leading to dephasing even when the computational-manifold cross-Kerr is exponentially suppressed. This was 'not previously predicted.'

anomalous_observations.description: SNAIL thermal population nth = 2.8% is unexpectedly high, causing measurable storage dephasing even without a KCQ stabilized. The origin of this heating is described as 'a subject of ongoing research.'
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Not fully explained. Authors note the heating also causes a staircase-like pattern in KCQ coherent state lifetime as squeezing drive strength increases (seen in refs 28-30), but state 'we do not yet have a quantitative understanding of the KCQ lifetimes in terms of the underlying physical error rates of the SNAIL.'

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that as α² increases from 0 to ~0.5, storage T2 decreases (more dephasing), then increases again for larger α², indicating suppression of cross-Kerr shot noise dephasing by exponential factor exp(-2α²)
correlations_observed.nature: Non-monotonic: T2 decreases to minimum at α²≈0.5, then partially recovers with increasing α², but does not reach 2T1 due to KCQ heating dephasing channel

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that applying FSD (active cooling of KCQ excited states) restores storage T2 to a level consistent with zero dephasing (T2 ≈ 2T1) across all measured cat sizes α²
correlations_observed.nature: Positive: FSD eliminates the excess dephasing channel at all cat sizes tested (α² = 2.0, 2.7, 3.4, 4.1)

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Kerr nonlinearity K of the SNAIL/KCQ mode in MHz (K/2π), determining the anharmonicity of the Kerr oscillator and the KCQ gate time via TK = π/K
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: K directly sets both the KCQ gate speed and the exponential noise bias scaling; tracking K across devices would enable systematic comparison of KCQ performance across fabrication runs and material platforms

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Kerr-cat qubit bit-flip lifetime TZ in µs (coherent state lifetime, Z-basis), measuring the exponentially-suppressed bit-flip error rate at a given cat size α²
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Bit-flip lifetime is the primary figure of merit for the KCQ noise bias; it must reach >1 ms for fault-tolerant ancilla operation and is the quantity being optimized across KCQ experiments

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Kerr-cat qubit phase-flip lifetime TX,Y in µs (cat state lifetime, X/Y-basis), measuring the linearly-amplified phase-flip error rate at a given cat size α²
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Phase-flip lifetime sets the dominant error channel of the KCQ; the noise bias ratio TZ/TX,Y determines fault-tolerance thresholds and must be tracked alongside bit-flip lifetime

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Cross-Kerr coupling strength χab/2π in kHz between ancilla mode and storage cavity mode
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: χab determines idle dephasing of the storage cavity via ancilla photon shot noise; minimizing χab while maintaining sufficient CD gate rate gCD is the key design trade-off for high on-off ratio ancilla-cavity coupling

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Thermal population nth of the ancilla qubit/oscillator mode in percent at operating temperature
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Ancilla thermal population is the dominant source of idle dephasing for the storage cavity via cross-Kerr coupling; this parameter directly limits logical qubit coherence and must be tracked to understand heating mechanisms across devices

catchall.free_notes: This device consists of a sapphire chip with tantalum-based superconducting circuits (SNAIL and stripline resonators) sandwiched between two 3D high-purity aluminum post cavities. The SNAIL is a capacitively-shunted flux-tunable Josephson circuit operated at flux bias Φext = 0.32Φ0. Fabrication followed refs 48 (Place et al., Ta transmon) and 49 (Ganjam et al., Ta on-chip memories). The 3D Al cavities follow ref 47 (Reagor et al.). Magnetic flux is delivered via a solenoid of NbTi superconducting wire with an on-chip flux transformer. The storage cavity T1 = 204 µs and T2 = 381 µs are measured with a bare SNAIL present (no KCQ squeezing drive), so these represent the storage lifetime limited by SNAIL thermal heating rather than the intrinsic cavity lifetime. The storage 2T1 = 435 ± 17 µs is the intrinsic limit recovered with FSD active. The KCQ lifetimes (TZ = 147 µs, TX,Y = 2.32 µs) are at the standard operating cat size α² = 4 used throughout the paper. The paper demonstrates that frequency-selective dissipation (FSD) — implemented by hybridizing the KCQ first excited state manifold with the readout cavity via a three-wave mixing process at ωFSD = ωr − ωs/2 + ωgap — can eliminate the ancilla-heating-induced storage dephasing, yielding a conservative on-off control ratio bound of >6000.

SNAIL-mode (Kerr-cat qubit).profile_notes: Transport regime is unknown as no RRR or mean free path data is reported; the primary loss/decoherence investigated is ancilla thermal population (nth=2.8%) causing storage cavity dephasing via cross-Kerr coupling, which does not map cleanly to the standard loss_mechanisms vocabulary — quasiparticle and charge_noise are the closest proxies for thermally-driven and fluctuation-based decoherence channels discussed. Coherence tier is competitive based on the SNAIL mode T1=16 µs; the storage cavity T1=204 µs would be state_of_the_art but the primary sample here is the SNAIL/KCQ circuit element.

extraction_json.title: Hardware-Efficient Erasure Qubits With Superconducting Transmon Qutrits

film_material.source: Supplementary Section VII, page 11: '6-qubit Xmon qubit fabricated by MIT Lincoln Laboratory'

junction_present.source: Supplementary Section VII, page 11: Josephson junction-based transmon qubits

T1_us.source: Table S1, Q1_A: T1^ge range 30-70 µs; midpoint ~50 µs. Also stated in main text page 1 as T1^ge ≈ 55 µs

T2_echo_us.source: Table S1, Q1_A: g-e Echo T2E range 20-40 µs

single_qubit_gate_fidelity_pct.source: Figure 4b and page 5: average error per Clifford of 7.6e-4 with erasure detection post-selection, implying fidelity 1-7.6e-4 = 99.924%; converted from infidelity reported in paper

additional_measurements.description: Physical transmon ge transition frequency at zero-flux point
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Qubit frequency sets operating point and determines anharmonicity-related leakage into |e> during logical gates; directly affects erasure rate and gate fidelity

additional_measurements.description: Physical transmon ge transition frequency at operating bias point
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Operating frequency at sweet spot minimizes flux noise dephasing
Weak anharmonicity is the primary limitation on erasure qubit gate fidelity due to leakage to |e> state during two-photon g-f drive; directly sets erasure rate hierarchy
Readout frequency and dispersive coupling determine measurement fidelity and measurement-induced dephasing of logical qubit
Readout bandwidth sets speed/fidelity tradeoff for erasure detection; slower readout increases false-negative rate

additional_measurements.description: Dispersive coupling chi between qubit and readout resonator
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Dispersive coupling determines readout contrast and measurement-induced dephasing rate of the logical qubit
T1^ef directly sets the erasure lifetime (~2*T1^ef) and thus the erasure error rate of the g-f qubit; fundamental limit on erasure-to-Pauli error ratio
Sets baseline dephasing of the logical qubit encoding; determines how much dynamical decoupling is needed

additional_measurements.description: Logical qubit bit-flip lifetime (post-selected, XY4 DD, |+Z> state)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Logical T1 directly sets upper bound on achievable gate fidelity in QEC cycles; ten times improvement over physical T1^ge demonstrates erasure qubit advantage

additional_measurements.description: Logical qubit bit-flip lifetime (post-selected, XY4 DD, |-Z> state)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Logical T1 directly sets upper bound on achievable gate fidelity in QEC cycles

additional_measurements.description: Logical qubit phase-flip coherence time (post-selected, XY4 DD, |+X> state)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Logical T2 sets dephasing limit for gate fidelity in QEC; ten times improvement over physical T2^ge

additional_measurements.description: Logical qubit phase-flip coherence time (post-selected, XY4 DD, |-X> state)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Logical T2 sets dephasing limit for gate fidelity in QEC

additional_measurements.description: Erasure lifetime (timescale for detectable leakage out of code subspace to |e>)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Erasure lifetime sets the rate of detectable errors vs undetectable Pauli errors; ratio of ~13 between bit-flip and erasure rates determines QEC threshold advantage

additional_measurements.description: Logical qubit coherence time with spin-locking (post-selected, |+Z> state)
additional_measurements.source: Page 4 paragraph 4: 'coherence time after post-selection is > 350 µs'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Spin-locking continuous DD further suppresses higher-frequency dephasing noise improving logical T2 beyond XY4 sequence

additional_measurements.description: Logical qubit bit-flip time with spin-locking (|+/-Z> state)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Bit-flip lifetime under spin-locking is reduced compared to XY4 due to ac Stark shift from spin-locking drive; highlights tradeoff in DD protocol choice

additional_measurements.description: Average Clifford gate error without erasure detection
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Baseline gate infidelity of physical transmon qubit sets comparison point for erasure qubit improvement

additional_measurements.description: Average Clifford gate error with erasure detection (post-selected RB)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Logical single-qubit gate fidelity of 99.924% directly determines QEC code performance; seven-fold improvement over physical qubit demonstrates erasure detection benefit

additional_measurements.description: Physical gate error per gate (with erasure detection)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Physical gate error sets the fundamental limit on achievable logical error rates in surface code implementations

additional_measurements.description: Pure dephasing time Tphi of logical g-f qubit (with erasure detection)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Pure dephasing of logical qubit compared to physical Tphi^gf=440 µs shows erasure detection adds negligible dephasing; critical for confirming erasure detection back-action is small

additional_measurements.description: Physical pure dephasing time Tphi^gf (without erasure detection, XY4)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sets the upper limit on logical phase-flip coherence time; dephasing is the irreducible noise channel for g-f erasure qubits

additional_measurements.description: XY4 coherence time T2,XY4 without erasure detection
additional_measurements.source: Page 4 paragraph 3 and Supplementary Section X.C.2, page 15
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Baseline phase coherence without erasure detection; comparison point for logical improvement

additional_measurements.description: False positive rate for erasure detection (|0L> state)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: False positive rate causes unnecessary discarding of valid quantum trajectories, reducing QEC throughput and effective code distance

additional_measurements.description: False positive rate for erasure detection (|1L> state)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: False positive rate causes unnecessary discarding of valid quantum trajectories in QEC cycles

additional_measurements.description: False negative rate for erasure detection (|e> state initialized)
additional_measurements.source: Page 3 paragraph 2 and Supplementary Section X.A, page 14
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: False negative rate allows erasure errors to propagate as undetectable Pauli errors, directly degrading QEC threshold; dominant limitation on logical bit-flip lifetime
Detection cycle time sets the rate at which erasure errors are checked; must be short enough to prevent seepage (|1L>->|e>->|0L> double decay) from exceeding Pauli error rates

additional_measurements.description: Heralded Bell state fidelity between Q1 and Q2 (|0L0L>-i|1L1L>)/sqrt(2)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Two-qubit entanglement fidelity determines viability of parity measurements needed for surface code stabilizer checks with erasure qubits

additional_measurements.description: Heralded Bell state fidelity between Q1 and Q2 (|1L0L>-|0L1L>)/sqrt(2)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Two-qubit entanglement fidelity determines viability of parity measurements for QEC
Per-cycle error rate directly determines logical error rate scaling in surface code; comparison to theoretical 2.8e-3 validates error model

additional_measurements.description: Noise power spectral density measurement of g-f qubit via spin-locking
additional_measurements.value: PSD ~10^2 to 10^5 s^-1 over 10^4 to 10^6 Hz frequency range
additional_measurements.source: Supplementary Figure S10 and Section X.C.1, page 14-15
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: PSD characterizes the 1/f-type dephasing noise spectrum; shows noise is suppressed at higher spin-locking frequencies, explaining improved coherence with spin-locking DD

anomalous_observations.description: At ~13-15 hours into the 20-hour measurement window, a small dip is observed across relaxation time, erasure lifetime, bit-flip lifetime, and phase-flip times simultaneously
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: transient interactions with a TLS defect coupled to the qubit

anomalous_observations.description: Bit-flip lifetime under spin-locking (~200 µs) is lower than under XY4 (~580 µs) despite spin-locking providing better phase-flip coherence
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: ac Stark shift from the spin-locking drive induces dephasing that the Z-basis states are first-order sensitive to, while the X-basis states benefit from the continuous DD

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that more frequent erasure detection (shorter cycle time) reduces seepage rate (undetected |1L>->|e>->|0L> double decay) but requires the ancilla readout photon to decay sufficiently before next SWAP gate
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation: shorter cycles reduce seepage but are limited by ancilla readout photon decay time kappa_r

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Transmon anharmonicity alpha_q in MHz (= E_C/h), the frequency difference between ge and ef transitions
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Anharmonicity directly sets the leakage rate into |e> during g-f logical gate operations and determines the erasure rate hierarchy (erasure vs Pauli error ratio); tracking this across transmon samples would enable systematic comparison of erasure qubit performance across different fabrication runs and designs
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table S1: alpha_q/2pi = 180 MHz for Q1, Q2; 183 MHz for Qa

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Relaxation time from |f> to |e> state of transmon qutrit in microseconds
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: T1^ef sets the erasure lifetime (approximately 2*T1^ef) and thus the dominant error rate for g-f erasure qubits; it is distinct from T1^ge and is the primary figure of merit for erasure qubit performance that should be tracked alongside T1^ge
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table S1: T1^ef range 10-30 µs for Q1_A; main text page 3 states erasure lifetime ~26 µs upper limit from 2*T1^ef

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Timescale for detectable leakage from the logical code subspace to the erasure state |e>, in microseconds
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: The erasure lifetime is the key figure of merit distinguishing erasure qubits from conventional qubits; it characterizes the rate of detectable vs undetectable errors and determines QEC threshold advantage. The ratio of bit-flip lifetime to erasure lifetime (~13 here) is the core metric for erasure qubit quality

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Probability that an erasure error (qubit in |e> state) goes undetected by mid-circuit erasure detection, in percent
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: False negative rate directly determines what fraction of erasure errors convert to undetectable Pauli errors, setting a fundamental floor on logical error rates and QEC threshold; essential for comparing erasure detection schemes across different implementations
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Page 3 paragraph 2 and Supplementary Section X.A: false negative rate 6-8%, total ~7.3%

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Transmon ge transition frequency at the operating bias point in GHz
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Operating frequency determines proximity to flux sweet spot, which sets dephasing noise level and flux-noise-induced frequency fluctuations; operating away from sweet spot (as ancilla Qa does) degrades coherence and erasure detection fidelity
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table S1: operating point frequency for all three qubits

catchall.free_notes: Device is a 6-qubit Xmon transmon chip fabricated by MIT Lincoln Laboratory SQUILL Foundry. Three qubits (Q1, Qa, Q2) were used in this experiment across two separate cooldowns (A and B). Q1 is the data qubit (g-f erasure qubit), Qa is the ancilla for erasure detection, Q2 is the second data qubit used in parity measurement experiments. All qubits are flux-tunable transmons with X-shaped (Xmon) design and common capacitive coupling. Experiments performed in Bluefors LD250 dilution refrigerator at ~10 mK. TWPA (MIT LL) used for amplification. The g-f erasure qubit encodes |0L>=|g> and |1L>=|f>, with erasure detection via a microwave-activated four-wave-mixing SWAP gate between |eg> and |0Lf> states. Cooldown A data is the primary dataset for erasure qubit demonstrations (Table S1, Q1_A: T1^ge 30-70 µs, T2E 20-40 µs, T1^ef 10-30 µs). Cooldown B shows slightly better Q1 coherence (T1^ge 40-80 µs) but shorter ancilla Qa coherence. Parity measurement experiments were conducted during a period of slightly lower coherence performance (noted in Figure 5 caption). A TLS defect interaction was observed at ~13-15 hours into the 20-hour stability measurement. The ancilla qubit Qa is operated ~600 MHz away from its zero-flux point for erasure detection, which significantly degrades its coherence (T2^ge ~0.42 µs Ramsey, ~1.1 µs echo at operating point for FWM driving). This short ancilla coherence is the primary limitation on false-negative rate.

T1_measurement_context.source: Backfilled from similarity_profile device_type='transmon' by backfill_t1_context.py

Q1 (data qubit, cooldown A).profile_notes: Physical T1 of 50 µs places this in competitive tier; the anomalous TLS dip and 1/f dephasing noise characterization justify TLS_unattributed and flux_noise as loss mechanisms. Transport regime is not characterized — this is a device-level paper with no film transport measurements reported.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Parallel component A of hyperfine coupling between electron spin and individual nuclear spin, in kHz
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Hyperfine coupling parameters directly determine the available nuclear spin register size and the gate fidelity achievable for electron-nuclear entanglement operations; systematic tracking across devices would enable comparison of nuclear spin register quality

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Perpendicular component B of hyperfine coupling between electron spin and individual nuclear spin, in kHz
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Together with parallel component A, B fully parameterizes the spin Hamiltonian needed to design hardware-efficient multi-qubit gate protocols; without this, optimal gate sequences cannot be designed for a given NV device

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Effective timing resolution of dynamical decoupling control hardware, in picoseconds
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Timing resolution sets the minimum spectral feature that can be resolved in CPMG spectroscopy; insufficient resolution undersamples narrow hyperfine features and distorts extracted coupling parameters, directly limiting the size and quality of the characterizable nuclear spin register

catchall.free_notes: Room-temperature NV center measured in a shared user-facility confocal microscope. Diamond sample has natural 13C abundance (1.1%). Photon collection enhanced by solid immersion lens (SIL). Microwave delivered via wirebonded antenna positioned ~25 µm above diamond and ~15 µm lateral to SIL. Control system: RFSoC 4x2 running QICK-DAWG firmware achieving 200 ps effective timing resolution via waveform-offset method, overcoming native 3.25 ns FPGA sequencer granularity. Single-pass AOM used for optical control, which contributed to T1 degradation at saturation power.

catchall.free_notes: Different NV center from the room-temperature sample. Measured at 4K in a separate confocal setup. Control system: ARTIQ used for optical and readout control; QICK-based MW framework retained for microwave pulses with 200 ps timing resolution. Double-pass AOM used for improved optical extinction (compared to single-pass AOM at room temperature). Resonant optical readout and optical pumping used for bottom panels of Fig. 4. Longer coherence at 4K enables higher-N CPMG sequences (up to N=320) and extended tau values, revealing finer spectral features.

NV-center-room-temperature.profile_notes: This sample is an NV center in diamond — a spin qubit platform entirely outside the superconducting qubit materials vocabulary. All superconducting-specific fields (material_class, transport_regime, growth_method, loss_mechanisms) are mapped to their closest 'unknown' or 'other' equivalents. T1 of 1.71 ms places this in state_of_the_art by coherence_tier thresholds, though the metric is not directly comparable to superconducting qubit T1.

NV-center-low-temperature-4K.profile_notes: Same non-superconducting NV center platform as the room-temperature sample; all superconducting-specific vocabulary fields are inapplicable. T2_echo of 1050 µs exceeds the state_of_the_art threshold, though again this is a spin qubit metric rather than a superconducting qubit metric.

anomalous_observations.description: β-Ta has Tc = 0.7 K, far below α-Ta (Tc ≈ 4.2-4.4 K), yet achieves qubit T1 up to 958 µs and time-averaged Q up to 10.1×10^6, comparable to best α-Ta and Nb transmons. This contradicts the prevailing belief that β-Ta is detrimental to qubit performance.
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: QP-induced losses are negligible at operating temperatures (10-15 mK) even with Tc = 0.7 K because equilibrium QP fraction is <10^-19 at 20 mK, and dominant loss is TLS, not QPs. The low Tc is therefore not an impediment to qubit performance in the TLS-dominated regime.

anomalous_observations.description: Distribution of qubit Q values across 11 qubits exceeds the expected linear absorption limit imposed by surface TLS (expected QTLS,0 = 4.8 × 10^6 for pMS = 1.3×10^-4).
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Qubit operates at powers that partially saturate TLSs, increasing effective Q above the linear absorption limit, consistent with observations in α-Ta qubits.

anomalous_observations.description: OCS transmons (EJ/EC = 16-28) show T2,E < T1, while non-OCS transmons (EJ/EC = 74-122) show T2,E > T1, with very long pure dephasing times (500-1800 µs for non-OCS vs 150-470 µs for OCS).
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: OCS transmons suffer additional dephasing from charge noise, as charge dispersion increases exponentially with decreasing EJ/EC.

anomalous_observations.description: Qubits with higher qubit frequencies show systematically lower Q, with the highest-frequency qubit (5.80 GHz) having the lowest Q of (1.5 ± 0.2) × 10^6.
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: One possible relaxation channel is radiative coupling to spurious modes of the device package. Purcell effect ruled out (Purcell-limited T1 > 5 ms from simulation).

anomalous_observations.description: β-Ta on Si shows power- and temperature-independent losses (Qother = 0.8-2.8 × 10^6) independent of geometry, limiting Qint across all 7 resonators, unlike β-Ta on sapphire.
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: One plausible candidate is non-saturable dielectric loss from the β-Ta/Si MS interface, whose structure (~1 nm amorphous Ta layer) differs from the α-Ta/Si epitaxial interface.

correlations_observed.description: QTLS,0 of 71 β-Ta resonators scales inversely with metal-substrate surface participation ratio pMS, consistent with surface TLS-dominated loss
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation: QTLS,0 ∝ 1/pMS, described by single effective surface loss tangent tan δ = (1.6 ± 0.1) × 10^-3 regardless of film thickness

correlations_observed.description: Authors state qubits with T2,E < T1 are also in the OCS regime (EJ/EC = 16-28), while qubits with T2,E > T1 are in the transmon regime (EJ/EC = 74-122)
correlations_observed.nature: Threshold behavior: OCS (EJ/EC < ~30) → T2,E < T1 (charge noise limited); non-OCS (EJ/EC > ~74) → T2,E > T1

correlations_observed.description: Authors state qubits with lower time-averaged Q also have higher frequencies
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation: higher frequency correlates with lower Q

correlations_observed.description: Authors state Lk/□ increases with decreasing film thickness t and decreasing CPW gap width s across all 80 CPW resonators
correlations_observed.measurement_b: Film thickness t and CPW gap width s
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation: Lk/□ ∝ µ0λ coth(t/λ), increasing as t decreases; also increases with decreasing s for fixed t

schema_promotion_candidates.description: London magnetic penetration depth λ in µm, fitted from sheet kinetic inductance vs film thickness data using Lk/□(t) = µ0λ coth(t/λ)
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: λ determines the kinetic inductance of the film for all thicknesses, which directly shifts resonator frequencies and affects qubit wiring design. For β-Ta, λ = 1.78 µm (vs ~0.09 µm for α-Ta), making this the dominant parameter for compact resonator and qubit design. Tracking λ across samples would allow systematic comparison of kinetic inductance contributions and enable reliable device design without per-film calibration.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Sheet kinetic inductance Lk/□ in pH/□, a film-intrinsic quantity that determines the kinetic inductance contribution to resonator and qubit circuits
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Lk/□ is the key design parameter for circuits using high-kinetic-inductance materials. For β-Ta films used for qubits (~260 nm), Lk/□ ≈ 10 pH/□. This determines resonator frequency shifts up to 90%, affects qubit wiring layout compactness, and is essential for designing kinetic inductance detectors. Tracking Lk/□ vs thickness would build a predictive model for device design across material platforms.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text page 4-5, Figure 2c, Table S4 Supporting Information

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Normal state (just above Tc) resistivity ρn in µΩ·cm, an intrinsic material property independent of device geometry
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: ρn determines the mean free path (l < 1 nm for β-Ta), confirms the dirty vs clean superconducting limit, and allows calculation of λ_dirty from BCS theory. The agreement between λ_dirty and λ_fitted (both ~1.78 µm) validates the BCS dirty limit model. For α-Ta, ρn = 1-50 µΩ·cm; for β-Ta, ρn = 150-220 µΩ·cm—this order-of-magnitude difference is the fundamental origin of the kinetic inductance difference between phases.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text page 3: '(180 ± 1) µΩ cm'; Table 1

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Effective surface loss tangent tan δ (dimensionless) extracted from QTLS,0 vs pMS scaling, capturing combined MA+MS+SA surface TLS loss per unit participation ratio
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: tan δ is the material-intrinsic figure of merit for surface TLS loss, independent of device geometry. For β-Ta: (1.6 ± 0.1) × 10^-3 vs α-Ta: (8.1 ± 0.6) × 10^-4—a factor ~2 difference that directly predicts the qubit Q limit via QTLS,0 = 1/(pMS × tan δ). Tracking tan δ across materials and processing conditions would allow direct comparison of loss performance without running full qubit experiments.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text page 6 and Supporting Information S9

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Ratio of Josephson energy to charging energy EJ/EC (dimensionless), characterizing transmon regime
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: EJ/EC determines whether a qubit is in the offset-charge-sensitive (OCS) regime (EJ/EC < ~30) or the transmon regime (EJ/EC > ~50). OCS qubits exhibit exponentially larger charge dispersion, leading to additional dephasing from charge noise and T2,E < T1. This parameter directly predicts the dominant dephasing mechanism and is therefore essential for interpreting T1/T2 data and designing qubits for specific coherence targets.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Table S2 and main text page 6

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Thickness of amorphous interfacial layer at the metal-substrate (MS) interface in nm
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: The ~5 nm amorphous β-Ta layer at the β-Ta/sapphire MS interface is identified as a potential TLS loss source. For α-Ta on sapphire, the interface is epitaxially matched (no amorphous layer). Tracking this parameter across substrate materials and deposition conditions would help quantify the MS interface contribution to surface TLS loss, which currently cannot be separated from MA interface loss in the resonator study.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text page 6 and Supporting Information S9, Figure S10f

catchall.free_notes: This paper reports 17 distinct β-Ta films (F1-F17) spanning thicknesses from 21 nm to 2000 nm. Films F1-F14 were used for resonators, F15 and F16 for qubits (both ~260-262 nm thick), and F17 (200 nm) for β-Ta-on-Si resonators. The qubit sample entry above represents the characteristic β-Ta/sapphire film system; individual qubit performance data from Table S3 covers 11 qubits across 2 chips from F15/F16. The qubit record entry (Qubit 2) has EJ/EC = 19 (OCS transmon) with fq = 2.74 GHz. Substrate pre-treatment: piranha clean (H2SO4:H2O2 2:1, 20 min) followed by in-situ heating to 300°C for 1 hr then cooling to RT. Post-fabrication: 10:1 buffered oxide etch (20 min) to reduce native oxide. Deposition conditions: 300 W DC power, 3 mTorr Ar, 25 sccm Ar, 1.15 inch throw distance, ~2 Å/s growth rate. All devices measured in dilution refrigerators (Bluefors XLD400 or LD400) at 10-15 mK base temperature with magnetic shielding. Junctions fabricated by Manhattan-style double-angle e-beam evaporation of Al/AlOx/Al following methods from Bland et al. 2025 (Nature). The paper represents the first systematic demonstration of low-loss transmon qubits from pure β-phase Ta, challenging the assumption that β-Ta is incompatible with high-performance superconducting circuits.

β-Ta film on sapphire (qubit films F15/F.sample_id: β-Ta film on sapphire (qubit films F15/F16) - best qubit (Qubit 2)
β-Ta film on sapphire (qubit films F15/F.profile_notes: β-Ta with RRR=1.02 and ρn=180 µΩ·cm firmly places this in the dirty limit. T1 up to 958 µs (time-averaged 585 µs for Qubit 2) qualifies as state_of_the_art; loss mechanisms include MS/MA interface TLS (identified via pMS scaling), charge noise (OCS regime, EJ/EC=19), and radiation/package coupling (frequency-dependent Q suppression).

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Very high kinetic inductance fraction (α=0.971) enables compact resonators. Coexistence with Qint = 1.6×10^6 demonstrates β-Ta is competitive for kinetic inductance detector applications.

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Smaller grain size than α-Ta (50-200 nm) increases grain boundary density, potentially increasing effective native oxide volume at MA interface and TLS density. Grain boundaries are a known source of surface roughness and TLS loss.

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Amorphous layer at MS interface is a potential source of TLS loss. Its loss contribution cannot be independently quantified with current resonator geometries (pMA and pMS not independently varied). Future devices with independent participation ratio control needed.

additional_measurements.description: Sidewall roughness feature size on etched β-Ta resonators
additional_measurements.source: Section S9: 'scanning electron microscopy images of device sidewalls show protrusions with lateral dimensions of about 100 nm'; Figure S10d
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Sidewall roughness increases effective oxide volume at MA interface and can enhance surface losses in regions with high electric field participation (sidewalls). This is a plausible contributor to the ~2x higher surface loss in β-Ta vs α-Ta.

additional_measurements.description: Surface participation ratio (pMS) for qubit capacitor pads
additional_measurements.source: Main text page 6: 'The qubits are designed with a pMS of 1.3 × 10^-4'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: pMS determines the TLS-limited quality factor via QTLS,0 = 1/(pMS × tan δ). At pMS=1.3×10^-4 and tan δ=1.6×10^-3, expected QTLS,0 ≈ 4.8×10^6, setting the material loss limit for qubit T1.

additional_measurements.description: QTLS,0 (TLS loss parameter) for representative CPW resonator (F6-CPW-2, s=2µm, t=263nm)
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: QTLS,0 parametrizes TLS-limited Q at single-photon powers. Scales as 1/pMS × 1/tan δ. Used to extract effective surface loss tangent for β-Ta.

anomalous_observations.description: β-Ta resonators show Qint up to 10^7 despite having a magnetic penetration depth ~18x larger than α-Ta and a very high kinetic inductance fraction. Literature predicted that micron-scale λ would cause increased inductive losses and degrade Qint.
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: High kinetic inductance and high Qint are not mutually exclusive. The authors find a 'universal' Qint_max ≈ κσ2 envelope (κ = 0.16 ± 0.02 Ω·m) consistent across superconductors in the high-kinetic-inductance regime, suggesting the loss mechanism is surface TLS (not inductive loss).

anomalous_observations.description: β-Ta on silicon substrates shows power- and temperature-independent losses (Qother) that limit Qint to < 3 million across all 7 resonators, independent of resonator geometry. This non-saturable loss is absent in β-Ta on sapphire resonators.
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Non-saturable dielectric loss originating from the MS interface between β-Ta and Si, whose amorphous structure (1 nm thick from STEM) differs from the lattice-matched α-Ta/Si interface. The precise source requires further investigation.

correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation: QTLS,0 ∝ 1/pMS with single tan δ = (1.6 ± 0.1) × 10^-3; linear across pMS range 8×10^-5 to 2×10^-3

correlations_observed.description: Authors observe qubits with lower Q also have higher frequencies, suggesting a frequency-dependent qubit quality factor
correlations_observed.nature: Negative correlation: higher frequency qubits show lower Q across the 11-qubit dataset

correlations_observed.description: Authors find qubits with T2,E < T1 are in the OCS regime (EJ/EC = 16-28), while qubits with T2,E > T1 are deep in the transmon regime (EJ/EC = 74-122)
correlations_observed.nature: Threshold behavior at EJ/EC ≈ 28-74: below this range T2,E < T1 (charge noise limited), above it T2,E > T1 (near lifetime limited)

For β-Ta, λ = 1.78 µm causes Lk/□ = 10-300 pH/□ for typical device thicknesses, requiring systematic compensation in resonator and qubit design. Tracking λ across deposition conditions would enable predictive design without re-measurement for each film.

Critical for compact device design and for understanding inductive loss channels in high-kinetic-inductance materials.

Systematic tracking of pMS across device geometries enables direct comparison of material loss tangents across different studies and material platforms.

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Normal state resistivity ρn of the superconducting film in µΩ·cm, measured just above Tc
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: ρn determines the electronic mean free path l and places the film in the dirty (l << ξ0) or clean limit, which governs vortex behavior and kinetic inductance. For β-Ta, ρn = 180 µΩ·cm gives l < 1 nm (deep dirty limit), validating BCS dirty-limit kinetic inductance model and setting λ_dirty ≈ 1.7 µm. Tracking ρn across films enables quantitative comparison of material quality.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Main text page 3; Section S1: ρn = (180 ± 1) µΩ·cm; Figure S1c

catchall.free_notes: This record aggregates the resonator dataset: 80 CPW + 16 LE resonators across 14 films (F1-F14) on sapphire with thicknesses 21-2000 nm. All resonators underwent post-fabrication buffered oxide etch (10:1 BOE, 20 min). Measurement temperature 10-15 mK in dilution refrigerator. Film F6 (t=263nm) resonators shown in Figure 3a as representative example. The Hall bar used for DC resistivity measurement (Section S1) had dimensions: length=370 µm, width=25 µm, thickness=263 nm (Film F6 equivalent). A separate Film F17 (200 nm β-Ta on Si) was also studied as a resonator-only sample; those 7 CPW resonators showed Qint < 3 million limited by non-saturable MS interface loss rather than TLS.

Qubit-1.profile_notes: RRR=1.02 and resistivity ~180 µΩ·cm confirm deep dirty limit. T1=397 µs exceeds 100 µs threshold for state_of_the_art. OCS regime (EJ/EC=16) causes charge noise dephasing; TLS_interface identified via pMS×tanδ analysis.

Qubit-2.profile_notes: Best-performing qubit with time-averaged T1=585 µs and maximum single-shot T1=958 µs; clearly state_of_the_art. OCS regime (EJ/EC=19) contributes charge noise dephasing alongside TLS interface loss.

Qubit-3.profile_notes: T1=400 µs places this in state_of_the_art tier. OCS regime (EJ/EC=20) implies charge noise contribution to dephasing consistent with T2,R < T1.

Qubit-4.profile_notes: T1=423 µs (time-averaged), maximum 584 µs; state_of_the_art. OCS transmon (EJ/EC=22) with T2,E < T1 consistent with charge noise dephasing explicitly noted by authors.

Qubit-5.profile_notes: T1=329 µs; state_of_the_art. OCS regime (EJ/EC=28) with T2,E < T1 confirms charge noise as a dephasing channel alongside TLS interface loss.

Qubit-6.profile_notes: T1=236 µs; state_of_the_art. Deep transmon (EJ/EC=74) with T2,E > T1 and very long T_phi=1300 µs; loss is dominated by T1 channels (TLS interface); radiation/package modes suspected from frequency-dependent Q trend noted by authors.

Qubit-7.profile_notes: T1=148 µs; state_of_the_art. Deep transmon (EJ/EC=77) with largest T_phi=1800 µs in dataset; T1 limited by TLS interface and possibly frequency-dependent radiative coupling to package modes per author hypothesis.

Qubit-8.profile_notes: T1=97 µs places this just below the 100 µs state_of_the_art threshold, so competitive. Deep transmon (EJ/EC=82); lower Q consistent with frequency-dependent loss channel identified by authors.

Qubit-9.profile_notes: T1=111 µs; state_of_the_art. Deep transmon (EJ/EC=99); T2,E > T1 with long T_phi consistent with TLS-limited T1 and frequency-dependent radiative loss channel per authors.

Qubit-10.profile_notes: T1=137 µs; state_of_the_art. Deep transmon (EJ/EC=98); very short T2,R=11 µs noted as anomalous with limited measurement count; TLS interface and frequency-dependent radiative loss consistent with dataset trends.

Qubit-11.profile_notes: T1=40 µs; competitive tier. Highest frequency qubit (5.80 GHz) with lowest Q in dataset; authors explicitly hypothesize frequency-dependent radiative loss to package spurious modes after ruling out Purcell effect via simulation.

β-Ta-on-sapphire-resonators-representati.profile_notes: Qi=1.6×10^6 qualifies as state_of_the_art for resonators. RRR=1.02 and ρn=180 µΩ·cm (l<1 nm) confirm deep dirty limit; pMS×tanδ analysis explicitly attributes loss to TLS at metal-substrate and metal-vacuum interfaces; 5 nm amorphous MS layer and surface oxide (2.9 nm) are the identified TLS hosts.

additional_measurements.description: Qother (power- and temperature-independent loss floor) across 7 CPW resonators

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Geometry-independent Qother strongly suggests non-TLS bulk or interface dielectric loss from the β-Ta/Si MS interface; this loss channel is not present in β-Ta/sapphire, pointing to the MS interface structure as the critical differentiator

additional_measurements.description: Native oxide thickness at MA interface for β-Ta on Si
additional_measurements.source: Figure S11b caption: 'MA interface shows a roughly 2.5 nm-thick native oxide layer'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Slightly thinner oxide than β-Ta on sapphire (2.9 nm); not the limiting factor for the higher loss in this substrate

additional_measurements.description: Amorphous interfacial layer at MS interface (β-Ta on Si)
additional_measurements.source: Figure S11c caption: 'MS interface ... suggesting the presence of a roughly 1 nm-thick amorphous Ta layer at the epitaxial interface'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Amorphous interfacial layer at β-Ta/Si interface is thinner than at β-Ta/sapphire (~5 nm) but may have higher TLS density; this interface is structurally different from α-Ta/Si and may be the source of the non-saturable loss

additional_measurements.description: Substrate pre-heating temperature before β-Ta deposition on Si
additional_measurements.source: Section S10: 'substrate is heated in situ at 600°C, instead of 300°C, for 1 hour'
additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Higher substrate pre-heating used for Si compared to sapphire (300°C); despite this, Qother is still limited, suggesting film phase or interface quality is not the issue—or that 600°C is insufficient for good β-Ta/Si interface

anomalous_observations.description: β-Ta CPW resonators on Si show power- and temperature-independent Qother < 3 million that is independent of resonator geometry (pMS), in contrast to β-Ta on sapphire where losses are TLS-dominated and geometry-dependent
anomalous_observations.hypothesis: Author hypothesis: Non-saturable dielectric loss originating from the MS interface between β-Ta and Si. Authors note the MS interface structure differs from α-Ta/Si interface (which shows ms-scale coherence) and requires further investigation.

catchall.free_notes: β-Ta on Si is an exploratory result reported in Section S10. Native Si oxide removed by HF etch before deposition. Despite achieving the highest Qint among β-Ta-on-Si resonators reported in the literature, performance is limited by a geometry-independent loss floor.

Qubit-1.profile_notes: RRR of 1.02 and resistivity of 180 µΩ·cm with mean free path < 1 nm explicitly confirm dirty limit. T1 = 397 µs qualifies as state_of_the_art. Charge noise identified as dephasing mechanism in OCS regime.

Qubit-2.profile_notes: Best-performing qubit in the study with time-averaged T1 = 585 µs and maximum T1 = 958 µs, firmly state_of_the_art. OCS transmon with charge noise contributing to dephasing; β-Ta dirty limit confirmed by RRR and resistivity.

Qubit-3.profile_notes: T1 = 400 µs is state_of_the_art. OCS transmon on β-Ta/sapphire dirty-limit film; charge noise identified from T2,R < T2,E pattern.

Qubit-4.profile_notes: T1 = 423 µs is state_of_the_art. OCS transmon with T2,E << T1 consistent with charge noise dephasing in dirty-limit β-Ta film.

Qubit-5.profile_notes: T1 = 329 µs is state_of_the_art. Upper boundary OCS transmon (EJ/EC = 28); charge noise still contributes to dephasing.

Qubit-6.profile_notes: T1 = 236 µs is state_of_the_art. Deep transmon (EJ/EC = 74) from film F16; T2,E > T1 confirms charge noise suppressed. Radiation loss included because radiative coupling to package modes is hypothesized for frequency-dependent Q degradation in this qubit set.

Qubit-7.profile_notes: T1 = 148 µs is state_of_the_art. Deep transmon with very long pure dephasing (1800 µs); frequency-dependent Q reduction attributed by authors to possible radiative coupling to package modes.

Qubit-8.profile_notes: T1 = 97 µs falls in the competitive tier (10–100 µs boundary; time-averaged value is 97 µs). Deep transmon on dirty-limit β-Ta; authors identify frequency-dependent loss possibly due to radiative coupling to package modes.

Qubit-9.profile_notes: T1 = 111 µs is state_of_the_art. Deep transmon (EJ/EC = 99) with long pure dephasing; frequency-dependent Q reduction consistent with radiative loss hypothesis.

Qubit-10.profile_notes: T1 = 137 µs is state_of_the_art. Deep transmon (EJ/EC = 98) on film F16; higher frequency correlates with lower Q, consistent with radiative loss hypothesis raised by authors.

Qubit-11.profile_notes: T1 = 40 µs is competitive. Highest-frequency, lowest-Q qubit in the dataset; authors explicitly hypothesize radiative coupling to spurious package modes rather than TLS or Purcell loss.

Resonator-F6-CPW-6-representative.profile_notes: Qi = 4.6×10^5 at single photon is competitive. Representative CPW resonator used to demonstrate power and temperature dependence of Qint; TLS loss attributed to MS interface via loss tangent extraction and pMS scaling.

Resonator-F1-CPW-8-high-Lk.profile_notes: Qi = 1.6×10^6 is state_of_the_art for a resonator. Highlighted as a key demonstration of coexisting ultra-high kinetic inductance (Lk/□ = 259 pH/□, α = 0.971) with high Qint in 21 nm β-Ta.

Resonator-F8-LE-35.profile_notes: Qi = 4.8×10^6 is state_of_the_art; this lumped-element resonator has the lowest pMS in the dataset and highest QTLS,0, directly supporting the pMS-to-TLS-loss correlation central to the paper's analysis.

Beta-Ta-on-Si-resonator-F17.profile_notes: Qi = 8.4×10^5 is competitive. Performance is limited by a geometry-independent, non-saturable loss floor attributed to the β-Ta/Si MS interface, distinct from the TLS-dominated, geometry-dependent loss in β-Ta/sapphire samples.

relevance_reason: This paper reports original qubit coherence measurements (T1, T2, gate fidelity) on Ta-based 2D transmon qubits with Al/AlOx Josephson junctions, uses Ta explicitly as a qubit material, studies TLS/dielectric loss in superconducting resonators, and acknowledges C2QA funding.
title: Millisecond lifetimes and coherence times in 2D transmon qubits

anomalous_observations.description: Qubit 57 has T1avg = 81.8 µs and Qavg = 1.99 × 10^6, dramatically lower than all other qubits in Extended Data Table 2 (next lowest is 290 µs). This qubit shows both poor T1 and relatively poor T2E.

additional_measurements.suspected_relevance: Qc sets the coupling loss contribution; well above measured Qi ensures accurate internal quality factor extraction
Gap variation enables systematic study of surface participation ratio vs Q_TLS,0 to extract loss tangent
Trench depth from Cl RIE etch reduces SPR below what gap width alone would predict; critical for accurate SPR-Q analysis
High-resistivity Si bulk dielectric loss is at least 7x lower than sapphire bulk limit (which limited sapphire Qi to 7.7 × 10^6 for 2D transmons)

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that as SPR (pMS) decreases, QTLS,0 increases, consistent with TLS loss being located in a thin surface/interface layer
correlations_observed.nature: negative correlation; higher pMS → lower QTLS,0

correlations_observed.description: Authors state that for the largest devices (lowest SPRs) on high-resistivity Si, QTLS,0 continues to increase with the same scaling (no saturation), in contrast to Ta-on-sapphire where a bulk substrate limit was observed
correlations_observed.measurement_b: QTLS,0 saturation behavior at low SPR
correlations_observed.nature: Si shows no saturation; sapphire shows saturation indicating bulk loss limit

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Electrical resistivity of the substrate in Ω·cm; for Si substrates this is a key parameter determining bulk dielectric loss
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Authors explicitly identify substrate resistivity (>20 kΩ cm) as the critical substrate specification enabling low bulk dielectric loss; resistivity is likely a proxy for impurity content that determines TLS density in Si substrate — tracking this would allow systematic comparison across Si substrate grades and vendors
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Methods: resistivity >20 kΩ cm; Page 4 paragraph 4: resistivity is probably a proxy for material purity

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Base pressure of the junction deposition chamber in mbar before evaporation
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: The paper demonstrates that HV (10^-7 mbar) vs UHV (3×10^-10 mbar) junction deposition produces statistically significant differences in T2E (P < 0.01), directly linking chamber base pressure to qubit dephasing via hydrocarbon contamination. Tracking this systematically would enable cross-lab comparison of junction quality.
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Methods: UHV base pressure 3×10^-10 mbar; HV base pressure 1×10^-7 mbar; Page 3 paragraph 1

schema_promotion_candidates.description: Thickness of SiOx native oxide regrown on exposed Si surfaces after ambient exposure, in nm
schema_promotion_candidates.why_important: Authors directly characterize SiOx regrowth to 1.5 nm after 65 days ambient exposure and show it contributes less than 10% of total surface loss — tracking this alongside resonator loss tangent would allow quantification of the SiOx loss tangent and set limits on oxide passivation strategies
schema_promotion_candidates.source: Page 3 paragraph 1: 1.5 nm SiOx after 65 days; Extended Data Fig. 7

catchall.free_notes: This record covers Ta-on-Si CPW resonators used for loss disentanglement (Fig. 2). Lumped element resonators also measured (Extended Data Fig. 5) with gaps 2-100 µm, pMS 6×10^-3 to 7×10^-5, Qc ~10 million. The resonator loss tangent extraction uses SPR fitting following Crowley et al. (Phys. Rev. X 13, 041005). Ta-on-Si resonators also remeasured after 65 days ambient exposure (1.5 nm SiOx) yielding tan_delta = (9.5 ± 0.6) × 10^-4, consistent with no significant SiOx loss contribution.

Qubit-1.profile_notes: Alpha-Ta (bcc) is the clean-limit phase with Tc=4.2K; T1avg=610µs and T1max=1031µs are state-of-the-art qubit performance. HV junction deposition is noted as a factor limiting T2E via TLS dephasing.

Qubit-2.profile_notes: Alpha-Ta on Si with HV junction; T1avg=424µs and T1max=763µs are state_of_the_art by qubit T1 threshold (>100µs).

Qubit-3.profile_notes: Alpha-Ta on Si with HV junction; T1avg=580µs and T1max=876µs exceed 100µs threshold for state_of_the_art.

Qubit-35.profile_notes: UHV-junction qubit; T1avg=464µs is state_of_the_art. T2E not measured.

Qubit-39.profile_notes: UHV-junction qubit approaching T2E=2T1 limit with Tphi=40ms; T1avg=378µs is state_of_the_art. Nearly pure T1-limited coherence makes this device a key reference for identifying relaxation mechanisms.

Qubit-57.profile_notes: Anomalously poor performer in Extended Data Table 2 with T1avg=82µs and Qavg=1.99e6, dramatically below all other qubits; classified as competitive (10-100µs threshold) rather than state_of_the_art. Likely defective junction as noted in record.

Ta-on-Si-resonators-CPW.profile_notes: CPW resonators used for loss disentanglement via SPR-QTLS,0 scaling; Q_TLS,0=5.3e7 is state_of_the_art for a resonator. Authors explicitly evidence negative SPR-to-QTLS,0 correlation and contrast Si vs sapphire substrate bulk dielectric loss saturation behavior.
